Not a fan, but I guess it has its place in the world if you want to see a dragon breathing fire - this is probably the best you’ll get. I’d recommend watching GOT at home for that though. 🤷🏼‍♀️🤷🏼‍♀️🤷🏼‍♀️ or just look at the photo below, it will do. 😂

It's a beautiful bridge, this rating goes for the show - it's 10 mins in total, and around 2 minutes of actual action combined. Its 10 seconds of spraying, 3 minutes of nothing. It seemed to be very exciting for kids, but it is honestly not worth the time:) For those who do want the 3 min entertainment - people start gathering around 20-20:20 to get good spots, there are mini vendors along the river so you can sit back and watch the show for the price of a drink (25-30k). The chairs are filled by 20:30 tho, so be there earlier.

The bridge itself is beautiful and makes for a great photo spot, especially in the evening. However, the fire and water show was quite overrated and underwhelming. There was no music, lighting effects, or atmosphere to enhance the experience — just a few bursts of fire and water. It felt a bit flat overall. To make things worse, the entire area was extremely crowded, which made it hard to enjoy the moment. Worth seeing once if you're nearby, but don't expect too much from the show itself.

Just came back from waiting almost 30 mins with no fire or water from the dragon. So I guess that means it's not always guaranteed to put on a show. Be warned. I have more chance of seeing fire from the hot pot restaurants on the way back to my hotel

Edit: okay I take some of this back. We checked with a taxi driver today and apparently Friday and Saturday was a religious day which is why the fire didn't happen. It's supposed to happen tonight though, so fingers crossed

NOT SURE WHAT THE HYPE IS ABOUT.

Probably ok to visit once, but don't expect anything too extraordinary. The dragon spits fire a few times, and there are water jets but it’s fairly brief.

The bridge itself gets very crowded, especially during peak times, which can detract from the experience. If you're not up for the crowds, there are other nearby spots where you can catch a view of the dragon and the spectacle without being in the thick of it.

One downside is the high prices for food and drinks sold around the bridge, which can feel a bit like a tourist trap.

We had dinner nearby and ran and hurried to see this 10-minute show. And what a disappointment it was when we saw it! My head would burst into flames exactly 3-4 times, then rest for a couple of minutes. Then it would burn again. Then there would be a 5-6 minute break, and then smoke would come out of my head. A terrible show. If you live nearby and can easily walk to the bridge, then it's okay. But I don't recommend driving across the entire city and running to see it. You'll be disappointed.

Nothing special. And totally overcrowded. A fire fountain shoots out of the dragon that decorates the bridge. The road is closed for it, and all the locals are annoyed by it, in my opinion. The spectacle 🤣 lasts 10 minutes, and then several vendors swarm into the crowd, and then everyone goes to the night market right next door. It's practically a queue that takes you through... Pointless.

They said it was the Yongdari fireworks show at 9 PM... I got on a Grab at the right time, found a spot where I could see it well, and waited... It finally started, but... ㅠ... They just spit fire out of their mouths, I think a few times... And there was even a water show where they spit out river water... The higher your expectations, the greater your disappointment... I highly recommend avoiding the front of your head.

During the day, it looks like a normal bridge.
On Saturday nights only, flames seem to blow from the dragon's mouth.

The air is bad and the view is poor, so it's not much to see.

Oh well, you can do it if you happen to be there. But you don't have to. It's pretty unspectacular, and I don't understand the hype. The flame isn't particularly impressive. Grandpa can do it better with his old flamethrower.

If the wind is bad, the spectators downwind get quite wet from the subsequent water fountain. That was the only thing that brought a smile to my face.

The bridge and its construction in and of itself are worth seeing, as is the view of the city from it. It's best to take advantage of the opportunity when everyone is distracted by the little show and walk across the closed-off road to see the dragon.

This is the Dragon Bridge in Da Nang 🐉
I heard it breathes fire and water on weekend nights, but unfortunately, I've only ever seen it during the day.

I heard that if you stand nearby, you'll get quite wet.

Da Nang used to be a deserted place,
and when I first saw this bridge, I thought it was pretty tasteless...
But since there are no other symbols of the city, it's become a popular tourist destination.

Excited and thrilled, I imagined it would be something special... but this tiny city, with only a long stretch of pristine blue beach and Hoi An... is no match for present-day Hai Phong, with its breathtakingly beautiful Hoang Van Thu Bridge and Royal Bridge... and its connection to the Vu Yen resort area, a place so beautiful it feels like stepping into a fairyland.

I think it's worth seeing just once.
It's near the night market, so I saw it while browsing,
but I wondered, "Why bother?"
It was hard to catch a return Grab, so I walked for an hour before finally catching one.
It's very crowded, and it's incredibly hot.
There's a seating area, and it seems like you can just order a drink.
Sometimes, while you're sitting, a foreigner stands in front of you.
You have to pay to use the restroom.

This bridge is connected to the Seoncheon Night Market. On weekends, Yongmeori puts on a fire-breathing show, so it's packed with people, but the area beneath the bridge is occupied by street vendors...
You'll probably feel a sense of emptiness when you actually see the fire.

The dragon's head mounted on the bridgehead breathes fire three times in a row at 9 pm on Fridays, Saturdays, and Sundays.

It's fun to watch, but it's all over in three minutes.

We came all the way from Hoi An, but it wasn't really worth the trip.

We were expecting more of a spectacle.

We went to the bridge on July 7, 2025, to see the fireworks show, and then the Dragon Bridge show.
The fireworks were announced for 8:00 PM, but they started at 8:40 PM. The crowd stood on their feet the entire time in the sweltering heat. When the fake fireworks started, it was simply disappointing. We decided to wait for the Dragon Bridge show, which we waited until 8:20 PM, but it never started.
Tired and disappointed, we left the place; we have no desire to return.

Very disappointed.
It spit fire for 2 minutes, and it sprays water just as much.
It's very busy, so good luck finding a spot if you're driving.
And if you're on a scooter, you'll be forced to park in a paid lot (even if the price is ridiculous).

There were so many people, it felt like I was fleeing a war zone. They were yelling and spraying water.

Maybe I was expecting too much...ㅜㅜ
At 9 PM, they'll spray fire and water three times.
It's great for taking a walk. Since the hotel is right in front, I often went back and forth. I think it looks even better at night from the hotel.
It is literally a bridge with a dragon model placed on top.

They do a fire/water show on the weekends at 8pm if I remember correctly. I wouldn't go out of my way to see it but if you're around its pretty cool. My and my friends did get interviewed by VTV which is their local news station? I would love to have seen what they actually aired lol.
It's nice during the day, but seeing Jiragon lit up at night is definitely worth it. I only passed by this time, but I would love to stop and take a closer look sometime.
Considering the overall light show offered nightly in Da Nang, the fire-breathing dragon seems rather lazy.

Spent the evening in waterfront restaurant - then wandered along the riverside watching the bridges change colour, the brightly lit up boats in the river and the people walking until 9pm when the dragon breathed fire and water!!!The fire and water were a bit of an anticlimax to the evening.
Great bridge to see at night when the lights change colour; Disappointed not to have seen the beast breathe fire since it was only the weekend; Very lively area on the dragon's tail side.
Lovely to take a romantic stroll along the Riverfront with your loved one to view the Dragon Bridge lighted up after dinner on weekends to view the Dragon spits both water & fire, simply cool !!
We were able to admire the bridge from our hotel room, having previously crossed it in our tour bus.

I walked from my hotel to see the dragon bridge. Make sure to go at night because that is the best time to view it all lit up with the various rotating colors. Not much to do besides walk along the sidewalk and take a few pics. Lots of good restaurants nearby.

They've transformed the arches of a bridge into an attraction that draws huge crowds, both tourists and locals, at 9:00 pm every Saturday and Sunday. A few minutes before 9:00, the bridge is closed to traffic, after which the dragon's head begins to spit fire. This happens for two sets, and then, in the final set, it spits water instead. The whole thing lasts no more than five minutes. It's especially popular with Asians, but nothing particularly exciting.

Good but it let out fire 5 times then paused for ten miniutes and it was boaring to wait and if you go there make sure to go somewhere where you can see the head and make sure you donˊt sžand on the bridge because youl get wet.

The dragon bridge become "Chameleon" de the the light show. The walk along the river at night is nice, beside the dragon bridge there is another swing brigde at the opposite direction. A lot cafe, pub and bar along the river front, grab a cup if you are tired after walk.

The bridge faces the sea, and every Saturday and Sunday night at 9 PM, it breathes fire from its mouth. Afterwards, it sprays water to extinguish the fire (laughs). It's a beautiful bridge and can be seen from far away. Many shops line the bridge, making it an excellent tourist spot.

The bridge itself is beautiful, but those heading to the fire/water show shouldn't expect too much. It's just a brief burst of water followed by fire, and then it's over. The crowds are much more interesting!
We was underwhelmed by this experience, although very good for kiddies.The surrounding bars are extremely pricey.

The show was interesting and it's probably worth seeing but do not expect too much cause it only lasts for 10 minutes. If you like to get wet, you should probably be on the bridge during the spectacle. It's good to get there a little earlier and observe the preparations.
While staying in Da Nang on Saturday and Sunday, I went out for walks after sunset.
Photography enthusiasts will surely enjoy taking pictures from the best possible angles, capturing all the colors. But that alone doesn't justify a trip to this city.

My partner and I had this on our to do list during our stay in Da Nang. I had found out that it only breathes fire and water on weekends at 9PM so decided we would go in have dinner and do a bit of shopping before heading over to the bridge. When it become dark the lights of bridge light up in various colours changing the colour of the Dragon. I couldn't wait to see the big finale. It was also cool to see the wooden boats in the water who must do dinners all lit up in different colours travelling up and down the river until it was show time. To be honest i was a little underwhelmed by it. I mean its amazing a bridge can breath fire and water but i guess i was expecting it to be bigger.
I saw it while passing by, it's a nice idea that all towns should adopt, to make the views more lively, when lit it gives the best
It s nice to see,  but not that big of a deal.Friday and Saturday nights, the dragon spits fire and water!
I stayed in Da Nang two years ago, so I saw it from the car window on my way back to the hotel from the city.

On Saturday and Sunday evenings at 9 PM, there is a fire and water show at the dragon head on the bridge. If you happen to be in Da Nang, it's worth seeing, but if the timing doesn't work out, it's not a big deal. The decorative lights on the bridges over the river are also beautiful at night.

First of all, I realise that I'm just too spoiled with spectacular shows of all sorts back home. Our expectations of the fire show (every saturday and sunday, 9pm) were high, especially when we saw the massive crowd gathering on and around the bridge. Local families and bus loads of tourists come to the happening. The 'show' consists of 3 bursts of fire coming out of the mouth, followed by a big stream of water. Maximum 5 minutes or so. Don't get me wrong: the bridge is truly beautiful and I've never seen a firebreathing bridge. I just think the show is a bit overrated. Only go there if you happen to be around, would be my advice.

A guidebook called it a quirky bridge and it is indeed! We didn't stay for the fire breathing at 9pm but i must say they must really like dragons to build a massive decorative structure like this on a bridge.
I didn't go out of my way to visit it as a tourist, but you can see it from almost every taxi and tour bus.

To be honest  I went and saw the dragon breath fire and water! Apart from the spectical of seeing the insane traffic chaos after the event, it just wasn't worth the effort.The show went for about 5 minutes max with a few spouts of fire and a lot of water spray. Ordinary!It is worth seeing the dragon bridge at night as it is unique and the light changes that makes for a nice splash of colour.
The fire-breathing only happens on weekends, and unfortunately, we didn't get to see it.

Every Saturday and Sunday at precisely 9:00 PM, the dragon breathes fire and water. Many locals and tourists gather at the head of the bridge to admire the spectacle, which lasts about 2-5 minutes. It was worth a visit, but nothing particularly special.
You'll see it. No problem. It's the bridge in the middle of the city and everything along the river is around it. Can't miss it. A good photo opp.

It is just an ordinary bridge. Although they claim to have expressed the details of a dragon, don't go there specifically for sightseeing. It's only worth visiting if you're combining it with some sightseeing in the city center.

If you are going on weekday, try going about 5.30pm when there is daytime, then go for dinner nearby, and then see it with the night lights on. Nothing much amazing but if you have nothing to do (which there is not much to do in Da Nang), it is to take a look and a photo. There are some local eateries near the dragon head, but there aren't much English.
I stopped by this large bridge in Da Nang to buy souvenirs. The view from DHC Marina, a popular spot for couples, is a great photo opportunity. The location is fantastic.

It's truly an impressive structure, and despite the large dragon, which, we were told, only breathes fire and water every Sunday, it remains just another bridge, of which there are quite a few in Da Nang. If you're planning a visit to see the fire and water spectacle, be aware that it gets crowded and you might get wet, depending on how close to the front you stand.
Why a dragon?!
An interesting bridge to visit, especially at night when it's illuminated. During the day, there's nothing particularly noteworthy.
Maybe it's because I went on a weekday and missed the activities, but it
I saw it on my way to the restaurant for dinner, so on my way back I walked across it as a stroll.
Hired a pedicab from the city which takes you to the next bridge so you can see the whole area. Went to the eastern end for the fire works and water show which was a bit overrated but worth the look.
It is literally just a bridge, but I heard that it shoots fireworks on weekend nights.

We were expecting some kind of show, but it's just 15 minutes of fire and water, a color change, and that's it. It takes place on Saturday and Sunday at 9:00 PM. Vietnamese people are constantly wandering among the crowds of onlookers with their food carts and various silly souvenirs. Basically, it's all designed to draw more people to the embankment and sell them all sorts of useless things.
Even if you pass by or view it from a distance during the day, it doesn't leave much of an impression, but at night, the scenery changes dramatically as the lights reflect off the water.
Went in the day time and did not get to see any lights. The bridge looks pretty average. The dragon head looks a little too small for the dragon body.
Therefore, you don't need to go to Da Nang. If you happen to be there, you can take a look, but in my opinion, going there specifically is not necessary.
Best to visit during weekend where both locals and tourist will flood area surrounding the "Dragon Head" where it will then "blow fire" for 3 times when the clock strike 9pm
Interesting without being spectacular. The fire from the "dragons" mouth is only on Sat and Sun evenings and is a bit over hyped.

Due to time constraints, we didn't go at night and only saw it when we passed by at noon. It's basically a bridge with dragon statues on both sides, but the tour guide said that the dragon heads spew water and fire every Saturday night, which unfortunately we missed.

When we heard the dragon 'spit fire' every Saturday and Sunday evening in Danang we were super excited. The view at night is absolutely beautiful however the show they described was hardly that. At 9pm fire emerges out of the dragons head followed by a shoot of water. It wasn't as cool as we were hoping but it's worth seeing if you're in Danang anyway. Takes around 5 min. Tip: get a drink at the restaurant to the right of the dragon head.. there's an upstairs terrace. You'll have the view, a drink in hand and won't have to worry about getting a 'spot' to see!

I took a walk after having dinner at a nearby restaurant, and the dragon-shaped bridge was impressive. It isn't really worth making a separate itinerary to visit, but it is a nice place to check out if you get the chance.

At sunset, its very pleasant to stroll across this bridge.  Night lights, riverside chill spots and mingling with the locals as they unwind. Busy traffic as this is a popular route but still pretty orderly.  The river cruises and seafood restaurant boats dressed in blinking lights beckons..

If u are about this place, remember to wait n see the breathing fire dragon, we didnt get to see. It should be good. Walk across the bridge at night and see / take pics of the led light bridge and ferris wheel in the distance.  Watch time go pass by the Han riverside broadwalk with Richard Clademen......... piano on the public loud speakers.......

I was staying at a hotel a bit far from downtown Da Nang, and since I had passed by the area many times by taxi during the day, I decided against going out at night to see the illuminated dragons and the fire-breathing dragons.
Ok so this is a nice enough looking bridge. But during the day it's really nothing particularly special - a yellow steel dragon above a traffic bridge. I didn't get to see it at night.

You wouldn't know what it is if you saw it during the day. You have to see it at night after the lights come on to realize, "Ah, it's the Dragon Bridge." If you take a walk along the river at night, the lighting is beautiful and the bridge is uniquely well-made.
Its a bridge. Pretty to watch at night, but not that special. We didnt get the weekend "show" though.

The bridge itself has an interesting aesthetic solution, taking advantage of the structure to create the shape of a dragon – an animal present in the popular imagination – which is illuminated at night, with changes in color. What is most unusual, bringing together many locals, is that on weekends, at 9:00 PM, the dragon breathes fire several times and, at the end, releases a large jet of water. Unsuspecting people who are near the dragon's head can get wet – which, on a cold night, was the subject of complaints from some of those who were with us. If you are in town on the weekend, it's worth checking out.

I had high expectations after a Vietnamese trainee told me, "Da Nang is great," but unlike the Vietnamese, I felt that "while the night view is beautiful, I prefer the night view of Hoi An." The city of Da Nang is illuminated with lights at night, which is nice to see for free, but it's purely a modern show. Tourists who aren't looking for that kind of thing might not give it their highest rating.
I arrived in Da Nang at night, and the Dragon Bridge, which I saw from the hotel transfer vehicle, was impressive and beautiful.
Pride of Danang. Great feat of engineering - Saturdays night at 9pm the dragon spits out fire and water - worth a view

Since they are tourists and it is a designated tourist spot, everyone will probably visit. Just one of a few bridges that cross the Han River in Danang. It breathes fire on a Saturday and Sunday night at 9 pm but that is all I can say about it..

So we went on a Saturday night where at 9pm the dragon breathes fire and water whilst the bridge changes colour. It lasted about 10 minutes and was okay but not very exciting so don't expect too much. I preferred the bridge in the day as you could see the detail better.

This is a sculpture where a golden dragon shines during the day, and a colorful dragon bathed in lights at night. Actually, there isn't much to do here, but it is a good spot to take a commemorative photo from a distance. It is a landmark of Da Nang.

If you are in Da Nang,you have to see this dragon Bridge. To see this you need not add any slot in your travel as where ever you go you have to pass over the bridge so you are any which way going to see it! But it's worth seeing once.

Went over this bridge a dozen times in the day time. Its nothing special but the locals are proud of it and was told that it lights up and shoots water and fire 10.30 at night. Just didn't feel like the sort of thing that we needed to keep our infant kids up late to see.
we waited along the river which is a short walk from our hotel. the show is about 15-20 minutes. Many tourists and locals take pictures from our spot.
I didn't watch the fire show at night so I didn't find this bridge being spectacular. It was cool to see it from afar but I would not go out of my way to check this out.
the bridge is very impressive to look at during the night, it will breath fire & water exactly at 9pm every Saturday and Sunday. The traffic will be quite heavy at that time so you must be caution.
It looked nice at night but was ordinary during the day. It was

Just a metallic structure bridge composed with three arcades looking like a dragon,  nothing more to say. It's enlighten during the night.
This is one famous landmark of danang. We tried walking the long bridge and it was a beautiful view from the bridge. The bridge might be more beautiful with lights at night.

It is just a bridge. I went to the Dragon Bridge because I had some time to spare before ticketing. One of the bridges that crosses the city is the Dragon Bridge, a bridge with a dragon that changes color and from what we understood, every 15th of the month it breathes fire, very particular but nothing special.....

The best thing about the Dragon Bridge is that it breaths fire and water on Friday and Saturday nights at 9pm. It's very beautifully lit and watching it spew fire and water was a lot of fun. But I wouldn't go out of my way to see it again. It was fun to watch with my seven year old and if I happened to be in Da Nang again when it was going to show I would see it, but I wouldn't make a special trip just for that.
Best seen at night, as it changes color and even breathes fire (apparently). A must-see if you're ever in Da Nang!

Good to see the bridge at night. Having tasty Vietnam style coffee at river side cafe is recommended.

No need to make a special trip, because you'll pass by it on your way to the beach. I rented a motorcycle and rode back and forth several times. You can stop and take pictures; it's an interestingly shaped bridge, and the photos look better at night when it's lit up.

It's quite a show on the weekend, the crowds will gather surround the bridge from all direction specially at the head of the dragon! The best spot will be at the right or left in front the bridge but you have to beat the crowds! The option are the terrace boat house restaurant or the boat trip!

Because it's located in an urban area, there's a lot of traffic even during the day. I crossed the bridge by taxi during the day, and it was a yellow steel bridge. If you're going to visit, I think it's much better to see it at night, from a little distance away, when it's lit up. In any case, it gets very congested near the bridge.
A drive by curiosity. Not really that much to see. But if you are going to cross the river, and staying in Da Nang, it is more or less bound to happen, then choose this bridge at least one time.
Flames shoot out from Dragon Bridge on weekends!
It is a place where knowing just that such a bridge exists is enough.

They say fire comes out of the dragon's mouth on weekends, but for me and my friend, the Dragon Bridge is just something we notice while riding in a taxi and say, "Wow, what a pretty bridge!" Nice looking bridge! Wonderful at night seeing it from the seaside. The architecture is unique amd the lights enlights it with wonderful colour
If you see it in the daylight, it's nothing special. In the evening, with the entire waterfront lit up and the Dragon Bridge changing colors, the spectacle is pleasant and definitely worth a visit.

Dragon bridge. It's in Danang. It looks like a dragon. It's lit up at night. It has two other bridges near it, maybe 3. I like leaving Saigon on the weekends and spending my time walking through smaller towns, cities and villages. It's a bridge that looks like a dragon. You can walk over it and take photos. Would I take a tour, no. Would I recommend this as a must visit, no. If you are in Danang is there a good chance you will see it? Absolutely! Walk across and pop in waterfront for good food or Cong caphe for a great coffee!
At the end, a bridge with a dragon winding across it. Very busy, so you should walk unless it was raining heavily.
At daytime the bridhe is nothing really special but at night it lights up and is a nice view, you can see the dragon shape illuminated.
It's interesting to see the bridge lightened up like a dragon. However, it was raining cats and dogs when we were there and the wind was so strong that our umbrella broke!

Visually appealing from afar. Nice to walk on when you can also see the suspension bridge supposedly longest in Vietnam. Looked for an orienting map but was unsuccessful. Guide helped us but would have been clueless otherwise.

When I arrived Danang, it was Sunday night and the "Dragon" would roar with flame. I hurried to a nearby bridge to watch this moment but only find out it was three burst of flames for three times. Interesting but ...... I went past the bridge at daytime many times and it was more interesting to see how the bridge show its dragon look. I am not going to tell you and leave it to you to amuse yourself.

The bridge is widely used by people in their daily lives. The lightings on the bridge make the bridge come alive at night. The performance on weekend is overrated though. I was anticipating something spectacular but it just spits fire... and water... that's about it. Can come and take a look. But better without expectations. You will enjoy it better.

The bridge closed for traffic during the show. Once the show ends, everybody is on the streets trying to get a cab. Quite chaotic. There are also lots of bikes and traffic lights are few and not observed. Be very careful crossing the roads.

It is just a normal bridge that lights up a night. I wouldn't call it an attraction as it is simply part of their transportation infrastructure. Although my guide did mention that it will sprout fire over the nights during the weekends. We didn't managed to catch it as we left before the weekends.
We recommend viewing this bridge from another bridge as we stopped on the actual dragon bridge and up that close it was nothing much to look at. Night would be better too.
Bridge worked well getting us across the river, definitely worth seeing if traveling in Da Nang or passing through.

Its a bridge, its very good for getting from one side of the river to the other. At night its lit up, just like many of its bridge brothers around the world. When I went, the dragon head wasn't spouting water, so I just thought, "Ah, it's a bridge."
Excellent am just going to say get there early as many people there it's awesome.   Rather a Ten excellent, if out away from it use a taxi good and cheap

When I first saw it on my way from the airport to My Khe Beach, I wondered why it was called the Dragon Bridge, but when I looked at it from the Han River Bridge side, I could see a long, yellow structure shaped like a dragon lol. Both bridges are great for the night view as well.

Total 5 different type bridges in DaNang, I think Dragon Bridge is the one of beauties bridge, the design is mostly local culture of DaNang, and that there may be at least one thing about me worth your has take time to have a look in the night.

This bridge is a really great landmark in the city and the dragon shape makes a real connection to the Vietnamese culture.

Not worth the time if you are far. Don't need to invest your time and taxi money just to see the dragon blow fire out of it's mouth. Only last a few seconds and it's over. Nothing more...

If you are traveling back and forth in Da Nang, you can easily pass by the Dragon Bridge day and night by taxi; you don't need to make a special trip to see it. I had dinner at Madam Lan and walked along the Han River, watching the Dragon Bridge change color. I actually think seeing it by chance like this is more charming than making a special effort to go out of your way to find it.
By day it's quite inconspicuous, just a large bridge like many others.

Honestly, I wouldn't call this a 'must-visit.' While the idea of a fire-breathing bridge is cool. If you don't like being pushed around in a sea of people for a very short show, you’re better off skipping the crowd and just seeing the bridge when it's lit up on a quiet weekday evening.

Not entirely sure what all the fuss is about. Hundreds of adults gather crowding the place to see a dragon head spitting some fire and lots of water. Excessively touristy and naive for my taste.
The so-called "show"  takes place 9pm on weekends and lasts about 15 minutes.
The Dragon Bridge ( Cầu Rồng) itself is shaped like a giant golden dragon crossing the Han River, symbolizing power and prosperity in Vietnamese culture. It opened in 2013 and is about 666 meters long with 6 lanes.

The Dragon Bridge is an easy and tourist-friendly stop, with a night market right next to it. The fire-breathing dragon show runs every Friday, Saturday, and Sunday at 9:00 pm and can be viewed from both sides of the bridge and from the bridge itself.

Around Christmas, expect limited viewing spots. Street vendors often set up tables and chairs limited for their own customers, and a large Christmas tree and decorations can block some of the best views. Finding a clear spot can be tricky unless you squeeze in a photo opportunity in between trees or watch from a nearby café.

The dragon only breathes fire a few times before switching to water, which was a bit disappointing, and the whole show lasts less than 5 minutes. On our visit, the bridge was closed to traffic about 15 minutes beforehand, allowing people to walk onto the road and take photos. Security was visible, and everything felt safe and well organized. Worth seeing once—just don’t expect a long or dramatic show.

I visited the dragon bridge on a Friday, but unfortunately, the fire show was canceled and was only a water show. It was a bummer, especially since a large crowd had been waiting eagerly for the fire show. Despite this, the bridge itself was still beautiful, and the neon boats by the water created a nice atmosphere.

Mixed Experience at Da Nang’s Dragon Bridge

We visited the Da Nang Dragon Bridge during the daytime, and while it was definitely worth seeing, the experience was a bit mixed. The bridge itself is impressive and beautifully designed, but during the day, it’s extremely busy with traffic, making it difficult to fully appreciate its grandeur.

That said, since we visited during Tet, the usually crowded area was surprisingly quiet, allowing us to take in the sights without the usual hustle and bustle. However, if you want to see the bridge at its best, it’s probably more spectacular at night when it’s illuminated or during the weekend fire-breathing shows.

Overall, it’s a Da Nang landmark that’s worth a quick stop, but don’t expect a peaceful walk across it during regular hours! 🚗🔥✨

Every Saturday and Sunday at 9:00 PM, the Dragon Bridge offers a spectacular fire and water show. At the appointed time, the dragon seems to come alive, spewing gigantic fireballs for a few seconds. After a few minutes, it begins to spout a massive jet of water that shoots dozens of meters. The show lasts only a few minutes, but it's such a popular attraction that the bridge is closed to traffic so thousands of people can watch. It's impressive to see so many people for something that lasts so little time, but it's definitely worth taking a few minutes to see it; the photos and videos are fantastic. It's important not to get too close if you don't want to get soaked, and as soon as it ends, the police reopen the road to traffic within minutes, so be careful and move away as quickly as possible. The crowd will sweep you along, but it's best to stay to the side and wait a few minutes for it to clear. Afterward, a few meters from the bridge, you can enjoy a market with numerous stalls selling traditional street food and drinks.

Among the several scenic bridges connecting Da Nang's city center and beaches, the most famous and dazzling is the Dragon Bridge, the world's largest dragon-shaped bridge, whose lights change over time and are quite beautiful from a distance.

It's recommended to watch from under the bridge unless you want to experience the refreshing feeling of being sprayed with water by the dragon, in which case you can watch from the bridge.

Dragon Bridge in Da Nang, Vietnam, illuminated at night over the Han River. The bridge is a famous landmark known for its unique design shaped like a golden dragon, symbolizing power and prosperity in Vietnamese culture.
It is about 650 meters long and was opened in 2013. On weekend nights (Friday, Saturday, and Sunday) at 9:00 PM, the dragon's head performs a spectacular show, breathing fire and then water into the air.
The bridge's colorful LED lighting, as seen in the image, reflects beautifully on the river, attracting crowds along the riverbanks and on boat cruises.

The Dragon Bridge is absolutely beautiful, especially the show in the evening. It’s truly impressive to watch.

The only recommendation I would give is this: after the fire show, there is also a water show. Make sure to check which direction the wind is blowing and avoid standing directly under the dragon — otherwise, you will end up completely soaked.

Dragon Bridge — Cầu Rồng — is one of Đà Nẵng’s most iconic landmarks, and it’s impossible to miss its bright golden dragon stretching proudly across the river. By day, it’s a striking piece of architecture, but by night it becomes a real spectacle, glowing with colourful lights that reflect beautifully on the water.

If you’re in Đà Nẵng on a weekend, the highlight is the dragon’s fire and water show, usually on Saturday and Sunday evenings. Crowds gather to watch the dragon breathe real flames and spray water — a fun, lively experience for families, couples, and anyone seeing the city for the first time.

The bridge is perfectly placed for a riverside walk, with easy access to cafés, night markets, and viewpoints along both sides of the river. Whether you cross it on foot, drive over it, or admire it from below, it’s a must-see symbol of Đà Nẵng’s modern, energetic spirit.

Dragon Bridge is a must-see when you're in Da Nang. The design is super unique, especially at night when it lights up. The Dragon Bridge in Da Nang is one of those landmarks that actually lives up to its hype. By day it’s an impressive piece of modern design—bold, clean lines, and that massive dragon structure stretching across the river. By night it shifts gears completely. The lights kick in, the bridge glows, and the city feels alive around it.

The real spectacle is the weekend fire and water show. It’s touristy, sure, but standing there with the crowd as the dragon breathes fire over the river is flat-out fun. If you’re in Da Nang, make sure to catch the Dragon Bridge show at least once. It might not sound like much at first — after all, Da Nang isn’t known for flashy street performances — but it’s surprisingly impressive.

The show lasts around 15–20 minutes, and seeing a giant dragon breathe fire and water over the river is a pretty unique experience, especially with the city lights in the background. Just make sure to check the schedule — it usually happens on weekend nights — and get there a bit early if you want a good spot.

It’s not a life-changing event, but it’s definitely one of those “only in Da Nang” moments that’s worth checking out while you’re in town.

During our cruise excursion, we had the opportunity to visit the Da Nang Dragon Bridge, and it was truly an impressive sight. The bridge spans the Han River and is known for its unique design, which features a giant dragon that appears to be breathing fire or water at certain times of the day. Completed in 2013, the Dragon Bridge has quickly become one of Da Nang’s most iconic landmarks, drawing visitors from all over the world.

The design of the bridge is visually stunning, with its dragon-shaped structure symbolizing power, strength, and good fortune in Vietnamese culture. As we walked along the bridge, we marveled at its sleek, modern architecture and the striking view of the river and city skyline. The atmosphere was lively, with locals and tourists alike stopping to take photos and enjoy the view.

One of the highlights of our visit was witnessing the bridge’s nightly show. On weekends, the dragon comes to life, breathing fire and water at 9 p.m. This spectacle is a must-see and adds an extra layer of excitement to the experience.

Overall, we thoroughly enjoyed our visit to the Da Nang Dragon Bridge and would highly recommend it to anyone visiting the city. Whether you’re admiring the bridge by day or witnessing the fiery display at night, it’s an unforgettable sight that truly captures the spirit of Da Nang.

My recent visit to Da Nang's Dragon Bridge, complemented by a Han River evening cruise, was an unforgettable experience that beautifully showcased the city's vibrant nightlife and cultural allure.

The Dragon Bridge itself is a marvel of modern engineering, especially captivating during its weekend fire and water show.  At 9 PM on Fridays, Saturdays, and Sundays, the bridge comes alive as the dragon's head breathes fire and sprays water, creating a spectacular display that reflects brilliantly off the Han River's surface  . Viewing this from the cruise offered a unique and mesmerizing perspective.

The cruise was well-organized and provided a comfortable setting to enjoy the city's illuminated skyline.  The ambiance was enhanced by traditional Cham dance performances on board, adding a cultural touch to the evening  . While the cruise was generally smooth, it's worth noting that some guests have mentioned occasional delays and seating arrangements as areas for improvement  .

Overall, the combination of the Dragon Bridge's dynamic show and the serene Han River cruise offered a delightful blend of excitement and relaxation.  It's an experience I'd highly recommend to anyone visiting Da Nang, especially on weekend evenings when the city's charm is on full display.
It’s a must-do in Da Nang. The show starts at 9 pm, and watching the Dragon Bridge breathe fire and water is truly iconic and one of the highlights of the city.

The Dragon Bridge (Cầu Rồng) is one of the most iconic landmarks in Da Nang, Vietnam, spanning the Han River.

Here is a detailed introduction to the Dragon Bridge:

🐲 Dragon Bridge Overview

• Location: Located in the heart of Da Nang, Vietnam, spanning the Han River and connecting the city center with My Khe Beach.

• Opening: Officially opened to traffic on March 29, 2013, to commemorate the 38th anniversary of Da Nang's liberation.

• Design: The bridge is designed as a golden dragon facing the East Sea, inspired by the dragon of the Ly Dynasty in Vietnam, symbolizing strength, power, prosperity, and Da Nang's ambition for continuous development.

• Scale: The bridge is approximately 666 meters long and has six lanes for motor vehicles.

• Night View: Equipped with over 2,500 or 15,000 LED lights (sources may vary slightly), the lights continuously change colors at night, creating a dazzling night view on the Han River.

🔥 Weekend Fire and Water Show

This is the most attractive feature of the Dragon Bridge:

• Time: Every weekend (Saturday and Sunday) and major public holidays, starting promptly at 9:00 PM.

• Show Content: The dragon's head first spews forth roaring flames (approximately two minutes, about 9 times), followed by water sprays (approximately three minutes, about 3 times). The entire show lasts approximately 10 to 15 minutes.

• Viewing Tips: It is recommended to arrive early to secure a good spot. Best viewing locations include:

• The riverside walkways and viewpoints on both sides of the Dragon Bridge.

• The nearby Love Bridge (Cầu Tình Yêu).

• Viewing from a Han River cruise (advance booking required).

• Nearby riverside cafes or restaurants.

📍 Nearby Attractions

The Dragon Bridge is located in a prime location in Da Nang city, near several tourist hotspots:

• Love Pier (Cầu Tình Yêu): Located near the dragon head, it's a popular spot for couples to take photos and hang love locks.

• Son Tra Night Market: Located below the dragon head, it offers a variety of street food and souvenirs.

• Han Market (Chợ Hàn): A famous shopping market.

• Museum of Cham Sculpture.

If you're planning a visit to Da Nang, be sure to include the Dragon Bridge fire and water show in your itinerary this weekend!

The moment just before the show is most impressive. The bridge is already closed to traffic, people are gathering on both sides of the Han River, and there's a palpable tension in the air. The illuminated dragon – long, monumental, winding over the river – looks as if it's about to awaken. The city lights reflect in the water, and the silence and anticipation create an almost cinematic atmosphere. This is the moment when the scale of this attraction truly feels palpable.

🔥 Fire Show

The climax begins with fire. The dragon raises its head, and after a short pause, powerful tongues of flame shoot from its mouth. The heat is felt even from a distance, and the roar of the fire is immense. Each burst is clear, rhythmic, and spectacular – this is not a symbolic attraction, but a true, powerful display that elicits screams of delight from the audience.

💦 Water Show

After the fire comes water – contrasting, yet equally impressive. The dragon begins to exhale powerful jets of air, cooling the heated air and often reaching the audience closest to it. This element adds a relaxed and joyful touch to the spectacle – laughter, shouts, and spontaneous reactions are all part of the experience.

The fountain appears 5 minutes after the flames. It flies surprisingly far and gets blown around by the wind, so it's best to read the wind and find a way out of the way.
An ideal place for fun, with beautiful night views, excellent tourist services, and peace of mind regarding security and order. The locals are friendly and pleasant.

Learn from experience, don't go up the bridge. You'll get soaking wet. After the three fire blasts, there will be three water blasts. The water is incredibly powerful; standing underneath feels like being drenched in rain. It's best to stay below rather than going up the bridge.

The Fire & Water performance was really good. 
I especially enjoyed the water, even though I was already soaked.
This is a great place, there was a night show, I visited the fiery dragon bridge, it was crowded, probably because I went during the Vietnam Festival.
The Dragon Bridge, according to the tour guide, sometimes breathes fire; it's a very beautiful attraction.

Dangerous! We stayed in a room here and the fire smoke came into the room. From the fire they lit. I asked reception for help. They said close the windows…. Then open them…. Then it took 2.5 hours for a manager to offer another room. It totally ruined the experience for me. They said it wasn’t their issue and was possibly caused by wind??!!Quirky hotel, great rooms but not worth the safety of my children. Very poor customer service!!
Very poor place and not worth it and the amount paid About a staircase. Your ticket pays in . Five minutes in and out where it’s not worth it
Nonsense, all dilapidated, scary, dangerous for children. Very disappointed. Sorry to waste time. You can't use clump as an apartment, as an exhibit - dirty.
I had a very bad experience here. Ngọc Thảo the manager refused to talk with client when there is a problem. Should avoid this place.
Hideous, dreadful, horrible, words fail me to describe this place, which has absolutely no redeeming qualities...what a disgrace to have allowed such a building to be constructed

The staffs were very friendly at the entrance and spends a few minutes explaining the history of the house. As we enter, i realise how dangerous the stairs are for children as kids can just trip over the many high outdoor stairs to the ground. Very low hand rails. I was scared to climb higher. I would not visit again. It is a very crazy house that is unsafe for children.
Bus loads of of tourists go there ,and very hard to see anything since there is so many tourists, people come here cause there is not much to do in da lat

We visited this place as part of a tour to Dalat. I have just one question: what inspired this place? It's completely incomparable to Gaudi, with his architecture and style. The Zakos are certainly impressive, but this one just looks like a Chinese knockoff! The atmosphere is also terrible! Aunt Natasha (that's the name of the woman who studied for many years in Russia and came up with all this) clearly read too many Russian folk tales and fairy tales about Baba Yaga. Her apartment looks exactly like a hen's hut. The altar in the middle, dedicated to herself and her father, is also a bit creepy. It's a creepy place. Overall, if there's absolutely nothing else to see, you can go in and see room number 6. But the way it's marketed for tourists is absolutely terrifying. We didn't even take any photos.
I did this because Da Lat doesn't have much else to offer. If you have the time, you can do it. It's quite cheap at 60,000 VND. But it's boring.

A night spent in this hotel is truly a treat. Kids will probably love it. The rush to visit this cultural and recreational "monument" is baffling. But the Vietnamese have a chance to appreciate Gaudi-style work in their own country.
Don’t even bother visiting.Worst tourist trap ever.It’s like a kids playground . Supposed to be haunted.? Sure!

Ridiculous, pointless. It adds nothing, not even to the city's aesthetics. We basically only went in because it was raining, but it's not worth spending even a small amount because there's nothing to see.
Definitely unnecessary
Waste of time and money. I wish I could give it a zero review. Spend your money and time anywhere else but this overrated garbage.

We were excited to visit but found the whole place a bit depressing. Full of tourists after that perfect shot. All concrete and quite ugly rather than amazing architecture. Save money and go and have a drink in similar but better maze bar!! Cafe across road is nice.
There were a lot of people. Hard to get around and none of it made any sense. It was all the same. Can think of many things to do instead of 1 1/2 hours here

It was stated as must visit sit when I. Dalat. What a disappointment and it is an absolutely waste of time. It's a hotel where people can peek to see how the room is. Its quirky and in some parts eerie.  It's a small area and enable you to climb around. It's still under renovation.  Its 50k admission fee and it is absolutely not worth the visit. Better use the money for a good cup of coffee over looking the hills with beautiful scenery.

Not worth going there for 50,000 VND per person; actually, not even worth spending your time on. It is a maze of walkways through sort of artistic fake trees. Not comparable to the Efteling; I would rather walk through the Land of Laaf all day.

Once inside, I had no idea what to do. The scenery had no style whatsoever. It was rough and ugly. Rocks and construction materials were scattered everywhere. After leaving, I didn't even know if it was a tourist attraction or a place to book accommodation.

The house has a unique and beautiful design. Especially the Ocean Room, which wasn't designed exactly as I wanted; it's very vibrant and great for photos. The winding pathways high above resemble a dragon coiled around the entire house. I won't turn back.
I don't really understand why this is considered a tourist attraction and why it's included in tour packages.

Not the most fun thing to do in Da Lat. It is not worth the money. A lot of constructionwork is going on! Staff is friendly, but you won't get any information about the house or what so ever. Overhyped!

Really not very good at all and very tacky. The best aspect of this is the good views from the high points over Da Lat. I appreciate 50k Dong is not a lot of money but I really cannot recommend this place.

Heard this was a main attraction in Da Lat but really was uninspiring. Yeah there’s weird and wacky architecture, but just looked tacky and made no sense.A lot of it is under construction.A local asked me to hold his baby there, maybe white people are lucky? Lol

We had heard of the Crazy House before we went to Da Lat and it was a let down. A lot of construction going on it was basically a construction site with a bit of art on the walls. Go to the Maze Bar instead!!

Besides the fact that many tourists block the road with large buses so that you cannot see the house properly, the exterior has become unsightly due to the electrical wires and/or lighting. Consequently, this was a 5-minute stop for us.

I can honestly say we have never been treated worse, anywhere in the world. After six hours in the car , my family and I arrived and were immediately demanded money ( we were even able to put down our bags and were blocked from entering) Then we were made to pay 500 000 dong to get our passports back. There is no one to help you carry your bags, no “welcome” , no wifi , no restaurant , no bar and so on.When we tried to leave for dinner, they again demanded more money and shouted at us in front of everyone when we refused. Finally we spoke to a manager , who dismissed us like we were bothering him. The very worst part is that the tours start arriving by the bus load at 7 am. People stomp by the room, try to open the bedroom and even bang on the door to get in - even with the do not disturb sign on the door.You truly feel like you are in a zoo and are treated like poo by the staff. Just a nightmare!

I really do not see the point of coming to this place. I think it is a waste of time and money. Crazy house is basically a home stay with some animal-themed rooms, connected with endless stairs, bending and winding up and down. The rooms look small and suffocating, might not be suitable for people with claustrophobia.

Extremely overrated. Its a cheap Gaudi/Hundertwasser copy that is still under construction so it is dirty, poorly lit at night and some of the high walkways have low handrails and are dangerous. Save your money for something more interesting rather than subsiding their building project.

Some people call it Disney-style decor... uh, sorry, but Disneyland parks are a thousand times better maintained than this mess where the paint is peeling everywhere, some of the "effects" only half work, are half under construction, and are quite dirty (old cups lying around, cigarette butts everywhere... for lack of trash cans). Disney wouldn't allow that. Furthermore, the groups of Russians with megaphones arriving in waves of a hundred (yes, really) only add to the claustrophobia. In the end, it's not even a fun place, clearly not designed to accommodate so many people, and is hugely overrated!
Complete waste of time and money for the benefit of rich Russian with a weird mind. Poor performance

I’m sure this house had very cool architecture, however we couldn’t see anything because it was absolutely packed with people. I couldn’t enjoy it because it’s novelty was worn off by tourists with selfie sticks in every direction. I would skip this and hit the 100 rooftops bar instead if you are looking for some seriously cool architecture

When traveling to Da Lat, people usually visit the Crazy House, but honestly, there is nothing to see. A haunted house? It is a space decorated like a fantasy village, but taking one or two photos is pretty much all you can get out of it. You should spend that time eating pho in downtown or expanding your time exploring the night market instead.

A half built mess of a place. Full of Russian and Chinese tour groups. Perhaps young children may find it slightly interesting but surely not adults.I had read somewhere that it could be compared to Gaudi in Barcelona....not anywhere near it!! Just avoid and save 50000.00 VDong and your precious time.

It was essentially a giant child's playground. The architecture itself was disappointing and there was no nothing clever about it. Also disappointing was that it's largely unfinished in areas with nails sticking out. The view at the top was ok but not worth price paid.

It really resembles a larger version of a children’s playground environment. If you’ve ever seen a children’s play area with a plastic tree trunk frame and a slide coming out of it, you’ve essentially seen a scaled down version of this construction.I do appreciate architecture and I love seeing unusual applications of materials when the application opens your eyes to new ideas and possibilities. This didn’t tick any of those boxes. It wasn’t clever like a Guastavino and it wasn’t crazy like a Gaudí, it was just concrete: Ugly and disappointing. We found the old buildings in town with their patterned grills and old gambling men under their weathered verandas far more interesting. At least there was some creativity in those.

This is the only time I think that Trip advisor did not advise me correctly :) This place is 50,000 dong (not expensive) but not worth the entry in my opinion as after 15 mins we were out the door. Parts of it are still under construction while most of it are private rooms for their guests staying overnight. All there was are stairs going up and down and just some weird architecture. Nothing impressed me especially given that there are so much more beautiful things to do in Vietnam. I would not recommend this. Complete waste of time and money.
We went to the crazy house and we did not enjoy it so much. We left after 10 minutes, it wasn't crazy and pretty boring..

Two days ago we went to the crazy house. It is pretty much under construction. Entrance is 50k, but surely not worth it. It just is a “alice in wonderland” house, nothing more, nothing less. We left the place in about 30 mins. Even after construction it will be not worth to visit. Just go the the maze bar (100 floors bar) instead, then at least you can get a beer for your money..

Entrance to the place was cheap, 50000 vnd each. We read that the place is like Alice in Wonderland and eccentric of sorts. We walked around but the walkways were narrow, had to wait for the others to finish taking their photos or walk across before we could do so. Tried wandering around and there were so much construction ongoing. Exposed rebars, nails, planks all lying around and such places were not cordoned off. Will not recommend little kids or elderly there as there are also steep steps.Would suggest better signages to go around the place.
No point at all! It took us 5 minutes to walk around the circuit. The entrance is pleasant, but the rest isn't worth the trip.
40,000 dong to visit this place is a decidedly exorbitant sum. Spend your money more wisely.

Don't understand what's the fuss about this place. A labyrinth of narrow stairs and eager tourists trying to figure out which way to go. Won't recommend it. Could spend your 30 minutes better somewheee else.

Completely out of context. Doesn't celebrate any views or the beautiful layout of Dalat. A complete indulgence and waste of entry money. Rumour says the owner ploughed lots of daddy's money in to it and probably got away with the eyesore because he was a government minister. Some of the high stairs are, frankly, dangerous. Spend your 40,000VND on something better.
I only rode my motorbike past the building, but the aggressive touts trying to get people in were a real turn-off.

I would go even if it was free. If you stay there as a hotel perhaps is a nice place if you go with children . But if you are a traveler and not a tourist I definitely don't recommend you to go, it is no expensive but ... you can do million of better things with that money. It is not finished and you have to struggle with people taking selfies in very narrow path in the high.This house is supposed to be one of the 10 strangest houses in the world but I think it is just Gaudi worst nightmare.

Da Lat is known to its colonial heritage, nature, and unique culture. The story of Crazy House revealed an ideology to make a difference. The location is in an alley uphill. It is not very far from the government tower which even looks acceptable. Dinh III Bao Dai Palace is not far from Crazy House too. A commercial tourism investment project has a break even point to cover the maintenance. Apparently Crazy House operated and remained unfinished for years with some reasons well known by local residents. In a successful tourism city, most buildings have better share the same coherence. Crazy House does not well fit the environment.
Went June 2017, 40k to get in which is cheap but the place just isn't finished, wires coming out the walls and the rooms are empty, nothing interactive or to do, would recommend maze bar instead

Don't know why everyone loves this so much. Just a big load of stone in the shape of a tree. All you do is walk up and down stairs. Literally nothing to do or see. Sometimes there's a "room" you can look in but it just a normal bed or table.I don't know anything about architecture but they seem weirdly proud of something that looks like it belongs in a pub playground. Maybe fun if you're a child but I wouldn't bother otherwise.
Absolutely not as interesting as the reviews are. Mostly stairs and does not look like a house at all. You have to pay for your scooter, entrance and inside are lots of shops. Tourist trap.
We are a 23-year-old couple, we didn't understand what all the fuss was about, we were bored, plus there were a lot of people, and it was crowded to move between places.

It is a popular sight in town and has groups running through all day long starting from 830 am till the evening.  It is impossible to get a rest at the place during they day and there are always lots of people around just in front of the room. Don't expect to get a rest there. The hotel is very noisy.The staff didn't help us to find a guided tour on our arrival and also assigned us the wrong room. So after moving into the room we had to change to a different room. After all a pretty bad experience.

This place is the worst place ever 40k to visite a mac donalds kids play park and you dont get a toy with your meal a tourist trap that wasent even finished they are still building it and it is realy realy not worth the detour go see waterfalls instead
Spent less than 5 minutes in the place. Not worth the 40,000vnd admission fee. The rooms are off limits.

There's an entrance fee, which I thought wasn't worth it, but since I was already there, I decided to check it out. Personally, I didn't think there was much to see. The walkways are narrow, and I was afraid of falling when walking past each other.

Entrance fee is really high for what you see. It's an unfinished facade that you can walk around, feels very fake. Kind of like a back alley of Disneyland. You walk around, competing with all the other tourists for spots on the tiny steps and bridges. Many parts are unfinished and filled with trash. It's a little like a maze which I guess could be fun if you are really, really bored. But there are so many other things to see in Dalat! Don't waste your time or the money.
I am not a city person. So, I am not enjoy the modern architecture much. The entrance ticket  cost 40,000 dongs for foreigner.

Nothing much to see except for the weird architecture of the house. My friends and I were kidding that anyone with some money can build a house with weird architecture and maze and start collecting entrance fee from visitors. That is basically what you get. I will even say as the railings were very short at certain places, it is quite dangerous for children to visit. Not suitable for pictures as well.

People come here for the craziness. Yes, it looks crazy. The stairs and rails are totally unsafe. Stair width is design for only one person. If there is an opposite one, you have to bend your body over the outside to move. The stair rails are all over the place and far from reach. It is more like a long tiny bridge. I was so scared when there is a line of more than 20 people walking on that bridge at the same time. It was over crowded and hard to explore peacefully because I was pushed to move forward. Plus, I have to take photo for strangers along the way too. ;( waste of your time and money. I walked out after 10 mins. I also saw a small wooden mask at the gift shop that were sold for 250,000 vnd. It is only worth 50,000 max at Dalat market. In general, people give a good reviews because of the weirdness.

I was concerned that the engineering was so lacking that the stairs and walkways may collapse at any moment. Several sections of stairway walls and railings had collapsed and not been repaired. Architecture from an aesthetic Point of view is subjective, but this mismatch of styles and lack of consideration for space sizes, natural light or any consistent design form has appropriately attributed to its name "crazy house". I must have banged my head at least a dozen times whilst trying to follow the walkways. I am 169cm tall.
The building is partially unfinished, apparently for a long time, it is not worth wasting time on it at all, otherwise you will be disappointed.
I was really disappointed!! It's very ugly, not safe and they basically sell you rooms that look like Disneyland in a glaucous atmosphere. A waste of time and money
Very bizarre! Some madman who thought he was Gaudí, but how can I put it... well... he failed miserably!

People like to visit, so some come. The construction is well done. Tourists buy tickets for 30,000 dong to come in (for what?) take pictures. The rooms are nicely decorated but locked. And if you're going to walk through any of the alleys, be careful of the Russians because they'll push their way in and don't know the meaning of queueing. Overall, it's crazy, just like the name suggests. I went in for about 5 minutes and then came out because I felt like I was going to go crazy myself.
This to me definitely not worth seeing. But they keep adding and adding so guess someone likes it. It is crazy and your crazy to go see it. Lol

Something to do for half an hour. I do not think it is safe walking around the roof top walkways because the balustrades are too low and inadequate. There  are open construction areas where you walk past reinforcing rod sticking upwards and unprotected. Many other countries would not allow such a dangerous site to be open to tourists.   It is too crowded with no crowd control (i.e.  No control on the direction people move) . It is free for all and you end up with 2 people trying to pass on stairways built for 1 person at a time. Complete waste of  time and 40k  Dong each
Full of tourist, bad architecture, bad taste, bad everything. Probably fun for kids but I'm not fun of a kids and of a bad taste.

Came here based on local recommendations. Totally wish i hadn't. At 40,000 dong a person this is quite expensive for what was a string of tight corridors jampacked with selfie stick waving tourists. Maybe fun if you have time to kill but personally your time is better spent seeing the waterfalls. Short version: Overpriced and overcrowded.

Since it's a must-see attraction, we went anyway. Tickets are 40,000 VND per person. Parking costs 5,000 VND. We initially parked opposite the Crazy House. Then a man came over and yelled at us to park there. Later, the Crazy House staff also told us to park there. In China, this would be a monopoly. The most shameless thing was that the man, for the sake of his business, went over and yelled at the person who looked after our car. I didn't understand what he meant, but his attitude was really rude!
Hard to understand why they call it a house and what architectural value it has...an ugly, unfinished, full of garbage, bad taste, unsafe to walk around, no purpose...something

A waste of time. Anyone with any sense will skip it. It's just a bunch of souvenir shops and hotel rooms in some cobbled-together, wannabe amusement park with absolutely no attractions. Everything is in a terrible state.
Children can fall off the edge. It's a pretty scary setup. I don't recommend it. The house was still partially unfinished when we visited here. Not worth going.

Whatever you read from the 4/5 points reviews, those are base on perspective and through all the facts you can find, you will find this place horribly bad and a waste of time.Famous (or infamous) for its crazy structures, I have no idea why this is rated so high. It's just a pile of totally misconstructed statues of animals and human with tunnels and stairs in it. It's a part maze part hotel mostly tourist attraction spot and even though you might find it exciting when you were walking through the tunnels, you end up getting disappointed because there just weren't any reason to do it.I'm glad I didnt't stay there.
We visited there 2 days ago, that was nice and no more. the coffee is good, and it can be a nice break stop but no more than that...
Didn't find any interesting in this place. It's a small concrete house. Very hot. They tried to set up it naturally but can't. It's really crazy idea.

I hate writing negative reviews but on this occasion feel that I must. I'd say we were in there no more than 10 minutes with it feeling completely and utterly bizarre. Relatively cheap at 40k VND but that is the only positive for me. With much of it dated/ under constriction  it felt like a waste of time and I struggle to see it's purpose (even if visiting with children).

A visit to this complex was part of a tour of Dalat. I'm writing purely my opinion and posting photos. I don't want to go back; it was a shocking, slightly off-putting experience. Damp, cold, and smelled unpleasant.
We didn't like this place at all. It's all stairs, shops, and crowds where you're pressed against each other to climb up or down the narrow steps. It's very close to the lake.
Very poor. Not worth the entrance fee. Really a disgrace getting charged to view something this ugly and poor - was basically a construction site. Simply a tourist trap gimmick.
I've never seen anything so useless in my entire life. It's kitschy, and it's a good thing it wasn't expensive, otherwise I would have asked for a refund.

This attraction should have been in a fun fair ! A man made construction of narrow difficult paths around a construction that had nothing to see other than a few shops! I learned nothing for my money ,this site has no history or attachment with Vietnam other than a money maker off the coach parties ,no doubt the drivers are rewarded well. Give it a miss! Not for the disabled or infirm.
I admire the work and creativity that has gone into creating this quirky building but I do not think it is worth paying to visit.

I always want to see things with my own eyes, and while our twenty-minute visit was entertaining, it is a bit too chaotic, kitsch, and fairytale-like for me. Entrance costs 40,000 dong, motorbike parking 5,000.

We stayed one night in the bear room. Pros: One Staff member is friendly.Cons:30 seconds of hot water in the shower.Overpriced. 50 USD a night. Other hotels on the area are 10 USD a night.Loud noisy tourists will try to enter your room at 8 in the morning. Ants in the bed.Toilet doesn't flush.There is no benefit to staying here over visiting for the day. Save your money and stay ANYWHERE else. If you ask the staff about the lack of hot water, you will be questioned on if you know how to use a shower. So if you like wasting money, being treated like a fool, then by all means stay here.
I guess it's something to do and close to town but not at all interesting for me. I would recommend going to 100 roofs bar for a more crazy and strange experience.

I don't know what I was expecting, but not what we got.This is a tacky hotel, I have no idea why people would stay here.There is construction going on everywhere, there are 4 buildings and they were working on all but one of them

I heard some good stuff about this place, so me and some friends went to see it. It was really boring totally a waste of money. If you think you are going to enter some kind of museum or an actual crazy house, think again. It's just houses that are built pretty weird, kinda like Gaudí, but that's it. It's just apartments. Save yourself the 40.000 dong and get a coffe somewhere instead.
It's not worth paying $2. It's not traditional architecture; it's a hotel designed by a local architect, but not very different from what you can see in Barcelona.

You have to pay 40000 vnd to get inside. Not much,  but really a waste of your time. You can walk around but there is nothing to see... Al the rooms are now hotel rooms so you can only walk outside on the stairs. A lot of Asian people who makes photos everywhere....

A recently erected structure in the style of Gaudi. It might be fun for children, but not really safe. They are still building, and here you can clamber between the concrete wires and bags of cement. The view is nice, but it is much more beautiful to see from other places.
What a waste of time. The only benefit is the viewpoints overlooking Dalat.
Architecturally, it's probably quite impressive if you know your way around... But for us personally, it was totally boring. Completely overrun with tourists... nothing special.

Don't really know what I was expecting from this place but the whole thing is very same same as you say here. Half of this place wasn't even finished, wouldn't recommend unless you're desperate for something to do
Personally, I didn't find it worth the effort or time. But everyone is free to take a look anyway.

It just feels like an unfinished themepark. The weird "back to the nature" style is not there cause of all the unfinished concrete parts. If you like giant plastic spider webs and 'crearive' (read: just totally unlogic) stairs and roads you should go there.High stairs, not safe for young kids.You have better things to do when you are in Dalat.
Creepy and gaudy. Doesn't give me the back to nature feel. Would hate to stay there with tourists traipsing by your room and photographing it. Don't  bother.

Really do not understand what the hype is about this attraction. We were brought here as part of a day tour and felt obliged to roam around before leaving, though my boyfriend and I both felt it was hideous. If you want to see something fake and gaudy then go to DisneyWorld, not here.
Not my scene at all.  Positively dangerous clambering round this still unfinished accommodation place.

I was brought here by a tour guide and I have never In my 24 years of living seen as much absolute tripe. Ok they used creativity to make it but what is its purpose? I got one good photo out of it, do not go here if you aren't a complete clampet.
Total rubbish. Lots of stairs, no way to peek into rooms. Absolutely nothing interesting. A complete waste of time.
I personally thought this was a bit over the top & not for me. There were lots of visitors there and they are extending the house to make it bigger

For an entrance fee of 40,000 dong, you can see this, at first glance, strange house. A labyrinth of bridges, some of which have significant safety deficiencies and/or are unfinished. You also see a few rooms, but they aren't really unusual or spectacular.
If you're traveling with kids, it's fine, but compared to everything else in Dalat, this place doesn't really stand out. It'll just be a waste of time.
Damn, idling... This could only be found in an amusement park. What a waste of time. It can easily be removed from the sightseeing tour!

We planned our Vietnam trip more or less in late 2014, and based on info online, we thought it would be fun to have a cup of coffee or lunch at the Crazy House. When we arrived, there were many tour buses and throngs of tourists outside. It turned out that the Crazy House has been "open for tourists" since December 2014, which means: buy a ticket for US$2 per person. No coffee or lunch! We were trampled by Japanese and Russians; the rooms (which are precisely what make it so special) were almost all closed to the public, so the only thing you could do was walk through the maze of corridors and bridges. It was incredibly crowded because they have no limit on the number of people they allow in. At the time we were there, there were about 200 people walking around! We fled quickly. Crazy House, indeed.
Just a building. Nothing to do inside, really boring. Suitable more for family with kids and not the average backpacker. Total waste of time and money. Not recommended!!!

Don't even think about going to Dalat! Especially this place. Yes, it's unusual, but it's another scam for Russian fools, run by Russian hohols, like everything else in Nha Trang! Dalat is a cold, damp city. It's strange that the "guides" who sell tours don't warn tourists to dress warmly, but instead stand there in jackets and sneakers. It's a four-hour drive along a winding road to see this half-built building, where slanted-eyed Russian thugs crowd! It's not even worth the $30!

Seriously? You're thinking of taking your child here?  DONT!!I only visited the place for an hour so can't comment on its suitability for accommodation. However what I can say that this place is unsafe, incomplete, a waste of Money and a few hours of your life you won't get back.  Avoid.  Go and are something nice like one of the waterfalls.

The place was nice. Half of the area was under a construction and not so good. You can see the place in a hour. We went there with our bikes and theres a security person to take care of scooters and bikes. It Costs 3000d but dont trust them! I forgot my speedmeter on my bike and it was gone when i got back. I confront the security about the matter and they just raised their shoulders. Luckily i chained my bike to a lamp post. Otherwise maybe that had gone too.
Not at all what I expected. You basically pay to walk around rooftops on narrow staircases. Save your money for something else. Also very dangerous for children.

It's basically just another theme park (made of cement) without rides. I somehow feel that the architect try to copy Gaudi's work, but it failed. The color is the worst of all, it looks cheap. Really disappointed!

This place should be called the Tacky House. I was expecting to find an artsy creative place and I was very dissapointed. The "tree house" it's all cement, the rooms were locked so you cant see inside and most of the place was under construction with wires creeping around. Not worth it!

This place should be called the Tacky House. I was expecting to find an artsy creative place and I was very dissapointed. The "tree house" it's all cement, the rooms were locked so you cant see inside and most of the place was under construction with wires creeping around. Not worth it!

Visited the Crazy House with expectations of beautiful, quirky architecture having taken inspiration from Sagrada Familia etc. Not at all the case! After being charged a somewhat extortionate VND 40,000 entrance ticket to what is essentially a construction site, was pretty disappointed that all the buildings are cement made and plasticy like a child's play/theme park. The inside of the buildings mostly aren't finished and you're just walking through rubble and hanging wires (a little dangerous and generally unpleasant). All very tacky.

Crazy House is certainly interesting, being a large and truly bizarre building in a fairly small area. Be careful as you go to the higher levels, as the strange bridges have very low sides (not one for people with vertigo). The building makes for a fun excursion, but with no real understanding of its purpose or meaning, is not a place to spend too long visiting.
Was worse than I expected, would be kinda cool for children but as an adult yeh not so much. Half under construction too
Didn't expect this to be so small. But I guess if you have nothing better to do you can try. There's a very nice restaurant across from the entrance.
Not so spectacular view and  It is like a magical castle. There are very room with weired shape. So many tourists and you need to wait to walk to another rooms.
The architecture was spectacular and good for photo taking. There isn't anything much to do here other than taking photos.

Went to the Crazy House today. Definitely an interesting place to visit if you have time to spare, but it is still a work in progress with construction taking place. You get a good view of Dalat from the top and the accommodation rooms are definitely worth looking at.
An unusual place, worth stopping by and seeing for a moment. We visited it as part of a tour of Dalat in September 2015.

It'll be interesting to see once. We visited after it rained, so walking through narrow, wet, and slippery walkways at a height of four stories (and the walkway walls are knee-high at best) isn't safe.

Da Lat's "Crazy house" is a fun yet strange place, where you can wander around and get lost. To describe it: think of an architect that went to the Park Güell in Barcelona (for inspiration), took a little bit of acid, and decided to build a big house!I think it's a little overpriced (40.000đ) for what you get though...
Of course, if you come to Dalat, you have to visit this place. But personally, I didn't really like it and never got the point. The only plus is the gorgeous view of the city.

The place certainly has unusual architecture and is colorful, but I found it a bit gloomy and cramped. The hotel owner's car also impressed me.
