Our domestic auto industry is quite unexpected in the vastness of Asia. Be sure to try the fried banana pies sold by the granny across the street; they're delicious.
This is one of the very well-known structures and abroad with unique architecture does not follow a fixed path

The Crazy House is really unique and in case you are in Da Lat combine it with your visit. If you have booked a tour it is anyway already included. We were there for 30-45 minutes and it is quite interesting to walk through that building.
Nice view... For some reason I find it hard to recommend it. Maybe just come with no expectations to see something great

It resembles a huge tree with intertwined branches and roots, overgrown with cobwebs, mushrooms, and the animals that inhabit it. Dang Viet Nga (the owner) envisioned a house that would not only stand out from the rest of Dalat's architecture but also express a desire for independence and freedom, as well as remind people to protect nature.

It feels like a Gaudi-inspired homage, but Barcelona is more interesting. Even the unfinished building there is impressive, while here it looks like construction debris. The sheer number of tourists also plays a role, as they squeeze through narrow passages that would be better off with a one-way system, getting in each other's way. I recommend the museum staff draw arrows to direct the flow of tourists.
Crazy place in the crazy location.Try once for your trip 40,000 D.Not too bad and get some food around there too.
The Crazy House is a hotel built in a very distinctive style. Guidebooks say the architect was inspired by Gaudi's works.

A crazy assortment of adjoining rooms in all shapes and sizes. It looks like a set from the Adams Family series but in a loving and crazy way. A great way to spend a hour or so meandering through the many room with lots of crazy furniture and artifacts. It can get a bit crowded when the tourist buses arrive as the paths adjoining buildings are only meant for one so prepare for ques. The garden is whimsical and its nice to just sit and contemplate the workings of the mind of the creator.

Interesting to see but really odd that people are already staying there. When it's finished it will look incredible. We enjoyed it because it was so different to other attractions. If you have small kids maybe give it a miss as half is still a building site so walking around is a bit dodgey in places. 40,000 entry :)

Definitely seems to be one of the more popular attractions in the city. The cost was roughly around $4-5. It boasts a lot of stairs and some unique rooms as well as some highpoints to provide a good viewpoint of the city. It still appears to be getting updated with parts not finished yet.  Not one for health and safety a lot of the stairways have very low 'railings' but provided you are careful and don't try to cross at the same time as someone else you should be fine. I would suggest visiting this in a group so you can all discuss it as by myself I felt I just zoomed round. All in all its worth going to as it's easy to get to if you are staying within da lat; but don't feel like you have missed a big place on your trip if you have not been able to squeeze it in.
This place is overrun with tourists and with souvenir shops. The architect's story (in the entry) is very interesting and it gives a visitor to Da Lat "something to see."
It's cool to see, only 40,000 to enter and you're done in 30-45 minutes. Would be cool to stay there overnight

Just writing this review to warn people that are afraid of heights like me, one minute your just following the crowd and the next you find yourself five storeys up walking along a plank like walk way with the only sort of safety barrier at ankle height.. Great place to see but be aware when your following the crowd!
A must to be seen but not astonishing. Considering the experience, I'd say it's a bit too expensive if you are on low budget. If you cannot afford it don't worry and just skip it.

Ok price for us but if you're backpackers on a budget you may want to think twice.It was busy and there was no real direction to take so everyone had to wait for people to pass as it could only be done single file.It was pretty and very unusual but I think a little overrated.
It's very original, it feels like an Asian version of a Walt Disney tree house.
The Crazy House looked very interesting at first glance – the entrance fee is affordable, and you get a free short introduction in very good English at the beginning.
This hotel has a crazy and weird design. It is nice to visit there only if you have a spare time. Not necessary attraction in dalat

It's a nice walk to this attraction but it's nothing special. My friends and I went on our last day in Da Lat when we had nothing planned and it was pleasant. You can get quite high up walking around the house so your are treated to some good views of the city and mountains. I would advise visiting this attraction when it is sunny.

Visiting the Crasy House was a good experience but there were nothing outstanding about the place. It was fun to get lost in the maze of stairs and wird buildings. However, if you have seen the buildings of Gaudi it didn't seem thought through or in any way like there was a plan from the beginning. It seems random and chaotic. My advise is that if you have ekstra time on your hand then go to the Crasy House and if you are in lack of time then skip this activity.
Interesting place worth a visit to say you have been but still under construction. Would be good to see once fully complete.

Although Da Lat Crazy House has a unique design, part of it is under renovation and another part is a bit incomplete, so you don't need to spend too much time there. 30-45 minutes is enough time to take photos and walk around.

I could not think a better word to describe this place, it just goes on forever so weird it is hard to describe. It is still haveing parst added.There are several rooms available for nightly stays, but why would you. One of those places that leave you scratching you head
Truly Crazy! A very strange hotel with a bunch of insanely weird rooms. Scary animal statues with glowing eyes 😫 And there are always so many tourists here, you can't push through 😧
This is considered a must see when you're in Dalat, but in reality it doesn't measure up to the hype. At least it's cheap to get in.
It's not a spectacular attraction but it only costs 40,000 so it's a time filler. A bit of a building site at the moment as they are building more rooms.

The daughter of Ho Chi Minh's successor, an internationally trained architect, was given the opportunity to realize her "crazy" plans for a hotel/residential building in this attractive location thanks to her good connections with the Politburo.

We read a lot, wow wow, we arrived, there were a lot of people, maybe we were unlucky, there was construction going on, it was March 2016, I didn’t see anything special, we climbed through the labyrinths, everything was made of cement and no wood (there was no talk of staying there, like a hotel, that’s just talk, not many were even upset, probably expected more, you can take good photos

This was the only place in Da Lat that we came across mass tourism. Four tour buses were parked outside when we arrived. The buildings were heaving with people, many of whom were walking backwards since they were taking non stop selfies. You go round at the speed of the slowest person ahead of you since it is impossible to overtake people on the staircases or walkways. The walkways have low handrails and in some places are high up and quite scary. It is not suitable for young children or anybody who suffers from vertigo. Any comparisons with the masterpieces of Gaudi in Barcelona, Spain are fanciful, but clearly it appeals to a lot of people. It's certainly different!

It is the touristic place so it didn't impress me a lot. The ticket price is 40000(~2$) , no more charge. It was attractive by its structure, the architectures and the crazy thought of the owner. Like a maze and lead to many different view and also do business with renting room.

A very original visit to this rather crazy project, but unfortunately overrun with tourists – good luck making it through without being jostled or pushed around for a photo! As a result, the price seems downright expensive compared to what you get...especially since there's no information, directions, explanations, or anything else.

if you're around check it out, if not you're not missing out much. the house was packed with tourists, architecture is quite tacky and honestly i didn't really enjoy it...was happy to get out of there and go elsewhere. if you wanna see something really crazy check out duong len trang, the crazy  6 floor (or so) maze bar/cafe in dalat!

Funny build house, but yeah. That's it. 40.000 vnd to enter and pretty crowded with other tourists. Visit if you feel like, but definetly not a must. Go to the 100 roofs cafe in stead - it's a bar and even more crazy.
It was fun to experience, but you are not "blown away" by this place. It is excellent, and also for the price 20,000 Dong. We spent about 1 hour in there.

We we told the architecture was similar as Gaudi's, so we were a bit disappointed. If you've been to Barcelona, I don't think it's worth to go to the Crazy House. However we noticed that kids were having fun, so if you're traveling with your children, that's a good thing to do with them!

If you've seen Gaudi's magnificent architecture in Barcelona, you will feel that Crazy House architect was inspired by him and tried to copy his style. Miles apart thou... I felt more in a children playground than an adult fairy tale. Yet, if you are near by is ok to see to make your own opinion. I wouldn't stay there over night as the place is packed with tourists so you will not have peace.

The crazy house is still under construction, with many areas still construction sites and littered with trash. The completed rooms are quite unique, featuring bizarre, dangerous, and unpredictable aerial walkways, some even flooded and requiring climbing to cross. The souvenirs inside are not worth mentioning. It's still somewhat enjoyable, as long as you don't compare it to Gaudi's works.

You can certainly visit... just for show. It's a jumble of architectural structures and ideas, perhaps... but there's no real beauty or uniqueness to it. It's probably something someone stole from somewhere. Frankly, it's completely tasteless. They say you can rent a room here for the night. It's certainly exotic and extreme, but how comfortable is it? I don't know.

This is okay to visit if you have time.  You basically climb up and down narrow stairs taking photographs of the oddly shaped buildings.  It tends to get crowded with tour groups and there can be waits climbing up and down.  Hint this is not a place for those who are not fit to climb stairs or are afraid of heights. N

Thought I was going to see someone's unique home. Was a still-under-construction maze really. Very elaborate though. Was there 30-40 mins,lots of photos...only need about 10-15 mins to be honest. Ok for a look not an essential stop in Dalat

We were curious to see this place. We are no longer curious! If you have bad knees, don't like heights, have small children accompanying you or are short of time, maybe skip this! Crazy House is a work in progress. There are many steps. Stilettos are out! Yes, we saw a tourist wearing these. Our favourite part was the fruit juice at the café at the end where you sit on a concrete stool and stare at a concrete mushroom! Reach your own conclusion!

Crazy House is one of Da Lat's attractions. Personally, I think it's a beautiful architectural work. There's a 40,000 dong entrance fee. It's a very popular place, attracting many visitors. Unfortunately, it's not finished, and there's a lot of trash between floors. The tour is short, but the designer was very ingenious. I would recommend finishing it or at least cleaning it up.
Very impressed by the architecture which is crazy ! it is good to see if you have time, but not a most.
It was a rainy day - I wasn't impressed - perhaps you need to be a lover of bizarre architecture to appreciate this place.  It just looked tacky to me.
It'll only be of interest to small children. The structure itself is unusual and beautiful, but it's not worth a special trip.

This place is strange, and not always in the best taste. You can have a nice time there, but if you don't have much time, keep walking. Besides, it's not at all typical of Vietnam. It's more of a curiosity. 40,000 VND/person

There were certain aspects of this house that my partner and I enjoyed.  Some areas were attractive but many were over the top and a little bit tacky.  They need to stop building onto the property and invest in keeping what they have up together, i.e. repairing crumbling concrete.It is often compared to Gaudi but whilst I can see where the inspiration came from it most certainly is not.
It looks nice, but for a solo traveler, I found it a bit boring.
Easily accessible by scooter. Parking and entry are inexpensive. Unfortunately, it's a popular tourist attraction and gets crowded quickly due to the limited space. Overall, perfectly fine.

Everything is wonderful, sweet, and impressive, like being in Alice in Wonderland, if not for one thing (!!!): these wonders coexist with mountains of trash. How is that possible? Okay, so there's trash everywhere (it's bloody), but in a building where tourists go, give tours, and charge for it... friends, let's live clean.

This place is considered the most popular in Dalat. Its construction was, and still is, the work of its sole owner. She studied architecture in Moscow, then moved to Vietnam. Despite her advanced age, she still drives her Lada 7, the only one in Dalat, and is actively building her creation, which, thanks to the Russians, is called Crazy House.
I already visited the place back in January 2006.I decided to go again. Cannot say if lots of new things have been added!A mix between "Gaudi", "Art Deco" and some weird styles...

Lots of walking along winding and twisting stairs up and down and when there is a crowd as there was this Chinese new Years which is also Vietnam's New years it's complicated to go forward or pass others (going both directions) on the narrow stairs and difficult to even take photo's. It is curious and weird concept of concrete design.

Really zany place- one person's dream. Interesting concept but verging on a concrete monstrosity. Lots of stairs, uneven and not great support for those who need assistance on stairs. A concern for children in terms of safety. An interesting garden area with a shop serving drinks. Rooms are interesting, but not sure I would want to stay Lots of unfinished areas and places in need of repair. Concerns re building standards.there.  Even with these concerns, worth a look, quite out of the ordinary.
This attraction was very popular. Not my thing really but others from around the world seemed to be enjoying it.

We spent two nights here. As previous reviews have written, this is a fun hotel, not a place to live. It might be fun to wander around (though I wasn't impressed), but it's not a great place to live. The rooms are small, and tourists start banging on the door from 8-9am, despite the "do not disturb" sign and the curtains drawn. The hot water and the fireplace, which starts at 7pm, were a plus. There's
Paid parking. Admission 40 VND. Exhibition for about thirty minutes.Development of. Building is a hotel.

When we (me and my girlfriend) where in Da Lat the weather was poor and we decided to go here. It's something different and it killed a hour. The view from the top is nice but it's no big shake, probably better for a family or young kids

We were not expecting this house to be a genuine touristic attraction. We thought it was just a unusual guesthouse that we could admire when passing by. What a disappointment when we saw we had to pay an entrance fee and that several buses full of (Russian) tourists were arriving at the same time. Apart from that, it is nice and well made. Kids could have the impression to spend an hour in Wonderland.

It certainly is bizarre.  My young kids enjoyed exploring the place.  Some of the outdoor winding outdoor staircases get quite high and I had to keep them close to me to ensure their safety because the barriers have been designed to look interesting; not to maximise safety!  You certainly wouldn't want to let young kids run around the elevated staircases unsupervised!  We couldn't see in some of the rooms (and there's only about 8 of them) because they were occupied by guests.  Going in the middle of the day might maximise your chances of the rooms being unoccupied.  Two parts of the hotel have obviously been under construction for a VERY LONG TIME.  It doesn't look like the project will ever be completed, which is a shame.  It would be good to be able to see more rooms.

The place is very interesting, a very unique structure, but it's not a good place to stay. It's 14 degrees Celsius in Dalat in the evening, and since the buildings are made of concrete, the rooms are terribly cold. From morning until dark, the hotel is packed with tourists who have come to see this landmark, and everyone is trying to get into your room. So, rest is out of the question. And in the evening, when the hotel empties, it's hard to see much, and it's cold. On the plus side, I got up at 7:00 AM and explored everything in splendid isolation, without any crowds. For sightseeing, it's a must. I wouldn't recommend it for a stay.

Tour buses lined the street. Street vendors clogged the entrance.  The admission fee was cheap.  My advice to the operators is to create a one way route, so you don't have people going both ways on the very narrow passageways. Some of the stairways are very narrow and steep, if you have any mobility issues I would not go onto the 2nd level.

Honestly, I'm not a fan of these kinds of buildings, and this house didn't impress me at all. I respect the creative imagination and execution; the Vietnamese architect designed and built it, well done. The large number of tourists and the narrow paths... I wasn't impressed.

Well, if you've already found yourself in Dalat for some reason, you might want to visit this place. If you didn't get enough of the playground as a child, climbing on hanging structures and various ladders, this is a chance to relive those golden years.

Good to take some weird angle and pano view photo, also a place to lay your foot. Ticket is 40,000. If only choose one from crazy house or classy train ride to the near town (abt 20 mins ride and 45 mins stop at nearby town, another 20 mins back) from Dalat train station, def do the latter one.

Crazy house is as you'd expect. Crazy!Depending on your age, and your level of cynicism, the length and enjoyment of that varies.It's certainly something to be seen and if you have younger humans with you I can see this going down as a big hit. (Despite the slightly dangerous high walkways, and building works, so make sure to keep the kids close)Walking in and around you really do get a sense of wonder....mine was mainly how the 'house' could have any real purpose past being a visual, section lifted out of a Disneyland theme park, attraction.Apparently it's also a hotel, which does seem crazy as I don't get how it would ever be comfortable, functional or private.With tight weaving paths and a myriad of rooms, there is enough to keep you walking around for a while. Head to the top and you get great views of Dalat.Problem is, after walking on a couple of winding staircases and seeing a few "rooms" barely big enough to swing a gerbil, there isn't really much to consider other than how would anyone less nimble,  taller than 5ft 8" and anyone as old as your Nan get around this place?!Stannah would certainly have their work cut out making a chairlift!!!Worth a visit but I got bored pretty quickly.

Fun and trippy house/hotel that is still in the process of being built. You can spend an hour or so here, it's not very big. Since most of the paths and stairs are outdoors, avoid going here in case of rain. Be prepared to climb a lot of stairs!

This house is said to be the brainchild of a Vietnamese female architect, featuring an irregular and unusual design. It also has another name, "Ngê Nga Villa," after the architect. It seems you can register for accommodation here.

Despite the name, this place isn't actually that crazy.  Might be good for a quick afternoon visit but not much more.  I would have been perfectly fine skipping this place.  Maybe drop by if you are in the area.
Nice to do, partly because there isn't that much to see in Dalat.
The whole house is like a maze. Interesting but my friends and I didn't really enjoy it. Good view of the whole Da Lat city. Not a must go in Da Lat.

The first thing that has to be mentioned is that safety was not a concern when this place was built. When we went, there was an few rooms under construction that was open to the public. The rest of the place is built without considering whether it was safe for people. I would recommend keeping an eye on any children and your steps. It is an interesting place but rather pointless.
The unique and unusual architecture of the Vietnamese-French owner is a must-see for those who enjoy the spooky. Aside from the scenery, there's nothing else to see.

The fantastical work of a Vietnamese female architect who studied in Russia, with its intricate and complex design, makes visitors feel like they are on an adventure! Originally part of a hotel, it is open to the public even when there are no reservations. The building materials, style, and design are full of surrealism. Maybe Blue Coffee across the street is also worth a visit!

Interesting architecture, but a bit over the top... the description "Gaudi meets Disneyland" is spot on. Worth a look, but not a must-see. About a 20-minute walk from the city center. Entrance fee: 40,000 VND.

Almost every tourist visiting Da Lat will visit this place. I think it's perfect for children; it's quite fun. Unfortunately, Da Lat often rains, and it was cloudy when we visited, so it was difficult to see its true beauty. The winding staircases connect each different floor. The colorful buildings in the distance are definitely worth seeing. The entrance fee is 40,000 VND, equivalent to about 10 RMB, and it closes at 7 pm.
Nice place ... If you have time go to visit if not ... it's OK .

My partner and I started off here on our day in Dalat. Very surreal kind of place; the architecture was indeed interesting. But other than walk around and marvel at the weirdness of the place, and get some decent city views, it was a bit over hyped as an attraction. Especially considering the cost - my partner and I were out in 30mins and I think it cost 120000. You could easily find cheaper, more interesting attractions elsewhere

The architect went a bit crazy; he wants to transport us to a fairytale world. Did he succeed? Hmmm...it didn't move me—a little spooky, but more kitschy (too much of everything, which doesn't help). If anyone's interested, they can rent one of the furnished rooms; each one has a different story—it's possible.
We came here after the Dalat Countryside tour and it was nice to take a quick look around. If you happen to be in the area, it's quite fun.

Lots of Russian tourists, taking "selfies", going about as a large group.Some of the handrails are rather poor and in bad weather would be very dangerous.If nothing else to do it might be worth seeing.

The crazy horse is a bit overpriced because when you enter it is cool and crazy and everything but still half under construction and it gets kinda old after 20-30 minutes. Much better off going to the 100 roof bar
It's nice for taking photos and looking around, but it's hard to expect anything more than that. 😂
It's not usual, but we were expecting something that would blow our minds))) Everything was very confusing, we didn't get into half the rooms, we didn't even want to take photos

Went on a rainy day and it was bad.  Stairs and floors were slippery so it was dangerous especially with children.  Very little walking space between steps and not well-lit.  Will only encourage to go if not raining.
Nice house to walk/climb through for an hour. Beautifully made and it does bear some resemblance to Gaudi. It was a bit crowded due to the many tourists.
Well, it was unfinished. I wouldnt go again. Beautiful but not worth the entry fee. And a lot of tour buses go here.

One of the weirdest places I've been to in Asia! You get a nice view over the city and it's funny to have a look in the house. Many rooms aren't ready though and it looks like a construction area, not tidy at all, that's a shame.
It's a truly unique house and well worth a visit. You can easily lose yourself in its endless pathways, and it's a pleasant place to spend an hour or two.

Cool attraction if you have time to spare. Unlike any architecture I have seen before and a cool back story. Quite a bit under construction still. Avoid going when tourist buses there as narrow walk ways can make it difficult to get around.
Very novel architecture which comes straight out of Disneyland and is really quite unique but not really worth going out of your way to see

Crazy house is located in Hang Nga hotel and this one is one of tourist attractions in Da Lat. We went here around 5pm when it was turning to be dark. The ticket is 40,000VND. Now the house is still under construction. We just visited some parts of the house and took pictures. This house was not really special, but the architecture was quite attractive. We visited for 30mins and went back hotel.You should try if you are curious about the reason why they call Crazy house.
We happened to pass by here on a tour. We wouldn't have gone there on our own. There is a bar in the center (100 Floors) that is built in the same way. Actually, that one is even nicer!

If you're in da lat and have a free hour I would recommend to visit the crazy house. The place is unique and nice to see. But I didn't found it as a good architecture, it was more like sculptures garden for me.

Upon arrival you buy tickets from the counter left of the entrance (40 000vnd).When we entered it was a nice surprise to see all the different buildings. However, it was rammed with Russian tour buses which made the experience less enjoyable.The Crazy House isn't what we expected. It is made out of mainly brick and cement and a lot of it is being refurbished making the place seem very unfinished and disappointing.Exploring the House was fun and different with very few places off limits. You get some good views of Da Lat. But would be nice to have some information about the House/Project.
So, ok, the initial concept was clever, but now this place is showing some serious signs of age and poor repairs here and there. Would I go there again? NO
It is a pretty average attraction but not a must see in my opinion.  100 Roofs bar in Dalat is much more interesting and crazy!
I visited this crazy house in the rain; it's an interesting house to see, but not essential.

We arrived in the rain, got our raincoats, and off we went! This slightly shortened our stay at this site, but we still had time to understand and partially examine this marvelous creation. I think that even if this complex didn't have such a famous owner, Mrs. Dang Hang Nga, it still wouldn't have gone unnoticed. It's impossible to describe "Crazy House"; it has to be seen. In my opinion, it's not very acceptable as a hotel, but I highly recommend visiting it to appreciate Dang Hang Nga's genius and flight of fancy. Interestingly, it's still under construction, and this is most likely due to Vietnamese tax laws rather than the constant ideas that come to mind of this far from young woman.
We spent a night in the Termite Room.
If your trying to fill in time maybe worth a look credit to the facility it is different with some interesting reading re history
It was bigger then either of us thought. Super quirky and good way to spend 30 min wandering around.

This is one very unique place and it's a shame there are so many other tourists. Having to navigate through them in such tight spaces detracts a little from the experience. Still commendations to the builders.

The overall appearance is reminiscent of Gaudi's buildings, specifically the "Quarter of Discord." Anyone who has been to Barcelona or is familiar with his work will understand. This guesthouse is also a great place to stay.

it is nice to see the crazy buildings but as a lot of parts are under construction it is at the moment not worth it.the staff is helpfull and friendly to provide you with informations and directions.it is dangerous as well to climb on the roof.as it is used as a hotel as well you do not get to see all of the crazy room which is not cool as you pay the full entrance fee.

The building is imaginative, with guest rooms inside and ongoing expansion. It's somewhat reminiscent of Gaudí's style. There's also a Catholic church nearby, and the Bao Dai Palace isn't far either. However, it's not particularly interesting in terms of its own unique charm; it's rather monotonous and best suited for a quick visit.

We got there relatively early in the morning, and it was pretty quiet. The building itself is a concrete maze, and although it attempts make you feel part of nature, climbing up and down trees it didn't really do it for me!By the time we left, lots of buses rolled up and was all a bit of a shambles!

The house may be somewhat unusual, but it's not a must-see and, even in the off-season, completely overrun with tourists. The entrance fee is also rather high for what's on offer (40,000 dong per person), and we were through it in 15 minutes; we'd simply had enough of all the tourists. Most of it is outdoors, so it's not really recommended in bad weather. We could have easily skipped it.

The crazy house is worth a visit but don't go to late when it is dark. It has very unique architecture and decoration,We just walked around but the hotel rooms did look cool, but a stretch for our budget. The building is currently undergoing some restoration and construction so a few parts are quite untidy. Still worth a visit though for 2 bucks.

But it was nothing more, nothing less ㅠㅠ There were a lot of interesting buildings so it was good for taking photos, but if you are pressed for time, I don't think it's worth making time to go! Also, the walk there was pitch black and scary at night.

Crazy house is one of first places which i was introduced by hotel's staff to visit. its not really as impressive as i expected, the architecture is just ok to see. Plus point for this destination is staff, she is very friendly, she introduced overall history of house as well as advised me the best way to visit
The architecture is original, there are many different passages, there are themed rooms you can look at, you can visit

Skeptical when I went in, I was just as skeptical when I left, wondering what the point of such a place was. It's kitschy, and to top it off, the music is truly awful—it's a perfect combination. There are indeed lots of noisy Russians.

Maybe I just prefer natural scenery and have no interest in man-made landscapes. Parking costs 5,000 won and the entrance fee is 40,000 won. It only takes about 20 minutes to walk through the whole area. The stairs are very narrow, so be careful. There's a good spot for taking photos at the top.
This is a remarkable house built among the trees. It bears a strong resemblance to the architectural style of Gaudí. If you are near Da Lat, you can visit it.
We walked through it during our tour. In my opinion, it's not worth making a special trip there. If you like it, that is...

We stayed here during our 3 days trip in Da Lat and overall we find this as a charming, and unique place inspired by Gaudi's work.We stayed in the Gourd Room with a round bed, not very comfortable to sleep as it's round and hard but the decoration makes it nice like a little nest.During the day the visitors always try to open our room, maybe it's best for the staff to mark which room is visitable and which not.We rented a motorbike and a pair of helmet.Not the best bike but ok, we can explore Da Lat with no problem. Although the motorbike is not so powerful and the helmet is funny.

One of the few attractions in parts of Vietnam which dont use animals to attract business. So a big thumbs up there !Interesting place to visit, odd but I guess thats what youd expect from the name. Very cheap to visit and worth a look about.
Just as a trip changes depending on who you go with, this is a place that could be really boring. Construction is still ongoing, and it looks a bit shabby and dangerous.

This is actually a hotel. Kids might be interested in playing here, but if it were up to me, I probably wouldn't choose to stay here. I might have a fear of heights, so I don't think it's suitable. Once is enough.
Some part of it is still under construction. It also offer accommodation if you wish to try something new, but of course with a higher price. Price: 40,000 dong per pax.
Just a not-very-crazy house with a lot of tourists, it didn't catch any of my interest and I was so disappointed that I just passed by for a very quick look.

We were expecting a bit more buzz, walking around the place is good and the architecture is interesting but you get bored quite quickly.We stayed the night in the Gourd room, there are no bars/restaurants nearby or in the building which we were a little dissapointed with.
I was really excited to go here after reading all the great reviews everywhere. The design is pretty impressive but I felt like I was at a kid playground. I didn't linger and won't go back.

The architect initially had an strange idea but after it came into reality, the building did not reflect her spirit. It looked like a fairy tale cottage for children more than an unique structure. The stairs were not safe in some places.

Very confused as to what this attraction is supposed to be... Design is similar style to Gaudi, however this was the only interesting part. Cool to look at, but not really worth the 80k/pp entrance fee 😅

I think it is very unique and well built as a building, but the stairs were too difficult for us because we were tired of walking in the city of Dalat, which already has many ups and downs. Maybe I just had to look at it from the outside.

I was not impressed, it was a clumsy conglomeration of diverse structures and sculptures, the feeling of a kindergarten.There is no general style, no system, no ensemble, no meaning.I more or less liked only the room with a marine theme.
We went there at the end of the afternoon, hordes of tourists, the stairs of this horrible construction are very narrow, it is difficult to pass each other. We did not complete the visit.

This bar structure is amazing, but one of the barmen is incredibly rude. Doesn't smile at all and before the closing time was already refusing to put some songs, answering in a very bad tone. Total buzzkill. The drinks are expensive and is only worth it for the maze, not for drinks or dancing 👎

It is very dated and like a desserted theme park where all the attractions are closed and themed characters have left for the day. It also feels like an accident waiting to happen. With cables and wires petruding from the walls, free and easy access to the unprotected building site, very high narrow stair cases and little to protect people should they fall or hate heights, it would never pass any health and safety assessment. You can watch people shuffle along the high narrow stairs cases searching for the nearest grab rail and then yelling at eachother as they try and pass by on the open, high staircases with huge drops. The look of sheer terror on people's faces as they worry about falling is a little unerving!The intriguing thing is the overall design and creativity. I would say only go if you want to try and get in the mind of the architect. For sure it will make you wonder why and how would anyone design this? A combination of Disney, Lord of the Rings and Dungeon and Dragons in a dusty space squeezed between houses in Dalat! . If this sounds good to you, then take a visit. Please note though in my opinion it is not safe for small children or anyone with any height or walking difficulties!Be prepared, be very prepared!

This is an interesting place to visit while you are in Da Lat, but not a good hotel in which to stay. The multitude of persons touring during the day are very disruptive and loud. The place has no elevator and had no attendent to help with luggage when we left early in the morning. It is an amazing place to see, but the hotel does not merit a very good rating for accomodations.
I can't really tell you that you need to go there, since I wasn't particularly impressed. It was just going up and down and over quickly, so I wondered why I even bothered coming.
brilliant business idea

We visited crazy house at the end of our countryside visit. Seemed like all tour companies go there at the end of the day. The place was overcrowded. Some stairs seemed pretty dangerous to me. The crowd and windy narrow pathways was a turn off to me. Perhaps it would have been better with less people.

Too crowded, too ugly, and risky on top of that due to expansion work resulting in loose electrical cables lying here and there, concrete reinforcement sticking out viciously, and tools lying everywhere. On top of that, the most Instagram-worthy spots (insofar as you would want to post anything of this bizarre sight there) are heavily and annoyingly annexed by tourists traveling in groups of photographers. We were back outside within 20 minutes, blushing with shame because we had briefly linked this to Gaudi at the entrance. It cannot even stand in the shadow of the great Spanish architect.

This is the very definition of a tourist trap. While I can appreciate the level of imagination that went into designing this, I am flabergasted at the fact that it is considered a "must see" in Dalat. It s kind of tacky and very much the same. Once you've seen one room, or climbed one set of stairs, you've pretty much done it all. Overall, this was a waste of 50,000 VND and an hour of my time.
It's just a big house with a narrow street!
I went because it was a tourist attraction near my hotel, but
Interesting concept but overall we were fairly bored. If you have nothing to do then check it out but we spent about ten minutes here after paying entry fee and for motorbike parking

When I bought banh mi from a street stall in front of Crazy House on June 1st, I almost got shortchanged. The banh mi was also expensive at 20,000 dong and tasted bad. Many tourists who come to Crazy House are foreigners and are not used to Vietnamese currency, so be careful as they may try to shortchange you.
There is nothing to do there, suitable for children or adults who love architecture.
Boring tourist trap. It’s a maze and you get lost in endless stairwells. Half of it was under construction. Not worth your time.

The building strongly resembles an amusement park. Very confusing and, all in all, unspectacular. A shame. Some of the bridges have very low railings, and you have to squeeze past other people. I can't understand why it's considered one of the top attractions.

The house itself consists essentially of the guesthouse rooms and numerous passages between the various rooms. It's probably beautiful, but having visited Europe, where various exotic houses are on display, the place itself was unimpressive. The most frustrating thing is that this house was recommended by friends as something fantastical)) and I'm far from alone; half the tour group on the bus thought the same. It's worth noting that the owner was the daughter of one of the Vietnamese leader's friends, so she had access everywhere, meaning this building, whatever its appearance, was bound to be praised. 🤷🏼‍♂️

lots and lots of bus tours. I guess it's sort of neat, but there are plenty of other things to see around town. You can stay here as it is also a hotel but I don't know why you'd want to since there are tourists tromping around all over the place.

It's only suitable for sightseeing, nothing particularly interesting. The scenery is beautiful for photos. The rooms have an artistic, hidden design, so even if you look at them for a long time, you won't understand them. It's better to just go and see for yourself.
Kind of crazy to go here. It's not completed, kind of a "zoo" and crowded. Also, dangerous because the safety rails are too low for adults. I would not recommend spending the money to go here.

Never really intended to visit, but I was in a tour which stopped here, so I paid my 50k and went inside. Honestly, it's like the bits in between the fun at a theme park, all fibreglass, and in places, not finished. There are one or two slightly thrilling parts when you can walk over a very high, narrow walkway, and people seem to like it, but it seems to be lost on me.
it's interesting but maybe I missed the history part.good for kids, tall people watch your head. cute snack bar at the ground floor. People who are into arts may appreciate it more
This is a building with a unique architectural style. Space is limited, the entrance fee is expensive, and visiting it would be a waste of money.
So so.As per above, interesting, but not enthralling. Not really worthwhile. Must be better things to see or do. Just a nice tasty Viet luch with a view would be great.
It was what we expected. Ok, but not much going on. You pay 50.000 VD to go inside. It's a funny building, nice for children.

Me and my boyfriend stayed in the kangaroo room for one night and it was a nice room kind of small but still really cool. Unfortunately we got woken up at 6:30-7 in the morning by construction going on above and next to our room. Apparently this is an ongoing thing as the crazy house is constantly adding new features, but we couldnt stay in the room because it was so loud and the banging vibrates the whole room. Reception staff didn’t care when we complained. Also people were coming past the room at all hours and kept trying to open our door and looking through the windows and it was very noisy. All in all cool place but wouldn’t recommend paying to stay the night there.

A group of 4 of us went here, it’s good that it’s very cheap and I’m glad we didn’t pay anymore. We were only there about 10 minutes because it’s only half done it seems. You go up some stairs and actually it’s still a building site and there’s no signs to say ‘no entry’ or anything which gives off the impression it’s unorganised. I think it would be a great place to take children but once it’s complete and there isn’t any half done stairs or cement mixers around the place.
You either hate it or love it... still a lot of construction works going on, area for about 70% completed. Not really my favorite type of activity.
I don't think it's worth paying 50,000 VND to go here... It's a waste of money. It just feels like a weird theme park. Let's save the 50,000 VND and go to Bun Cha Hanoi instead.
We didn't find anything special about this place. A lot of confusing and convoluted staircase, and most of the time it was crowded. Also, the whole area is dirty.

Most of the area was under renovation during my visit. However the site was still opening without notifying you the situation. Renovation work was carrying just next you and therefore the environment was quite awful.

Nothing much inside there and we paid VND 10K for it. The entrance fees is OK for only VND 10K per person but actually nothing much inside there except some weird design of architecture building. I can see there are still many work in progress. Maybe it is just me but I don't event take a single photo. Just walk in and out in 15-10 mins time and we left.
its cool for about 5 mins but nothing to write home about. Take a look if you have time to burn but much better activities in Dalat.
It is crazy design but you climb pretty damn high and the barrier was low and unsafe.The place is cute but nothing I never seen before.In a good day worth half an hour visit, tops.

I thought it's a tree house or something about nature. But it's all concrete made. Not really interesting for me. When I was there, there are few kids were following. That look reallyn dangerous for kids. That's very high.

My family had heard a lot about it, so it was decided to pay the house a  visit. Honestly it was nothing for me, however, the children found it very interesting. We went there  a Saturday  afternoon, and there were “millions” of people also too many. IMHO then the place is very dangerous especially for boys 6 years and up, as they are climbing all over and the fence are only knee high!! As majority of Vietnamese tourist attractions, then the tickets are far too high, but no alternative as the Chinese pay.
Crazy house made of cardboard concrete. What a tasteless structure. By all means go there if you are bored, if you like amusement parks without attractions.
We went as soon as it opened, and quite a few people followed.
50k dong entry fee, nothing big. It is interesting to see, but not worth the money. It is half built and very kitsch.

Went past the Crazy house in a countryside tour, not very special. Even though the tickets are just 50.000 dong I wouldn't have entered in hindsight. Instead, go to the Maze bar with free entry and a way better ambiance!
Unique but not so spectacular. Sky bridges feel unsafe as the fence is too low on some sections. Definitely not worth a stay as it is flooded by tourists at any given moment.

It is nice in itself to walk through the Crazy House. It is a unique architectural style. You can also view the various hotel rooms from the inside and potentially stay there for $70/80 per night. These are quite expensive by Vietnamese standards, though understandable for a unique location.

Our impression of the place was that it was like walking around a temporary set. There are some different winding staircases set up, but aesthetically the place is not very nice or inviting. Under renovation, which further complicates an aesthetic experience. Comparisons with Gaudí are not appropriate, as this had more the character of an amusement park than an architectural work.

The Crazy House was okay. It looks like a replica of some rollercoaster in a crappy amusement park. It’s nothing more than a gimmick. Half of it were under construction while we were there. Go early before all the Russian and Chinese tour buses come - otherwise you won’t be able to move in there.
This place is alright. Walked around for half hour or so but thought it was  kind of boring. An ok activity if you have nothing else to do
It was a lot of fun - but it all seemed half-finished. The idea is great, but the experience is not something we would recommend to others, unfortunately.
I am puzzled by this place , not sure what it is. There are a lot of stairs and lots of colors , it has no shape. Cannot call it an architectural construction. Is a nightmare.
If you're planning to go trekking, go here.

A typical Vietnamese artificial attraction... You walk through what is claimed to be a spectacular house, but actually it is a mini Phantasialand, but without the attractions. As far as I'm concerned, those are the other visitors pushing, squeezing, and letting out little cries of 'ooh! aah!' while taking photos.
It's meant to be daring, reminiscent of the "children's kingdom" structures you see in amusement parks... but without the games, of course.

50k for this place are not really worth it.Half of the place is still under construction or not accessible as is private room for guests.I went to maze bar yesterday and I found it way more interesting!
There’s definitely far better things to see in dalat. I would recommend prioritising other things to see.

Wouldn’t really recommend, too many people walking over the bridges, constantly stopping and waiting for people to have pictures and the railings are not high enough! Quite dangerous if people are trying to pass you.The pictures/drawings in the front reception room are more impressive than the building itself

A visit that's not worth the effort. This house has absolutely no redeeming qualities. The most ridiculous thing is that it's not even worth living in. It's anything but functional. You risk bumping your head at any moment, and you're constantly tripping over steps. There are drafts everywhere, even though it's not that hot or dry. Good taste is sorely lacking. Don't waste your precious time. There are infinitely more interesting things to do in Dalat.

I was in Crazy House in 2018 January. There were a lot of construction work going on that time. Besides construction it is a meaningless activity to be in. It is not that interesting either. I see no point to be there except maybe to be a playground for children. Even for that purpose it can be boring. Paintings and installations very below average to be an artistic or 3D game environment.No need to waste your time in that bizarre and below average location. Dalat is a beautiful place. There are much to see outside in the nature. Or just stay in a cafe and watch around. That is much much better.

Went to the Dalat crazy house because it sounded like a very cool dr Suess meets a hobbits house, it was kind of like that, but a lot of areas were unfinished.  It appears as if the owner is attempting to make a hotel out of the place and to fund the construction, they’re allowing people to walk through the open spaces.  There was a cool under the sea looking room that looks like it may be come a bar someday.  It would be a cooler place if it was complete or had some information on the artist
Cost 50,000. Lots going on, but I find it ugly and tacky. Maybe more appealing to children.
Some quite good views from the top but it Crazy House just felt a bit like a busy theme park but without the rides. Lots of it was also under construction when we visited
Not really worth the 50k dong. Pretty average house and not particularly interesting visit. Felt like a part of a cheap theme park waiting for a ride.

We were not impressed by the "crazy house", it may be nice for children as  a kind of "fairy tale" house  but I don't see the value it gives to adults.The building is very rude looking, I don't see how it is a return to nature. It is built out of tons of concrete very impractical, in my opinion not impressive at all.

We visited there around 10 a.m. the entrance ticket was 50k for adults. We spent time there less than 30 mins. It was not WOW at all. We walked around and climb up all the maze stairs on those maze building. I felt it was not safe when walking onto the walk path on the roof which is too narrow. Only one by one can walk there and some parts of walk way is so high from the ground. And some parts are underconstruction without warning sign, some people keep walking inside that area which is not safe.

I went because my Chinese roommate wanted to, but I wondered if it was worth the money. It seemed like a place famous among Chinese people. It was a small building with parts under construction. It is not suitable for traveling with children.

Maybe I just didn't "get" it, but it that cool to visit. It had tiny passages that could only fit one person at a time, but had people walking in both directions so there were huge traffic jams just trying to walk through. They were also doing upkeep/expansions during the visit so I touched a wall that was wet with paint (luckily just with my hand, not my bag or clothes). Maybe it would be cool if it weren't crowded.

After having lunch at a restaurant across the street, I decided to go inside to digest my meal. It's a place where various strangely shaped buildings are connected like a maze, and it feels like you're just wandering around and exploring. It doesn't really feel like Da Lat, so I'd say it's a place to visit if you have the time.

Not sure what to expect from this when we got there, but with high praises from lonely planet we were kinda disappointed. Not a whole lot to do apart from walking around on the stairs which is like a small but cool maze. Other than that seems like waste of time to me. Maybe cooler for kids

Very busy, but remarkable to see how someone can completely lose themselves and their surroundings in the construction of such a unique building. If it weren't so crowded, you could still enjoy the beautiful view over the city of Dalat. Furthermore, as far as we are concerned, you will be back outside within an hour, and due to its unique character, you won't have missed anything of Dalat if you don't go. Fun for slightly older children.

A nice tour of the house of an artist who was influenced by the style of Gaudi in Barcelona. In fact, the entire structure is an attempt at imitation. In the past, there was only a tour of the complex, today you can sleep at an exorbitant price. And now they are in the accelerated stages of construction in the complex itself to add bedrooms... that's where the big money is. The entrance itself is cheap, 40,000. You can come independently and buy a ticket to tour the place for free. I wasn't enthusiastic.... Not beyond that, nice.

Despite its interesting history and architectural motivations--and its reputation--it felt more like a tourist attraction than anything else artistically pure: gift shops throughout it, with terrible background music. To add to our unpleasant experience, it poured with rain when we arrived which was not a good combination with high and narrow paths and inadequate hand rails. If you suffer from vertigo, avoid.
Absolutely a touristic place to go. It just gives you the idea of fake. And the music in Disney stile could be abolished.
Granted this is a quirky building but I think charging 40k to visit a hotel is a bit of a rip off. Nive to see but wouldnt put it high on the list of things to see in Da Lat
Quite expensive to get in and not really worth it. Half built and so busy when we went we couldn't even make our way around the tiny narrow paths. I wouldn't bother going.
It was really cool but Was expecting it to be way better. It was still under construction when we visited.

Interesting in a theme park sort of way, but honestly you expect to hear some Disney theme music playing around every corner. The "house" seems like it should be  designed to be on a children's play park, except for the fact that it is extremely dangerous for little kids. There are high walkways with low banisters and it would be easy to fall backward over one of these rails. The best thing about this weird place is that it has great views. There is an overpriced gift shop inside and tickets are 40,000 VND.
Funny house but not much to see.. I wouldn't make a detour to reach this spot. Much more beautiful areas in dalat!

As this rated quite highly my husband and I visited this house on our first day in Delat. We didn't really rate it as an attraction and spent about 10 mins at it. It'd be fun for kids to visit. Not a must see in  our opinion.
The hotel concept is very interesting. The rooms are individually designed, fairytale-like. Cozy and unique.
It's a mix of Disneyland and Gaudí's Sagrada Familia.

We wanted something special for the birthday of our daugther... Nobody was impressed with the night spent in that location! Tourists start knocking at your door from 08.30 am ... in order to get in! The management is privileging daily visitors as opposed to residents! The place is under construction and it doesn't meet safety standards... Our kids wanted to play 'Hide and seek' towards the end of the day ( after the departure of tourists) but we didn't allow them! Otherwise, it is an interesting and unusual place to visit in Dalat!
It is incredibly beautiful and super coolly designed, but the atmosphere is completely ruined by large groups of tourists being dropped off by tour buses right outside the door (Russian enclave).
I guess I just don't "get it." I didn't think going up a million staircases to peek into hotel rooms was that entertaining. I thought it was boring and a waste of money.

We heard about this from our lonely planet book. We decided to check it out one early evening- this place is actually a hotel which we weren't staying in. For 40,000VND you can go in and walk around the hotel but the bar was closed and most rooms locked due to guests staying (as expected). After a quick walk round we realised that most of the building was inaccessible - the venue is pretty cool and worth a look around but maybe better to stay at the hotel than just visit.

Half the guesthouse is a construction site, the other half is besieged by 20-strong Russian tour groups with microphones, making it impossible to get through the narrow corridors. I definitely wouldn't stay here. Moped parking costs 5000.

Tickets cost 40,000 VND, but it's basically a huge construction site. Almost a third of it is still under construction, and it's not even sealed off; the steel and concrete are exposed, and visitors are allowed to enter freely. The overall flow is poorly planned; you can barely brush past each other on the narrow, elevated walkways. The most ridiculous thing is that there's serious water accumulation on these walkways, forcing visitors to climb over them on all fours – it's truly laughable.
We found the Crazy House to be over crowded, over commercialized, and overrated.  It is interesting, but only recommended if one has lots of time in Dalat.

Honestly, it wasn't fascinating, and it wasn't fun. If you really have nothing to do in Da Lat, give it a try. You might be able to get about three decent photos. Also, the minimum 50-character review limit is way too much.

Crazy House is undoubtedly a creative exercise in imagination. But I'm not a fan of that kind of creativity! Twenty minutes into the tour, I started feeling dizzy, and my eyes tried to focus on the bridge of my nose.
Crazy yes... interesting.. Not really.   Winding, exhausting and worthless.  Gift shop and coffee too expensive.   Cool place (restaurant )across the street though

Get there early. When it's a lot of people there(especially Russian) you will need to force you through. Small bridges and loud and demanding Russian will make it hard to see all the fun there. Overall it was a fun place. But Russian people make it hard to get anywhere because they do as they want.

What do you think? It's an upside-down house you can climb around, walk around inside, and that's it! There's absolutely nothing to see. I think it's only for kids; adults won't be interested, and it's generally unsafe for kids to climb without parents (there's no safety net). It's a rip-off for tourists.

Skip the house if you only have little time in Dalat. It was packed with people during our visit (which was on Saturday to be fair). The house itself didn't offer anything crazy or fun. It was outdated and dirty.
Nice for kids but you dont have to go there. It has a amusementpark atmosphere and there's no real cultural thing about it.

This place could be for those who are intersting in kind of funky architechture but not for me. It's small and still underconstrucstion even it's built from 1990s. The entrance fee is expensive in compare to the other tourist sites in Da Lat. Disapointed visit and be careful if you are going with kids as they moving up the tiny corridor.

Newly constructed attraction for tourists. It's a hostel as well. Need to pay for visit (around 30 dong). Has nice skyline view. Very good restaurant - Le Chalet just opposite the house (owner from Belgium offers a good break from local food)
There's not much inside,

I recommend visiting, but not staying overnight. I was tempted to stay overnight in one of the 10 coolest hotels in the world, but I don't recommend it. It was an expensive whim, and on top of that, hundreds of tourists, mostly from Russia, visit the Crazy Hotel during the day, paying entrance fees! 😉 The traffic on the hotel grounds is terrible, and every visitor tries to peek into the room, which is very annoying. Just saying that the room is occupied doesn't always work. It seems a bit tacky to me.

The architecture and building is rather impressive but the whole place itself just seems strange and somewhat pointless. I wouldn't deter anyone from visiting however, go there and judge for yourself if you're looking for something to do. The 40,000 ticket each is quite cheap so it isn't too bad if you don't like it, and if you do like it then it's a win. I would be careful having children there though, some areas seem a bit dangerous, so just keep and eye out. Also there was a part still under construction that was still open to everyone, that was very strange.I feel that if the place had more of a maze aspect to it with a goal to complete, like finding hidden objects then it could be great fun. Sadly it was not for me though.
Narrow, small, and nothing so special. Doesn't worth to pay a visit. Poor maintained facilities as well.

We visited here and were a little confused by the whole attraction. There were masses of tourists on tour buses and it was really crowded. I would hate to stay here as accommodation despite some of the room looking cute. Imagine trying to relax as hordes of tourists walk around the hotel being super noisy! No thanks!

We were told the purpose of this house was to connect people with the nature but to be honest I think the only purpose was to become one of the "10 strangest places in the world" and earn money from tourist. I'm giving 2 points just because the view from the rooftop is nice.
Pretty disappointing. This place is only about half done, looks like they dont have the money to finish. Looks not taken care of. Waste of money and time. Pathetic tourist attraction.
We walked through it briefly but quickly realized it isn't much to look at. It might be fun with children. The entrance fee is too expensive for what you get in return.

It is what it says a really crazy designed house. They are refurbishing a lot of it and adding a restaurant. It will not be full open until 2019I would drive by and take a photo until they are fully open
Over rated activity. The place is nice but it gets boring after a few minutes. Came there only because of trip advisor. The view from the top is really nice though.
Despite being designed by a famous architect, it feels like a tourist playground.
The house is poorly constructed with decorations stuck to the walls; it's rather badly done. Not worth the detour except perhaps for the view of Dalat.

It's not necessarily something you have to see; I got an Efteling feeling! The architecture is unique, but I didn't find it very special! The only thing worth it is that you have a nice view and can take beautiful photos of the city! But otherwise, I was done with it pretty quickly!

It's a bizarre place I'll give them that, but for 40,000 I was expecting a bit more- there's nothing to do apart from wander around the plastic sculptures and weave in and out of all the tourists. It feels a bit like a theme park, but with none of the rides to get excited about, just the tacky plastic walkways. Best bit was walking above the house where there's a nice view over the city, but as there were people behind us we couldn't stay there very long at all- there's no platform, just part of the path. Much preferred going to the 100 floors bar- has a similar maze-y feel but way cooler.
Nothing interesting, in the middle of construction works since the house is still being built! Not authentic and kitch.

We had booked three nights in advance, but it was a huge disappointment. It isn't really a hotel. It is just a busy attraction. Between 8:15 and 19:00, loads of tourists arrive in tour buses. There is no decent reception. We had to wait first until the staff had explained the Crazy House to the tour bus full of Russians.
The architecture is pretty impressive but basicly it is just an interesting house and nothing more. We spent there 15 minutes before getting bored.
I think it is nice for families and small kids, otherwise it was nothing that special..like bette the Hundretwasser house.
A waste of time. Especially in the rain. Not suitable for children! It could never have been opened according to European or American safety standards.

I'm not that trilled - rather go for some trips in the fantastic nature surrounding Dalat. Young kids (3-10) might find it funny... Or you should book a stay - the hotels rooms seem nice and different.

When we went there a lot of the house was closed off due to renovation. I guess the house could be fun for children but it is pretty worn down. We went in, walked around for 5 minutes and then left. But quite cheap if you want a look. Think it was 40.000 VND.
I don't really know why this is a top attraction. It's somebody's weird house and gates you can walk around at a price. I'd prefer a walk up the mountains to see some real trees.

We didn't find it all that great after all. In the end, you walk around and see strange architectural styles, but somehow it still didn't interest us. There are significantly prettier and more interesting things in Da Lat.

Crazy crowded more like..busloads of tourists are walking over each other. The house looks over worn and lots of details are damaged. Builders are working on new parts of this hotel/tourist attraction during opening hours. The added building work has spoiled the original building. If you have nothing to do in Dalat go see the Crazy house (40k pp) but don't expect a cultural experience.

I think I would win the award here for shortest visitor ever I was in and out in less than 5 minutesFound the whole thing unimpressive and boringMost of it was a building site at the back Only do it if u have time to kill in dalat, much better things to see!

Just a weird place. You kind of have to go if you are in Dalat but is a 20mins job. Was scared for my life in some places - wired sticking out of walls and walk ways with no barriers 3 stories up.  It was built to be 'closer to nature' but as far removed as possible, birds are kept in very small cages in one section, the rest is just concrete. Not for young kids!!

After reading the reviews I went there with high expectations and was very disappointed to find a pretty normal structure, sure it would be a bit crazy to have that be your house, but the way it is set up it feels like you are walking through one of the sets in a Disney theme park but without the cool attractions. In hindsight I would definitely skip it.
It's just a unique house. Just stopped by to take a look..

This is my 8 year old daughter's review. I really don't have much to say about Crazy House. - 'crazy house is so dangerous and weird'- 'inside is very cool' - 'it's a little bit fun'- 'I don't want to come back again'I'm just glad it's free for kids, but 40k Dong for adults is crazy. It is a first and last time experience.

The architecture is weird, not fun nor creative.... And I was chocked when I knew it is a hotel! Walking around is very difficult and not safe (very high passages with barely any handrail as the sides are so low...). Definitely not the place to go to if you have kids. However, the view from up there is nice... But you can have a better one from the cable cars.
Very simple.... just visit this place if you have no idea where to go at Dalat. Still have another choice to visit many interesting place : Flower Park, Waterfalls, Valley of Love
We just visited this. It is nicely done in itself, but there is absolutely no story or logic to it. We walked from ruin to shop to ruin. We would not recommend it.
$2 for the visit... Why not... An architectural curiosity can be cool!

The build up to this place is a little over the top. The creation of a local architect the theme is somewhat Disneyish but on a much reduced scale. Beware that some of the structure is unfinished and you can find yourself in a semi building site, its been this way for some time so don't expect completion any time soon. Beware that some walkways are quite high with very little in the way of safety barriers so not good for people with hight issues.I

You pay 40,000 dong per person for entry here. Which is totally not worth it. We didn't quite understand the purpose of this building and were back outside within ten minutes. It might be fun for children; it's just like a fairytale village.
30,000 Dong to end up in a concrete jungle with no harmony... Admittedly, this house is crazy:

The Crazy House in Dalat is definitely one of the city's attractions! All guided tours include a visit! However, if you're traveling on your own and don't have a particular interest in haunted houses, bizarre structures, or architectural expertise, you can probably save yourself the visit and the entrance fee!

The place is uncomfortable. The construction is crude, with a strong smell of cement and a musty odor. The narrow hallways, some sections of which are outdoors in the hot sun, can be dizzying. The rooms are small, open, dusty, and smell of cement, along with unclean furnishings. It's an unpleasant place to stay.

This place isn't particularly worth the trek up the hill. It's a bit of a novelty and worth spending 5 minutes wandering around but it's a bit tacky. I imagine it'd be great fun for kids but that's the main appeal I think.

Satisfy your curiosity by making a visit. It's better than a Disneyland set because it actually has some history to it. The project itself is a testimony to extemporisation  rather than architecture. For 20,000 dong what do you have to lose.

As a backpacker in Da Lat, I visited this recommended attraction. There's an entrance fee (around $2), and the guide at the entrance presents the site in rather broken English. As for the site itself... It's certainly a "crazy" house in terms of its architecture, but it feels more like an attraction than a real architect-designed home, especially since other accessible buildings are still under construction and could be dangerous!

We went here, because of people in our hostel telling that it was nice here. But not for me. They are still constructing the site. So obviously it would be nicer if it was finished. Lady told me 2020. But I would not come back and see the finished crazy house.

For starters, it's not even finished yet but you still have to pay an entrance fee. Some of the stairs are very narrow and steep with only ankle high railings so it would be extremely dangerous if you were to fall. It is also full of tourists. Over all I just found it kind of ugly and strange.
Went to take a look..
It was fun to walk around and the houses were uniquely designed, but there was nothing particularly interesting or exciting about it. A waste of the entrance fee.
If you have better things to do you can give up.                   A very strange place and it's not worth the time the effort and the money to go there.

A must-see for any trip to Dalat. A strange place that leaves me with mixed feelings. I would never want to live here, not for any price. Crowds of tourists, from morning until night, strange architecture. The concept, too. It's no wonder one doctor, seeing this building, decided the architect had schizophrenia.
I visited after seeing it featured on a Korean TV program.

The hotel didn't wow me. The exterior design is still interesting, but the room design is not. The small rooms are very uncomfortable, each one dedicated to a different animal: the tiger room has a tiger, the bear room has a bear—pretty much the same.

We were close by so we had time to go. It was fun to see but felt more like a play garden for kids. Yes, different buildings but did not see the added value of this particular architecture. They were also busy building another 'candy' house. There are some nice views of the city if you are in top of the stair of these houses.
Maybe its just not our cup of tea, but it was.nice for few minutes and it felt artificial a bit and little crowded. If you have a spear hour it's nice but for sure not a must see.
Visit to a maze house with narrow bridges and staircases with a sort of Efteling theme. Not very special, but fine to fill an hour.

Didn't enjoy this place at all, just continued to end up walking the same areas. Only did this because I had time to kill. Was 40000 dong.... So not bad but only do it if you don't have anything else to do. Wasn't my sort of thing. They seem to be working on other areas. Also could be dangerous for kiddies.
Go if you have tonnes of time in Dalat but otherwise don't bother. Interesting concept but just a man made concrete building
It was Actually just a hotel with crazy bringes and shapes of the houses. It is ok to see, but to pau nearly 2 dollar to get in is too much

Visited Crazy House today. It's clearly a different kind of architecture than the rest of the city, but after 15 minutes we left again.. There was just nothing really special about it. I can see the fun in spending the night there, though, if you can accept that your room functions as a tourist attraction during the day... I would probably rename it from "Crazy House" to "A completely indifferent house"...

A waste of your time and money. It is still under construction and incredibly busy. Walking along the high paths is nice, but that is really all there is to it. If you are in the area... well, that's up to you. I could have seen plenty on the internet too. I can imagine that staying there with children would be fun, but wait until it's finished.

Crazy House is a typical contrived attraction. A bit Efteling-like. Cleverly designed with narrow paths and stairs where you can't pass each other, which sometimes leads to funny situations. Watch out, children, as it is not yet finished yet accessible, so there are holes etc., meaning you could take a serious fall. As far as we are concerned, adults can skip this attraction; you don't come to Vietnam for that. For children, it can be a fun change of pace.
Really strange but a must see if in Dalat. It is a  hotel as well but would I stay here with tourists wandering around?

We went there because it was close and reviews were ok. However, would NOT recommend. It is a strange concrete building which is not finished and has materials lying around everywhere. It seemed to be dangerous at some points too. Architecture is somehow interesting but not worth the time and ticket price.

We visited crazy house on a rainy day - not advisable as most of the walkways etc are outside and get slippy. The place is fairly crazy but areas are half finished and we found ourselves walking around building sites at times. Felt sorry for the people staying in the hotel parts as visitors can just march up and have a look in. Overall underwhelmed and at 40,000d entry I probably wouldn't recommend it

Everyone who bought a tour to Dalat is taken here... but there's nothing interesting here... at all. I don't recommend it. It's just a slightly unusual hotel. There are tons of people, you can't walk up the narrow stairs, and the safety is zero. And the view isn't all that great if you climb up. The toilets are terrible. I don't understand the point of coming here at all.

As a tourist attraction the crazy house is great, definitely visit! But do yourself a favour and sleep somewhere else. There is no customer service, no one ever knew who we were and kept trying to make us pay to enter, they couldn't find our passports when we went to check out, didn't clean our room and the room itself was dark, damp and dingey! (We stayed in termite room) only reason not to give it a 1 star is it was great exploring crazy house at night when all the visitors had left.

Went with a friend and his family. His kids had a blast running around the buildings and their faux tree house architecture, but honestly within 5 minutes I felt I had seen all of it and was getting bored.

While the basic idea is astonishing and audacious, Crazy House has become a tourist attraction or mini-museum. The first surprise is the amount of construction work underway, leading one to wonder if its owner, Ms. Dang Viet Nga, has either lost her self-respect or gone mad herself in the face of the waves of tourists paying 40,000 dong to visit her creation.

The Crazy House is novel, sure. But, it's really just a bunch of buildings seemingly thoughtlessly strung together with walkways too narrow for the dozens of tourists meandering throughout. I would hate to actually sleep in one of the rooms with so many tourists around. There are some "crazy" or "fun" houses I've been too elsewhere that are really quite cool, but this is randomness without purpose.
Yes the concept and idea is nice,... But charging that money for a 15 minutes walk is quite expensive for a unfinished project...
Good if you have kids otherwise don't bother. All a bit Disney but not in a good way. Plastic fantastic.
Look nice in picture but not when you are in there!!! The area around is not so big and not much to explore but only taking some picture...
still a building site nothing of intrest. you pay 50000 to walk round a hotel thats still under construction, you do get a nice view at top but not worth it.
It was not like I thought, not natural, and when we went inside, many things still repaired. Many Russian came... they play Russian music!

We went here as our final stop as part of a day tour. For the most part it is under construction with the odd nail and bit of wood sticking out along your path. All there is to do here is walk along the various stairways, it's a bit of a maze. It's fun for a few minutes and then quite boring. If you're looking to kill half an hour you can take some nice pictures of the view at the top. Otherwise I probably wouldn't bother.

Taking a Saturday morning walk to this attraction only to find it is not open to public on weekends.  Opening times are 08:30 to 19:00 Monday to Friday, closed Saturday and Sunday. Entrance fee is 40,000dong

A few nicely decorated themed rooms connected by a maze of walkways. We found it a bit kitschy and dangerous on some of the high staircases with minimal handrails on the left and right. It was certainly unique, though more of an overgrown idea by a wealthy lady than a genuine attraction.
A meager mix of a decaying Efteling building with a touch of Dali. Back out within 5 minutes. The 2nd star is for the flowers. They were beautiful.

This place is not worth the 40.000vnd fee. Lot's of work are currently under way to build new rooms for rent. Architechture that wish to be à "like" Gaudi in Barcelona but not at the right level of expectation. 5.000 vnd for motobike parking which is more than twice thé price every where else in Vietnam ! Go away from this visit and spend more time visiting market, pagoda or lakes.

It's funny that they call the house eco construction and 'close to the nature' building while everything is made of pure concrete. For me the place was very fake and cold. After seing Helga's Folly in Sri Lanka this is a big dissapointment.
Not as impressive as I thought it would be and half of it is still under construction! Saying that, it is only a couple of dollars and in the city centre.

In short: meh... it's low-quality Disneyland construction, nothing like Gaudí's work, it's cheap, and very dangerous for children. I did like the view from the rooftop. I would never spend a night in this hotel.

Maybe I just don't get it but this place is a waste!  It reminds me of a very ill-conceived Disney attraction.
