It is sloppily done, tacky without merit, its not even interesting and it is the most expensive entrance fee of anyplace we visited in DaLat.  I can not even imagine returning here.  Save your money!

We just visited today and we are more disapointed than surprised. I am pretty sure kids without parents will have fun but its not for adults at all. For me is just to build something to earn money. Nothing special

Dalat is a beautiful city, but CrazyHouse is a bit overrated. You won't miss anything if you don't go there. In my opinion, it lacks artistic or aesthetic value. Something prevents this eclectic structure from becoming a work of art. Gaudi's influence is evident, but there's no comparison: there are architectural masterpieces there, and here, the work of a local enthusiast. There's a decent souvenir shop, and it's quite interesting to walk up the narrow stairs.

The design of the house is a little interesting but after 5 minutes it's quite repetitive. Also the super thin passageways where people constantly need to go both ways are a bit of a design flaw. Admittedly there are some good views of the city and also some good photo opportunities.There is some potential danger though as the place hasn't been completely built yet and construction is going on while everyone is visiting. If I had a child I would be reluctant to take them with me. There are some passageways that are on the ceiling of buildings of quite high up and there is only a very small railing, I would have to watch them like a hawk. Overall I think this place is pretty skippable if you don't have much time and are tossing up things to do while you're in town.

While interesting and certainly a novelty in Vietnam, this place is not original (heavily influenced by Gaudi and Dali) and is so touristy, it's not worth it. Good part: decent views of Dalat from certain points. Bad parts: much of it is under construction (not good for pictures), but more importantly, it is flooded with tourists. There are parts/bridges that are pretty dangerous - narrow, high, extremely low "guard" railings, and jammed with people. Honestly, it's a recipe for disaster. For example, my husband had a very overweight woman behind him stumble in one of these very high parts, and she grabbed onto him to try to steady herself. In doing so, she almost knocked him off, which would have seriously injured if not killed him. If you still really want to see the Crazy House after reading this, I would try first thing in the morning before crowds come - for your own safety and enjoyment.
It is fun to wander around, and cheap enough... but I would highly recomment the 100 roof bar... much better for getting lost in a crazy house!!

An interesting experience. A lot of the site is under construction and still open to the public. Some of these areas are quite unsafe so be very careful. We felt 100 Roof Bar was a far better and entertaining experience!

It was definitely an interesting place and the entrance fee of 40,000VND was worth exploring the oddly designed buildings. When we were here, some parts of the Crazy House were still under construction so we think they are expanding it further. But you could see a nice view of the whole city if you climb all the way to the top. We spent less than an hour here but it was more than enough to take photos and wander around :)
The crazy house is ok, but not spectaculair. Just a weird architectual build house. Don't think it is worth spending more than one hour here.

The Crazy House is worth a visit if you have nothing else planned, which is pretty much always the case in Da Lat (the area outside is truly beautiful, but the city itself has little to offer). The 40k entrance fee is quite high compared to other places. Many of the buildings are unfinished, and the narrow paths aren't very safe. On the plus side, there are two souvenir shops selling generic trinkets. We were done with our visit in 40 minutes. The view is nice enough, but since the city isn't particularly attractive...

It's just so-so for adults, but it's fine for kids. It's best for the elderly to avoid it. It's basically a bunch of weird staircases to walk around in, and some parts are still under construction. I would recommend it to kids if you have them!
Nothing much. Just walking around, taking photos and sightseeing Dalat city view. It's very beautiful.Some zone are under construction that you should be walk carefully.

This place is cool, you will probably spend a good hour here.. Cheap enough to get into, if you have a moped you get charged 5000 to park beside the building..  I think it was 40000 to get into the place..
It can certainly be called a "peculiar" building. Many small bridges over great heights, with only a low balustrade. Not child-friendly. Still worth visiting if you are in Dalat.
Unique architecture! Under renovation, expensive for what it is! But nice! However, it's not the visit I would recommend!
The place is too small and crowd. It was hard to move especially on steps. Taking photo was also hard.It is hard to find place which has less human beings on background :)
There's not much to see in Dalat, and you end up seeing the Crazy House. People compare it to Gaudí, and honestly... it's incomparable...

We had a nice visit to the Crazy House. As we entered, we had a very informative introduction to it by a young lady whose English was really good. We then wandered around looking at the very interesting architecture. The only suggestion I would have is to a route around the house as the stairs are only just big enough for one person. Most of our time was spent squeezing past people on the stairs and not actually knowing where to go. Whilst I appreciate this is probably one of the charms of the Crazy House, squeezing past some rather large people does reduce the enjoyment hugely and I feel we missed out parts by not knowing where to go.

It was raining the day I went, so there weren't too many people. My friend, who also studied design, kept saying how amazing the design was. Actually, there are only three buildings in total, and you can walk through them in a short time. Because the shapes are so unique, it was difficult to take good photos, which was a bit of a shame. Two buildings were under construction, one of which looked like an underwater world; I'm quite looking forward to it. If I go again, I hope it will be finished by then.

We stayed two nights here in the cabin decorated with chandeliers (honeymoon). Everything was very exotic and beautiful, and there was no need for air conditioning. If you like novelty, I recommend it, but if you're looking for peace and quiet, it's not a good choice since this place is also a museum open from 8:30 a.m. to 7:00 p.m., and many people wander around the house. Therefore, to maintain privacy, we had to keep the curtains and the door closed.

The hotel is very interesting and enjoyable, but It is really dangerous for kids (and adults) on the roof and we are surprised they don't pay more attention to security. They even renovate some of the bridges and you can possible fall 15 meters if you don't pay attention.

An unusual work of art, which is absolutely worth seeing, but is currently still a partially completed and unsecured construction site. Therefore, a visit with small children is not recommended until it is finished. The safety precautions at dizzying heights do not meet European standards.

You can walk around the entire house in 20 minutes. Everything around it looks a bit rundown. It's worth visiting for some beautiful photos from the highest point, which offers a beautiful view of the city.

We went here as we were wanting to find things to walk to...not a challenging walk from the city center.Amazing imagination of the creator of this place..the buildings are like a maze/walking through tree houses. Random but fun is my best description.
The house is unique, cost a lot of money to build. As time goes by, the owner may not have enough money to do maintenance for it.

It is a very special house with very nice rooms. It is a pity that it is not finished yet and that the staircases are a bit narrow in some places – passing each other is not possible. I will definitely go back in a few years to see how it has turned out!

The crazy house is something I would recommend you visit. However I'm in two minds about this review. As while I did find it entertaining and amusing to venture through what I describe as a series of houses created in the midst of an epic LSD trip, I did have some major safety concerns. Half of the "crazy house" is a construction site that is not at all appropriate or safe for people to be walking over. The majority of the small walk ways from one building to another are suspended high in the air with out any safety rails. I'm not being dramatic on a lot of these little bridges one slip and you could literally die if you fell over the edge, with some of the rails being at knee height it's more a case of when not if they have a major incident here. A lot of elderly tourists and young children climbing over this amazing construction made for some of these tiny walk ways to be quite congested. I feel if you go here please make sure you are sober and if you are elderly or have small children be very mindful of them as the risk of fall or even injuring yourself on exposed steel and wire in the parts still under construction is quite real. You will how ever get an amazing view and see something that you are unlikely to see any where else in the world.... So go but please tread lightly.
A few winding staircases, but overall nothing really special. Worth a visit if you have some time to spare, but not a must.

A concrete mystery walk. Around every bend is another pretty niche. Like hollowed out trees then converted into little rooms inside the trunk with stairs support ed by branches around the outside.book a room for the night if you get a chance.
I expected much more than I got. With kids it was ok. But for adults - I think it's kind of boring. And there was an ongoing construction, which made it difficult to get to some places.

This was a fun activity during our stay in Da Lat.  As one piece of graffiti claimed "this is Gaudi on Acid." It was fun and worth the small entrance fee although. This activity is not recommended for anyone afraid of heights or narrow passages as the climbs through and to buildings are rather heigh (with not much safety equipment in place) and a tight squeeze - it was virtually impossible to have any sort of two way traffic. It was a quick but enjoyable visit and worth popping into during a stay in Da Lat.
It was rather a small area. The kids will find it interesting as some of houses are small with different names like Ant Bear and a few more. This is another touristy place.

I am sure the Disney designers got some of their inspiration from this bizarre creation. Product of a vivid and perhaps contorted imagination, the house is worth a visit if only to see what can be done with concrete and iron bars! Only a word of warning - health and safety doesn't get a look in here so if you have children beware!
Quite a sight, a little Barcelona perhaps?

Worth a look round if you have an hour to spare in Dalat. The architecture is bizarre - like nothing I've ever seen before! The story of the architects is interesting too. The staff are really helpful and friendly. It's not the safest building - lots of high slippery staircases with low handrails. Perhaps not the best for young children. We went in the rain and although it wasn't too bad some of the stairs and paths were very slippery when wet.

We spent a night staying there. The room is quite creepy. The wifi sucks though there is a router in your room. Got wake up by tourists in the early morning. Can't understand why tourists would still attempt to open your door and keep on knocking on your windows when there's obviously someone inside. There's also no elevator and steps are narrow, definitely won't recommend you to stay if you are with luggages. But the staff are really nice and helpful.

A certain daughter of a Vietnamese party leader from the Brezhnev era found herself in Moscow, at the famous Moscow Architectural Institute (MARCHI). After a wild time in this "soup" bubbling with ideas, talent, alcohol, and (!!possibly!!) light drugs, upon returning home, this young woman built an intricate, highly eclectic structure. It blends reproductions from Gaudi albums, a touch of Abramtsevo near Moscow, a touch of Italian romanticism, and many other grains of various architectural masterpieces. All of this is carefully blended and seasoned with the creator's Vietnamese genes. Take a look, it's hilarious.

It will be pretty cool when it's finishhed but the at least half of the place is under construction. There were no barriers between actually finished parts of the building and the areas in various stages of completion which made for some pretty precarious exploring.

Winding staircases, terraces, and bridges, this unique architectural masterpiece has been brilliantly crafted. However, as a group of 4 recent college graduates, we probably didnt appreciate it as much as the typical tourist. Atop the structures there are also beautiful views of the city. Overall, definitely worth the 40,000 dong...or $1.75

A parody of Gaudi. Unusual, yes. The hotel's comfort is beyond words. Crazy is what this place is all about. It feels strange there. Like inside an inflamed brain. Maybe that's how it's supposed to be? I don't feel like staying here. Just gawking once and that's it.

The Crazy House is worth a visit and seeing the amazing weird style hotel. It is really cool. You go up and around walk ways so small that you are lucky to pass other people in some places. They wind up down and around. Go at 830am when they open to avoid the crowds. Arrive  early and have breaky at the nice cafe in front of the crazy house (with your back to the crazy house go to the left cafe, down the stairs) they have nice food and coffee.
I've been here for 3 times i think. The first time was nice and a little tired though. But for the second times is definitely  a waste of time.

Designed by Vietnamese architect Dang Viet Nha, it is still under construction. As you climb the precarious staircase to the top floor of this building, which also functions as a hotel, the city of Da Lat comes into view.
A few years ago, visiting Crazy House was very interesting and fascinating. But this time, it wasn't as good as before. The owner expanded it for business, so it no longer looks spooky. :(

I booked in Crazy house as a new experience. Its a crazy place indeed with lots of bizarre decorations all about animals and nature , rooms are like caves or animal houses, but the problem is this is a touristic site where they open the place for visitors from 8 Am till late 7 and they get around 500 visitors per day.So imagin you wake up in the morning just like an animal in the zoo people try to break into your room so noisy and you definitely can't have any privacy.They even wanna take photos of you inside the room, it's insane.Also if you are claustrophobic you should never consider this hotel for your stay as the rooms are too small.My recommendation is to book into a normal hotel in Da Lat but Crazy house is a must visit place.
Seems to be in a Disney attraction at some amusement park...it is nothing special, but it is a fun way to spend 15-20 minutes!
If u like photography, this is the place to visit to. It is a amazing creation from the designer and made you to know how the architecture works. But be careful when you climb up and down.
It's amusing for the first 5 minutes, but then it's just confusing. For 40k VND I'd recommend going, but it's not finished yet, and I feel like the place has more to offer.
It's fun and you get to see a lot of beautiful views from the top. Around 20,000 to get in per person. Lots to see and explore and you can stay there too if you wish (it's also a hotel).

Was kind of fun to see but the web promised more than it actually was. It's a fun to go through the house with some friends goofing around. If your not sure if you want to go to da lat don't do it just for the crazy house.
It'll be nice when it's finished. Still nice and kinda cool but still construction work taking place.
I thought it would have been crazier. Was cool though and worth the 40k ticket price. Great for pictures and up the top there is a great view of Dalat.

I've given it 3 stars but I would still recommend a visit as its relatively cheap and central. Essentially it's just a mass of winding staircases to walk around with no perpendicular construction. It offers good views of the city from the top. There is one room however that contains antique furniture that is so out of place, but I suppose it is a crazy house.

You can see the view of the city from there.  It has a strange architecture...small rooms in a tree-like structure where you go up and down the narrow stairs and you'll find different rooms. Worth a visit.
interesting architecture concept.. integrating with nature to form a mini hotel in this area.. other than that.. nothing much to see.. part of it is still under renovation..

Maybe it's because we have the Efteling with our Dutch neighbors, but it bears a strong resemblance. It's quite nice to walk through once in a while, but like everything in Da Lat, the "attractions" truly live up to the name. It's all rather kitsch and too much of a hassle.
It's defiantly a cool place and something to do if you have a free day but it's nothing special cost is 40000 so if your on a tight budget skip it and go see some waterfalls

I guess the crazy house still needs some time to finish. Although it looks amazing and if you have kids, definitely worth a visit. It's a fantasy hotel, but not finished yet. So everywhere you go you will find unfinished bits and peaces, this spoils the whole fantasy experience for me. Entry is 40k a person and kids go for 20k. Time spend around the crazy house was about 30 minutes, keep that in mind.Would like to go back when finished and see how it looks like.

Cost 40k to get in and wasnt reeealy worth it. Some cool 'crazy' stuff to take some piccys with but not worth it as a trip by yourself, would be ok if it was included on a tour or something maybe. Go for a walk around the lake or to the museum instead

This was the first crazy house I have visited in the top ten craziest houses in the world - and it won't be the last. The other nine are now on the bucket list. Built by "the crazy lady" This house was crazylicious . Fun to wonder around for an hour or so.

I think is a bit overrated. I didn't find it interesting but for the kids, is a good fun. Safety is a concern though. The stairs are narrow and if you're afraid of heights, this could be a challenge. We only spend about 1 hour or so here.

It is said to be the top 10 craziest houses in the world. Indeed, when you climb on it, you'll never know where you are heading to , but a lot of the parts are under construction and gets quite messy. The steps can be steep and slippery when wet.
Crazy climbing wall. Be careful where you step and definitely don't take children with you, it's a pain in the ass. Lots of tourists everywhere and the entrance fee is quite high.

I had to put some thought into this one, as it is different and as others have put quirky, my wife isn't great with heights and passed going up the steps, I raised this was a good move as a few pole did trugglecwith heights and nothing to hang on to.For me the biggest disappointment was the van or buses of tourists who turned up and have no manners, it is difficult to pass one another but a lot easier with some courtesy.One highlight for us was outside where a young lady had a coffee stand, the coffee was great.
If you have seen Spanish moderns (Dali and Gaudi) then some if this attraction will look familiar. Worth a look.
Ok to visit if you have spare time. Still lots of work going on so a bit of a building site in places. The rooms not occupied looked interesting
I came here in November and it was nice enough. Maybe once it is finished it will be a better experience. There is still construction going on and loads of tourists...

We spent an hour hear walking through all the twists and turns in this crazy place.  A lot of work and money has gone into building this place and if your looking for somewhere unusual to stay, this could be the place for you. Probably wouldn't return to this place, if you have time to fill in and want to see something a little different then this could be the place for,you.

The Crazy House in Dalat is undoubtedly one of the city's main attractions. However, it wasn't really to our taste (my girlfriend's and mine). It's certainly quite wacky... but we felt more like we were in a theme park than admiring great art. Of course, it's a matter of taste.

I was very excited to visit this place and walked away feeling a tad disappointed. Don't get me wrong, it's very interesting, with crazy architechture, walkways that wouldn't pass a health and safety inspection and great views over Da Lat. However it's walkways get very overcrowded, and that urge you get to go running around like a kid is prevented by people constantly posing for photos. The rooms are either locked as they are for guests, or unfinished with building materials left around giving you the feeling you shouldn't be in there. I was hoping there would be crazy things in the rooms. It's fun - but it's not what I'd hoped for. But that's just my opinion!The large birds in small cages in the garden is grim to see.
Not recommended if you don't like height. Stairs are narrow. And hand rails are low.Other than that very curious place with plenty of things to see and intricate architecture.

This place is a nice visit and it would have get an higher grade if it wasn't for the fact that a lot of the place is under construction. That wouldn't have bother me much but the construction areas are not close d to the public. That means you are able to walk around where you can cut, harm or even fall down from high up. Dangerous, especially for kids. I bet it will be great when it's done but until then they should consider doing something to improve the safety.

Definitely a unique and interesting hotel!!!Lots of twists and turns and walk ways over the roofs; they have some really interesting bedrooms although the hotel is still active so when the rooms are occupied you cannot see them.Apparently it is a great place for photoshoots and selfies so you do get held up when trying to get around by tourists stopping and posing. As many of the paths are quite narrow and high up i found it wuite terrifying since i'm scared of heights as i did a lot of waiting since i was stuck behind a large group taking photos!
Felt a bit like toon town in disneyland. Definitely a sight to see, but no need to spend more than 40 minutes tops there in my opinion.

My boyfriend and I visited here as part of a day tour! It is a very wacky place, pretty cool but I wouldn't recommend much time here it was fine as a stop off on the way home! There are various different rooms which host a lot of character I believe you can stay in the rooms but check in is 6pm and check out 6am due to it being a tourist attraction.

The visit was part of a tour, we wouldn't have purposely gone, the idea is interesting but already time and climate is rough, not very comfortable narrow stairs and crossings, and there are traffic jams of people!and beautiful photos did not turn out much!

Place is nice and quirky to spend a few hours wondering around, there is a caffe and shop but no one was in it.You are able to stay in the rooms but not sure I would want to with people walking around looking inside them
All the rooms were secured with chains and locks, can only view from outside. Average attraction with a nice underseas portion
A bit of a sought-after copy of Gaudi, but without Gaudi's creative mind.Nice to see.

I am very impressed with the architecture of the house, it is truly as crazy as the name. It would be nice to go during the day and leave before 6pm. Because the evening is mostly very dark, the lights are dim and it is very dangerous to visit.

The rooms are fun, reflecting the Crazy House's theme, but I don't think they'd be suitable for tall people, as the shell-shaped bed isn't really designed for that. Everything you need is provided: toiletries, tea, coffee, and a kettle.

What an imagination. No rhyme nor reason to any of the structures and the linking of each. The designer obviously knew what she was doing but I defy anyone else to interpret it. However if you don’t go you won’t be able to offer an opinion
It’s a crazy house and we’re glad we went but it’s a little tired looking and cheesy. Ticket prices are reasonable so if you’ve got an extra hour or two why not.
Definitely more for people who are interested with architecture. Interesting from outside or inside, pay the small fee and enjoy unusual colours,  shapes and structure

Very mediocre experience. No information provided about the house at all so, whilst impressive to see, it was all a bit lost on us. We stayed close by and luckily visited in the morning as by early afternoon it was packed out with huge numbers of tour companies.
Worth a visit, just to see how other people can build their imagination into reality. Not a long activity (unless you get lost in the labyrinth of different stairs and buildings.

Crazy House is one of the tourist attractions in Dalat. It is unique and different from the usual tourist attractions. It also has its own history. There is an entrance fee. Suitable for the whole family to visit.

we were excited to visit this place as recommended by my friends. I appreciate the the very creative mind who made it.  But i discourage if your going there with small kids as it is not safe to explore the house with them especially going up and down on  an even stairways.

We found the crazy house to be very gimmicky and too busy. More like a theme park attraction than a traditional example of architecture. But perhaps this is the intention ! Kids would probably find it fun!

The construction is a mixture of Modernist structures that provides many stairs joining different levels of the building. Some shops and accomodation included in the confusion set on a large suburban block of land with further construction underway. A performance area and Twisting gardens are also included. I had hoped it would be similar to those wonderful constructions in Spain,  but alas no. You would need to be fit and careful if visiting as the stairs are lethal in terms of safety. Heaps of visitors.

A very plesant and interesting experience walking about this hotel/tourist attraction. A bit of Alice in Wonderland meets Vietnamese Style. Try and get there before the hoards of Russian tourists from Nha Trang decend upon it for thier rapid fire day tour.
I visited this place after seeing it on the media.
This just seems like an old amusement park.  Maybe if I had kids with me this would be fun, but as a solo traveler this was not interesting at all.  I could not have gotten out of there fast enough.
It was a crazy house indeed but not that exciting - maybe kids finds it more entertaining...It is also a hotel
It was larger than expected.
Some of the steps can be dangerous especially for young kids. It was high and narrow and when it’s wet, it can be very slippery

Crazy House reminded me a little of the innovativeness of Antoni Gaudi and Park Guell. While interesting, my main concern is the lack of guardrail safety features, for adults and particularly, for children.

Everything is undergoing renovation and restoration, and you can only see a couple of locations, which I didn't like at all. It's unusual, though, and there are some beautiful spots. There's an entire floor with historical photos and original sketches, which was even more memorable and intriguing than the courtyards and staircases. If you're in Dalat, you might want to stop by.

We visited the Crazy House for the first time.It is certainly a strange, weird and unique place, think a cross between Gaudi's Barcelona and Cappodocia in Turkey !!Some good views of the surrounding town though.Seems to have been designed by someone under the influence of psychodelic substances !!Admission price 60,000 VND per person.One visit here is probably enough.
I wasn't impressed. It's all jumbled up, but pointless. It doesn't compare to Gaudi's work. It's a pathetic parody. I didn't like anything there. Although I saw tourists who said they liked it.
At first, the façade is certainly surprising, but after a while you get used to it. There are a lot of tourists, as visiting hours are limited.
This place is like a bad copy of Park Guel in Barcelona. But I guess that if you are in Dalat it is one of the things to do. Maybe come early in the morning to avoid the crowds.

I felt it was oke. Suitable for Instagrammers and kids as it feels like a playground. Can surely take some nice pics in there. So, for families I would strongly recommend it, while for solo travelers I would say it’s optional depending on your interest. It doesn’t have much cultural value. If you go, I think it’s worth paying the entrance fee of 60,000 VND and going inside because from the outside you can’t really grasp the building. It’s a lot larger than I first expected.

The architecture is neat and there's a lot of stairs and walkways to explore. Good view from top. Would be a good place for kids. Lots of tourists and the price was a bit much considering I was bored after 15 minutes.

The building is unusually constructed; you can wander through its labyrinths and corridors, but it's not particularly impressive. It's a tourist trap. It has no historical value, and its architectural interest is questionable. They simply slapped together everything they could.
This area is actually quite kitschy with major safety deficiencies.
I had absolutely no intention of visiting the Crazy House
We didn’t had the best time there, found it very boring after 30 min of walking around.Not a bad place but  probably not great for people around the age of 20

Aptly named, The Crazy House is Disney like in many ways. Amazingly people do vacation in the rooms here surrounded by the constant flow of curious tourists. The storey behind it could be the most interesting part. Each room stled towards a different animal representing countries involved in the history of Vietnam. Go early to avoid the masses of tourists getting log jammed on the Maze of one person width bridges and stairs.

We arrived there around 4pm and were confronted with buses and a crowd of people queuing to enter. More buses were arriving and offloading more tourists and it was obvious that the visit was not going to be a good one. We turned around and gave this tourist attraction a miss. Probably a good idea to get there when it opens first thing in the morning and avoid the crowd.
Cool, worth a a visit but maze bar is better. This is over priced for what it is. I think families would particularly like it though
I kept wondering what might go through the mind of an architect whose imagination could conjure something like that. Well, I don't really want to know. I think it's not worth going there.

Located 2KM from the city, we walked all the was to crazy house with a baby stroller. It was bit difficult to cross the road as the city has no traffic lights but after some 30 mins we made it. Crazy house is really crazy a real architecture wonder. Some part of the crazy house looks unsafe for unaccompanied kids. Toilets and small shops are available next the attraction. The only problem was it looks very crowded maybe they could consider limiting number of people entering into the building. It looks very unsafe when it was crowded. But still worth visiting!

60,000VND per pax (just under 3USD). Parking to the left of the entrance 5,000VND (20c USD) Really interesting architecture & some cool spaces throughout but that's about it. Worth the visit if you're in Da Lat but truthfully, was only blown away by the bar/ dance floor area. Narrow, single-file stairs, awkward when there are loads of visitors. Some parts still under construction. I imagine it's a lot better if you actually stay there in the hotel rooms.
The architecture forms various shapes and maze. Curved and twisted buildings. I did not find it worth our time.

The place is really crazy. But in good way. The houses are fantastic. Remind me buildings designed by Antonio Gaudi. You can really lost over there. In one point i realised that im in the same place third time even i used different way. Its really good design. The only bad thing is it’s way overcrowded. They let inside everyone so sometimes are massive queues to see anything inside. Overall is very nice place
It's shoddy. Hahaha. Hmm, they say it was inspired by Gaudí, but the paintwork and everything is quite crude...

It's just an average-looking small house with an unusual design. The entrance fee is 60,000 VND, which I felt wasn't worth it. If you don't need to, don't come. But if you have nothing to do, it's okay to look at to relieve boredom.

Dont go on a full stomach.Bizarre. Weird & unusual. No warning about the heights or low parapet walls with plastered rebar railings.. with hairline cracks. be careful with putting yr weight or leaning on the parapet or "branch" railings.one we grabbed was broken. the balconies are narrow so it is unsafe when 2 persons cross each other. No regulation on route to take so traffic was moving in all directions. Some parts still hv exposed steel rebars.Avoid if u hv fear of heights or poor sense of balance.Doesnt pass my safety standard.

It is still building. I could see 11 rooms in buiding. And I had a headache after going around. :)) I love high stair road from the entrance to each areas. I can see overview by going to the roof of buiding. But there are not too many real trees. So, it is not a nature building.
I held onto the railing as I climbed up... my legs were trembling. A maze of paths and rooms. Is this some kind of top 10 architectural masterpiece in the world?! Do I really have to go...?

I like the design of the walkway, very unique and colourful, but the rails of the walkway and stairs very low, lower than knee level, if you loss your balance while climbing these stairs, you will likely to fall and get serious permanent injury or worse, death.
It's interesting how detailed everything was built. It's something you could take inspiration from. I was through it very quickly, though.

The CrazyHouse was built by a Vietnamese architect. It's actually a wacky hotel, but these days it probably makes its living from tours. Our guide took us here on a city tour. You can wander through the corridors and look around, but we didn't find it particularly special. It's certainly eccentric, and they're still expanding it.
Remarkable to see. Otherwise not very special. Mostly just a lot of tourists walking in and out. Not a single room is straightforward.
It's an unusual building, nothing more, in my personal opinion. But it's worth a look; the entrance fee is cheap.
I couldn't get enthusiastic about this place although I could see others were. It all felt rather pointless and dated somewhat as well. I could never have stayed there personally.

I had high expectations because it is said to be a must-visit place in Da Lat, but I hesitated at the expensive entrance fee and felt claustrophobic due to the bizarrely shaped buildings crammed together in a small space. Since the buildings are connected by narrow external staircases, it will be somewhat uncomfortable for those with a fear of heights to get around. Just take a look around with the mindset that since you are in Da Lat, a place like this exists.

We went in the evening and had high expectations given the reviews. It was a good place to check out but it wasn't the highlight of our time in Da Lat. But the restaurant opposite the crazy house was very delicious - good food and drinks!
Unusual design as its description suggests, something to do while in Dalat but not a must do. Nice little restaurant opposite.

Yesterday we spent a couple of hours in this strange place. It is very hard to get any good photos of the place as a whole, but hopefully you’ll get the general idea from these 2 photos. You can climb around the whole structure on walkways and stairs. You actually get to some good heights about 6 stories up. There were some nice views, but I wouldn't call it a safe place as handrails were very low or non-existent. The first photo is of one of several very high structures connected by narrow walkways high up in the air.
The unconventional architecture inspired by animals is unique and makes it a great photo spot for young people. Spending over an hour climbing up and down the hills is also a good form of exercise.

I came here with high expectations because it seemed like a must-visit place when in Da Lat... but it's just so-so compared to what I expected. It is unique, though. It's worth seeing if you go without high expectations. The staff member was friendly and even held my luggage for me.

It's beautiful, but also unique. The paths are winding and confusing, you might get lost, but it's not absolutely necessary to come. If you do come, do it in the evening; the weather is nice and the view is beautiful.

We visited the Crazy House in the afternoon and received a friendly welcome. Inside the house, you climb around and learn nothing else about the architecture, the concept, or the materials. You might want to book a guided tour; otherwise, it's only really suitable for children who are confident climbers.
It's something built for visitors to see. I felt indifferent about it. I walked around, took a few pictures, and then left. There were a lot of tourists.
This place is ok. Lots of stairs and walkways and cool graffiti/decor. But really not that great....
Crazy House is full of unusual buildings.

A nice visit and nothing more.. More suitable for children in my opinion.. There's not much to do except see the cool stalls that were set up there, which are actually impressive.. Entrance costs 120,000 dong (less than 20 NIS), an hour's tour will be enough for you there..
It would not be on my to do list in hindsight. Unique and interesting to see. I think kids would enjoy it more.

Even before visiting, I suspected there would be ridiculous comparisons claiming the Crazy House was influenced by the Spanish architect Gaudí, but I visited out of a sense of obligation as it is a landmark in Da Lat.
Not advisable for those with weak legs (like me) and those afraid of heights. It's an unusual architecture but too crowded and narrow to move around.
You can easily spend an hour in Crazy House, but it's not a particularly great experience. Gaudi-inspired art, but far from the same brilliance.
Nice building  that's itThey are making it bigger You have a nice viewAnd nice place to make selfies

Apparently, we were unlucky; when we visited this "madhouse," there were a lot of people, including Chinese. The rather narrow paths and stairs made it difficult to pass each other, and we often had to back up.

A really great, new, and amazing place! The ticket is only 50,000 (currency unspecified). You can see the country from the top of the house and enjoy the fresh air. I recommend going early, before 12 o'clock.

We went there as it was close to our hotel. I can't say I liked it much. Had afuneral parlour feeling.  The lady who owns it somewhere in the middle of the house???!!!  More rooms are being built. If your passing by have a look but I wouldn't  go out of your way.

The building has a unique architectural style. If you're visiting Dalat and have some spare time, I recommend visiting it. You won't find much to draw you in, but it will take you about half an hour of curious exploration. If you have extra time on your sightseeing day, it's a good option to fill your time. At the entrance, there are street vendors with lots of fresh fruit at very cheap prices.

50,000 VND to get in... is worth it if you have the extra time but I wouldn’t make it a ‘must do’. Lots of stairs and walkways to little rooms etc. All maybe a bit same same after a while. Some parts under construction but still lots to see there :) very quirky!

It is not completed. And also dangerous even though you are adults because the safety rails are too low .It easy to get there Fun to see and walk around but definitely not the highlight for Trip in Dalat.
It is crazy, but doesn’t feel so special or fascinating... if you’re not around don’t get there especially
Funny to watch in itself, but rapidly becoming a tourist trap... it is being expanded further and further, and as a result, little of the original story remains.
It is a bit peculiar. As an ordinary person, I didn't feel it possessed tremendous artistic merit.
This house is just as it says, a Crazy house. Rooms are disarrayed, abject, a must see if you are in teh area, but not really something to go out of your way to see.

I saw it frequently on blogs while preparing for my trip and went there specifically because I was curious about the bizarre yet fascinating architectural style. However, if you are short on time in Da Lat, I don't think it is worth making a special trip. It was disappointing that the maintenance was poor.
For those who can't quite place it, the Crazy House could be compared to a Hundertwasser building.

You can take a look, but you're not missing anything if you don't. Entry costs around €2, and you can walk through the entire area. The bridges are very narrow, and you sometimes have to wait before you can continue. It's very winding, and children will certainly enjoy exploring everything.
Absolutely unimpressive. It's all very fancy, but it's unclear what the architect put into this building. It's not exactly Gaudi.

50,000 Vietnamese Dong per person. It felt like just looking around; nothing particularly special. It's not a great place for taking photos; just browsing. Lots of foreign tourists~ and many places under renovation.

Known for its architectural marvel the place offers a fun activity day which lasts only about an hour. The ticket price proves to be expensive for the space. But it does feel like a walking roller coaster and one can admire the structural beauty.

Someone recommended this place, and I came because a friend wanted me to. But when I got here, what was that? There was nothing there! The walkways were narrow and very dangerous. All I could think was, "What am I doing here?" And I had to pay an entrance fee, just so you know.
Quite nice as a quick stop. It is not far, within walking distance of the center. Work is still being done hard on the expansion.
Nice, not a must. Entertaining house if you're around, come in, if it doesn't work out for you, no big deal. It has nothing to do with Vietnam, it could be in any other city in the world.
Nice and funny to see. Unfortunately, more than half of it was under construction. Such a shame! This made it less fun. The 100 Roof Bar was nicer.

Be prepared for a very very touristic place ( as in busses full of Russian tourists), but even then it is a fun house to explore. They are still building and expanding this crazy house. It is interesting to see how they build it in concrete.

Perhaps I had high expectations, but when I got there, I felt quite indifferent. The unique and unconventional shape is great for taking photos, but it seemed unfinished when I visited, so there wasn't much space left to explore.

I visited because my accommodation was nearby. Crazy House is constantly expanding, to the point where it would take a long time to explore every nook and cranny. If you go up to the higher areas, there is a refreshing view. There are also many narrow paths, so you need to be careful.

It's certainly unique, kind of along the lines of Gaudi in Barcelona.  I thought climbing the unusual staircases to the upper levels was a bit unsafe, as the railings or sides were quite low, and it was very crowded, with people passing both ways.  We didn't spend very much time here as I wasn't comfortable on the stairs.

I gather it is in an never ending state of construction and will continue to be treasure trove of rooms to see, corridors winding into nowhere and generally mass confusion. But highly interesting. This question one asks is : '' What am I doing here''

What the title says. I came the first time 5 years ago and then it was a really cool place to go visit. This time i didnt even get inside. People was parking down the street because the 2 adjacent parking lots was full. If you dont mind overcrowded tourist traps, then by all means go. The house is quite something else! If you dont like crowded places… come back in low season.

This place is stunning, the structure is a bit different. The engineering is amazing. Only thing I don't recommend is Do Not bring a child going up. And do not climb when it's wet because its quiet slippery and unsafe. There are souvenirs shop inside. The view is captivating and is instagramable. You can look for this place if you like adventure in a crazy way.

The Crazy House is a kind of miniature fantasy land. Very detailed and bizarre. However, it's absolutely overrun with Chinese and Russian tourists who create a cacophony of noise with megaphones and blaring cell phones. If you're into kitsch and don't mind it, you'll find your bliss here.
Yeah it's a crazy house. Easy to get there Fun to see and walk around but definitely not the highlight of your trip.
The Crazy House is certainly interesting to see. Unfortunately, it was completely overrun with tour groups.

In December, we visited the famous Crazy House. The guesthouse of Mrs. Dang Viet Nga is a working hotel built by the daughter of the former General Secretary of the Communist Party of Vietnam. The building is designed in a surrealist style, symbolizing freedom, unity with nature, and originality of thought. At some point, the funds for construction ran out, and Madam decided to open a museum in the building and rent out rooms to tourists. She called her hotel Hang Nga (Moon Villa), but the name didn't stick. Crazy House is difficult to describe. It's like a huge old tree stump, overgrown with moss and entwined with cobwebs, with protruding roots that serve as staircases leading into tunnels and labyrinths. Each room is dedicated to a different animal and decorated to match: the Tiger Room, the Kangaroo Room, the Ant Room, the Bear Room, and so on. There are practically no right angles in the building, and the stairs are so steep and narrow that it's impossible for two people to pass each other!

I have stayed there for two nights at bear room. Room was with big bathroom, but room itself is slightly small, and if ur tall and try to sit on the wooden sofa at the room, make sure u ll hit ur shoulder.To be fair, the place is nice, but u ve to consider ur needs before decide to stay there. Place is considered one of the 20 unique hotels worldwide. But u still can visit it with a ticket on daily basis from 8:30 to 19:00 which is mentioned during ur booking. But this makes place very noisy at tht time. Even if ur in the room and put don't disturb, visitors still insist to try to open the door to see the room. So if u plan to spend the night only and no rest during mid day so u ll not be bothered, but if ur for mid day nap it will be totally hard. Staff there are helpful but totally busy and exhausted during visit time.We arrived there very early in the morning and they let us in and helped us to stay in quit place to rest till the room is free. Really thankful for their efforts.So as summery, very nice place to visit. To stay at night it s very nice and quit, during visit, totally noisy.
It was nice to visit this crazy house but the Mazecafe was bether!! Still it is worth to visit this crazy place and you have a bether view over the nice city DaLat

We had fun exploring and climbing around Crazy House for about an hour.However, as of Dec 2018, there are several areas and rooms which are still being built/renovated, which makes the experience less magical. For example, you'd climb up a kooky staircase only to discover that the rooms at the top are still under constrution. Also, please hold on tightly to your kids and old folk! Several staircases wind across rooftops and have minimal or very low railings.

I'm sorry to say that I was completely underwhelmed by this place & fail to see the point of it. I realise that others would disagree, but it wasn't for me. There were some very steep steps in one part of the building and I felt the safety aspect wasn't at all good - it would have been really easy to fall over the side, as there are no safety barriers or rails. I wouldn't recommend.
We went as part of a private tour.It was rammed.If it had been less busy we could have walked but queuing to go up and down and people backtracking / stopping in large groups.Pretty surreal.

The relatively low price (50k dong) didn't deter me. And in the end, I'm glad the price wasn't higher. Ultimately, I don't know what to think of this house; "crazy" is the word that best describes it. If you have the time, go for it; otherwise, keep walking—there are much more interesting things to do in Dalat.
Crazy House is a very odd and unique experince! Making it ultimately hard to describe, would have been great if there was a map.  Would recommend coming here during a stay in Dalat.

A visit there is a must, the house is very special, the whole thing is nice, but we were there two months ago and there were a lot of parts being added and built or upgraded, which took away a bit of the fun, and there were also a lot of people. It's better to go at quieter times. Overall, it's an experience and it's nice.

Worth visiting this literally crazy house all you can say as you walk around the just wierd place is "this is crazy". Some nice views of Da Lat from the higher places. I dont recomed staying here, open to tourists in the morning people are very loud just outside your door and often trying to come into your room its very awkward. Also the bed was not very clean. We stayed here for the experience but is easily visitable as a day trip.
The Crazy House that was a hotel and became a museum. It makes you think of Gaudi. Already being augmented. I found it sad. As we saw there were people making it bigger.
The building is very cozy and special. It is still under construction (sice 1990), they add each year some places. It is a crazy architecture, for some extra money you can go for visit.
Anyway creative concept though it's crazy house50K VND per head One hour around enough It's inside the city, so can reach easily

This is the first and only question that arises when visiting Crazy House. And yes, the author, Madame Nga, smoked and still smokes marijuana, and is not shy about flaunting it. She studied in Moscow, clearly stole the idea for her creation from Barcelona, ​​and found the monetization in Hong Kong—and so the result is a formless mess with pretensions to intellectual chic. And this something is constantly being built, so you literally walk through scaffolding and bricks. Surprisingly, part of the premises are given over to hotel rooms—only a fellow crazy person would be able to stay in this chaos.
Great way to spend 30-40 minutes with little kids. They loved climbing and exploring the different areas. Just wished the bathrooms were cleaner. Had no toilet paper nor soap. Gross smell too.

These days the crazy house attracts more and more tourists, including busses with Russian tourists from Nha Trang. Still the crazy house remains an entertaining place for a short visit, but don’t expect too much.

I had high expectations because it seemed like a fun place, but it was surprisingly ordinary. If you have some extra time, it might be worth a visit, but it wasn't anything particularly unique or unusual.

A strange one to rate really, as this is a concrete work in progress, set amongst some beautiful French villas, I understand why this would draw a crowd if you like something very different, not my cup of tea but clearly took some imagination to get to this point. You can stay here if you want as well
Just a crazy building and are crowded with many tourist. Entrance is 50,000 dong and it is just a short taxi ride away.

With the crazy house its all in the name. It is a crazy strange and Interesting place at the same time. You will have to pay 50.000 dong to walk around the place. When i was there a lot of construction work and painting was being done. So not every part looked as good. Also the place should have been childfriendly considering the Disney meets art look but the stairs around the place are dangerous and not really child friendly. I am not easaly scared but those things got me to slow down a bit. Overal an interesting place but not much more then that for me.

My husband and I decided to go to Crazy House after dinner, which was a bad idea. You still can see the crazy structures and they have some lights around. However, we don't see much and not able to get a full experience if we would of come during the day. The stairs were quite slippery after the rain, and very tiny too, so it was a bit tricky to walk around for me, but I liked it.I feel like if they have more lights or charge lesser during the evening time, they could get more customers. 50k isn't worth it so much when we can't see much around. There were few times that I stumbled on random steps.I would come back again during the day time next time.

The history of this place and its founder is fascinating. It's a cool place to stay, and it is a hotel, and some of the guests are residents! It's worth a 20-30-minute visit out of curiosity. In the end, photos from this place didn't even make it into the top photos from my Vietnam trip.

After visiting it, I think there are far more charming places in Vietnam than the Crazy House. It's interesting, but nothing special. A crazy, poorly marked tour. If I remember correctly, the price is 40,000 Dong. More than the money, it's the time you spend there that you could be doing other things.
It doesn't feel like a hotel at all! I'd

Tickets have increased from 40,000 VND to 50,000 VND! The area is still under expansion and mainly caters to tour groups. Those with a fuller figure may find it difficult to pass other vehicles in the walkways. It's considered a popular spot for internet celebrities.
Quite nice to drive through for a bit, but certainly not worth the 50,000 dong entrance fee. I also found it quite dangerous. Don't take small children here! Did have a laugh there, though.

A very unusual place. If you're in Dalat, it's definitely worth a visit. Nothing spectacular, but still interesting. It's unclear how vacationers live there—there are crowds of tourists wandering around the hotel!!!! Parts are unfinished.
There's a lot of noise. I've read it a lot, and it's been so highly praised. But it didn't make any impression on me. Zero emotions.

This house really is crazy! It was like nothing I’ve ever seen before. Really interesting to walk around and explore. I think kids would especially love it! We got a brownie in the little shop and it was delicious and reasonably priced. It rained very heavily while we were here and as a lot of the stairs are outdoors, it took away from the enjoyment slightly. Definitely worth a visit if you’re in Da Lat though.
Worth a visit if you want a brilliant view of Da Lat and interested in construction and architecture. A bit tacky but worth a visit if you're in the area and visiting Bao Dai Palace which is nearby.
I arrived here and the staff were very friendly. It was cool architecture but the are building a lot so it took away from the experience as some parts are in pieces
Didn’t get a whole lot out of this place - it was enjoyable enough to walk around but needed more info on why it was built - it was just very random
It's an interesting building. Smaller than i expected. It's not far from downtown so you could visit!
It's quite novel, but the artistic feel isn't strong; overall, it feels rather messy. Don't have too high expectations. You can see the entire city center of Da Lat from the top, but it's optional.

The first thing we visited in Dalat was Crazy House. It's a very interesting place; everything about it is unusual. The buildings are even a little creepy. There are a lot of people here, and little time is spent here if you're visiting with a guide.
Different but although I walked all over I never thought it was amazing. Wouldn't go back or stay there as a guest.
I dont really get the idea of the place, it is fun to walk through, specially with kids I guess.It was very crowded when I went (around 12 am)
This was a very interesting experience, some parts of the house was dangerous so if you are a daredevil this is the perfect place for you (don't go if you have vertigo)

It is like a maze with different style of design. If this is first time you visit Dalat you can come here to have a look. This is very strange of architecture. I just go once and not like to visit again

Expected to see a proper crazy setting of a real house but this is not the case. Just lots of walls and narrow stairways. Can be difficult to walk through if it gets too crowded. So this place is overrated. Not necessary to take a private tour for this.
Gaudi like building, with rooms to sleep in. Lots of narrow walkways, cave like rooms and some strange additional features. Liked the vision but would not want to stay there.
The place and name was not match.it looks like the fairy tale place. Some parts were not yet completed.it was good for a few photo with group.Look forward to visiting again
Nothing special about this place.Crazy house was not crazy at all.aslo,this place was not finished building yet.

Many people visit to look around, but there is an entrance fee. It seems to double as a hotel, as rooms occupy the space here and there. Actually, it might feel a bit scary to stay there. Since it isn't too far from the center of Da Lat, it would be worth visiting if you have the time.

This place is literally what it says on the tin, it really is crazy! a great place for kids to spend a while running round and exploring.  worth the visit simply because you will never see anywhere else like it and there are also goo picture opportunities of the surrounding hills.

We decided to come here as we just wanted to do something a but different whilst in the city. It's interesting enough to walk around but it's more interesting for kids really. The whole place feels like an Antoni Gaudi knock off really.

A bit gimmicky, as expected, but the locals seem to think its pretty cool. Worth a look if you've got an hour or so to spare with nothing better to do, but I wouldn't go out of my way for it. Keep a close eye on any small children: they could easily fall from the elevated walkways and staircases if not closely supervised.

It's true, nothing out of the ordinary, but it's worth a look. Luckily, we were nearby and decided to stop in. There was a Russian-speaking girl at the checkout who told us a little about the building's history. Crazy House is a building with a very unusual architecture, ranked among the top ten most unusual hotels in the world. This unusual structure was designed by Vietnamese architect Dang Viet Nga. She studied architecture in Moscow and then returned to Vietnam. The building's essence was to express a desire for independence and freedom, and also to remind people to protect nature, that it's okay to use all the benefits of nature, but not to alter or destroy it. The cost of one night at the hotel is $100, and in the morning you'll be woken up as a visitor to the hotel-museum. For me, that's a dubious pleasure for that price.
The house was nice and the i dea of crazy house was fantastic. But the ticket is expensive compare with other place to visit, Any way it was famous place to visit in Dalat.

We didn't really get the point of crazy house. It was interesting to walk around for 30 mins but there's nothing really there.Certainly didn't blow us away. Felt a little pointless but offered nice views of the city. 50k entrance
The house is certainly interesting, like a fairytale. But it didn't impress me enough to make me want to go back. It's still under construction.
Actually nice.  But now kitch and overdone.  All similar design.   Good to walk around and explore.  Nice for kids.
Construction is in full swing, so there are many dangerous areas.
Everyone perceives everything through their own lens. In my opinion, it's worth seeing just once to appreciate the architect's efforts. Your impression will depend entirely on your imagination.
Cool to see but nothing special. Crowded. More or less a giant playground. The Maze Bar is the same idea, but free to enter.

I can really see the potential in this building but the downer is it’s simply unfinished so when you walk around, half of it is unpainted, unsculpted and still under construction. I feel this place should be shut until finished so when you pay you get a mesmerising experience of truly inspiring architecture instead of a half finished artwork. Definitely interesting in parts but I wish it said it wasn’t complete as I’d have maybe thought twice about going but for the price (50k per person) you ant complain too much. The underwater themed area is impressive and general architecture is also good but no gaudi

There is not heaps to do in Dalat and I visited the house as part of my MTB tour which was a good stop off. Well it is a crazy house and worth the visit, just be patient as you have to wait for all and sundry to take the required amount of selfies. One European even spotted climbing on the roof for that special shot. Yep.Look it is good fun and not expensive so it’s worth the effort.
It was interesting to go through this “house”. Obviously built as something “different” and as a money-maker. I don’t know the admission price but I wouldn’t pay too much.

Indeed, a crazy house. It is advisable to plan especially for moments when the weather is a bit of a problem to go out to the various attractions. Indeed, everyone will be satisfied, children and adults. An interesting, intriguing experience that impresses the viewer. Definitely finish and say that it is completely a crazy house...😂
A cozy place in the middle of the city. There is still construction going on and it can seem very unfinished. It was a decent experience but not something I would bother to see again.

Safety wise it's totally uncool, the railings are made for people under 4 feet tall. It looks like the creator was smoking some good stuff and was inspired by the Sagrada familia in Barcelona. Personally I would call it ugly house. Art is subjective so you may still enjoy. At 50k it's not expensive.
This space is neat, the design intriguing, and the view is excellent. It was a bit too overcrowded and the cost is a bit high for Vietnam. Enjoy it.

There were just too many people... It seems to be a popular tourist spot, and I wouldn't recommend staying here because you might accidentally wander in or be disturbed by other tourists! I thought it was just so-so.
The place is still quite surprising, considering it's a hotel and resembles a Disney set.

We did this because it is strange to see and interesting. It truly is cool. However, they are adding more to the building and they added a shop to buy tourist trap things and the place just doesn't feel very authentic. The other thing I should say is to actually be careful when you're walking up high in the narrow little pathways. There are obviously heaps of tourists and children everywhere and just be careful when you sharing the way. I felt like I was going to fall a few times. The little cafe on the ground floor is quite good though, the strawberry smoothie and the pineapple- caramel pie is really yum.
We were on a tour to Dalat. In my opinion, it was overhyped. There are more interesting places to spend time in Dalat.

The fee to enter this weird place is 50,000 and once inside you can climb thought the endless maze of steps leading through this oddball building. Very interesting to see it and it is in the process of being extended.A very popular tourist destination with various tour groups passing through.
When I was there, there were some reconstruction of the building but I would recommend people to visit this place at least. It’s like a maze and a witch lair, so don’t get lost in it!

So this was a really cool house/hotel that someone obviously spent a lot of artistic thought in designing. But the amount of tourists there was insane and there's no sense of direction from any staff. People were pushing past narrow stairways with no guardrails, people were letting kids run wild and it was downright dangerous. They need to limit how many people are in the attraction at one time, and make everyone else wait their turn. And maybe have someone directing traffic throughout so dumb people don't go the very obvious wrong direction down a 1 way staircase.
A pleasant visit, but I'm not sure it's worth 50,000.

Packed with people taking selfies and pushing over one another this place needs to figure out how to have a better flow of the guests so that it’s enjoyable for everyone rather than feeling cramped and overcapacity.

A good way to kill a couple of hours...if you can get past the Chinese and Russian group tours. It's pretty dangerous in places and won't be long until a serious accident happens. Also, it should be free or reduced entry until it's finished. I'd still say it's worth a visit if you're in DaLat.

This visit was a waste of time. It felt like hiking in a man-made labyrinth. The rooms, which are on themes and supposedly the attraction of this place were almost closed as they had guests. And half of the place was under construction. I wonder why do they sell tickets if there is nothing to see inside.

It was very crowded and moving about on the staircases was difficult. After about 20 minutes of squeezing by visitors at every turn I had seen enough. I might have enjoyed it more if I was 10 years old or so.

The idea is good, and it's clear someone designed it for living in. It would be fantastic for accommodation, but as a tourist attraction, it's rather boring. You might get some decent photos, but otherwise, there's not much to enjoy.
A very interesting but VERY busy attraction. Loads of tourist buses full of Russians and others. Also pretty scary stairs and it’s borderline dangerous in many spots.

It is a truly special experience to visit this place, it can be recommended as a different experience. The most impressive thing about it, in my opinion, is the thought of how much work has gone into creating something so special.

There are many artificial elements, and it's not as lifelike as I imagined; instead, it has a very rough appearance. It looks impressive from a distance, but once you step inside, the concrete feel is very obvious. There are people collecting money at the parking area outside, but they don't seem to be from Crazy House; they don't seem to care if you don't pay.
The girl who created this unusual hotel is anything but normal 😂

This place was interesting to see but could have been anywhere.  Many many tourists, I’m not sure I would like to stay here with hundreds of tourists passing your room all day.  Very interesting and it seems they were working on adding to the building.   Some of the railings seemed very low (mid thigh hight) which seemed to be quite dangerous.  Definitely worth seeing though.

Nothing special, everything is crowded, there are a lot of tourists, especially from the neighboring country ;-)), in principle, you can walk around, from the top point you can partially see the city of Dalat

This place is different n very very crowded.Viewing the structures n designs is pleasant....because of the crowd it was impossible to view everything in detail.The staircases can be precarious.The area is so condensed n limited.The Spanish version (Guadi)is more spectacular.The entrance fee is fair for the construction n maintenance of the  attractive site.

Hmmm a house or area like that never seen before. Thats true, but also never seen so much unfriendly guests there... nearly all of them russians.. 🙈 and also 50’000 Dong entrance fee.. i think for that what you get it’s too much..But interesting architecture for sure.But for overnight stay? NO THANKS.. too much people, too much noise... and too expensive
It's ideal for children. Not suitable for couples, though. The price is reasonable. We were there in the off-season, and there was some construction going on.

The crazy houses had some interesting architecture of course, but some were under renovation and I find there isn't much to see for the entry fee demanded. I still think it's worth it if you have the time and money.

Place really interisting. Have sense to visit. One time. A little bit simmilar like Gaudy projects , sometimes like Disney Land.  But all of Our group had headache in this place. Bad aura. Take power from You.
It is a nice visit... they were still doing new construction while we were there. We can view the room but you cant go in... interesting visit
It's a nice place, but if you already visited Gaudi and Hundertwasser designed buildings, you wouldn't be excited.

For someone who is not an architecture fan, it is simply such a special house, an interesting climbing wall. The information in a few sentences tells you about the place and the author. At the time of the visit, it was being expanded and built. There you can sit and have a coffee, buy overpriced souvenirs or stay.
This is definitely a tourist spot. It's worth a 15 minute visit, but little more. Construction is ongoing but did not take away from the experience. Take a few pics, venture around, and then move on
An interesting house to see, since already in Dalat. But maybe there are better free things elsewhere. Some parts were still under construction.

I went to visit the house with the sunset. It was not illuminated and same parts of the house were a bit dangerous at night, specially for kids. You could fall down if you stumble with the stairs. It´s not expensive, though. If you had something more interesting to visit in Da Lat I would go first. It is not as bad as it may sound, it´s just that it does not have anything special.

Something different Lovely lady just outside selling coconut water whisk was really great as ice cold House is teeming with Japanese tourists taking photos Not sure I would want to stay there but worth a look from an architectural point of view Only need about 20 mins to look at this
For those who appreciate unconventional architecture, this is a very original building.
I've stayed at this hotel before.
It's nothing special but if you have time or your tour includes the attraction it's worth a visit. I believe the guest house is worth a stay.

We went and strolled around this crazy house with some pretty cool artistic styles going on.. Unfortunately alot of the place in undergoing construction and it looks like its going to be for a long time. Admission entry was 50,000vnd each for adults. Would spend at most an hour there, if you have nothing else to do, check it out.
With its distinctive design featuring muted, dark colors and quirky textures, it's perfect for those who love exploration and adventure.

The building is worth a look; it strongly resembles buildings by Hundertwasser. I think it would be interesting as a hotel, as the rooms are already interestingly designed. On the other hand, the large number of visitors would bother me somewhat.
Quite an interesting space to explore but after a while it's all the same. 45mins - 1 hour is sufficient here
Interesting enough but nothing special; wouldn’t go to special measures to see it. 50,000VND to get in, bike parking outside for 5,000VND.

You can visit it, but it's not exactly a blast. Reasons: some railings are loose, part of one has broken off, and there are concerns about the structural load-bearing capacity. It takes 30-45 minutes to walk around. You should arrive during daylight hours. It's already dark at 5 PM, and there's no lighting.

We visited just before it started to get dark.  We didn't get to explore much before it got dark. We couldn't see much as we were walking around. I know I would have enjoyed it a lot more if I had visited during day time. It does stay open until 7pm so they should have lights around, maybe some people prefer it this way? It is a cool and quirky hotel, just visit during daylight. A lot of construction work was going on when we went as well.

It's not for everyone, especially if you're with children. You can see it once, but I wouldn't stay in this hotel; it's full of excursions and the small rooms are oppressive because the walls are so small.

Frankly, I don't regret satisfying my curiosity, and just as frankly, I won't be going back. Depending on one's sensibilities or cultural interests, you either like it or you don't. Nothing to rave about, but no contempt either for this ludicofantasticorocamboroccoco approach... I was lucky; there weren't many people, and the weather was quite nice. It's already magical to be in Vietnam, so in short, we wander through the architect's twisted mind. Why not?

It was fun to walk around and look around even though some parts are still under construction. Not entirely safe for small children so you have to walk together at all times. Had a nice juice in the garden which was nice.

It's just average! Unless you're going as a couple specifically to take photos, I don't think it will be very impressive. I went by bicycle, and when I looked around for parking, I noticed someone nearby parking for 2,000 VND! Just so you know!
The exterior was well-decorated, and the interior seems likely to improve as additional renovations are underway.

In short, it's a maze, a very complex maze. It would take about an hour and a half to two hours to see it properly, so don't come too late, otherwise you won't be able to navigate the maze properly after dark.

When we arrived, the site was under construction.. 1.10 detracts from the experience.. Overall, a nice place, but it's better to arrive when it's not under construction... Admission costs 50,000 per person.
The place dont look crazy. But quite Unique. Strange house of animal and human living together .Small stair going around the house. More of a Fantasy house.

The place is a lovely concept; it features stairs and steps of various shapes, and also has rooms for rent. I saw tourists renting rooms in the crazy house, so be careful while exploring and don't open the room doors, as there are tenants. There are some repairs and renovations underway in parts of the house.
We are on our honeymoon and treated ourselves to a room in crazy hotel but it wasn't worth the month - worth a visit but wouldn't recommend stayingSorry feels harsh as staff was awesome 👍🏼

This place has the potential to be really great and it certainly is for an hour or so. You enter and there is so much to look at and different walkways to explore and little surprises to find. When you climb to the top of the structure, the views are pretty great. The architect of the place has an interesting story and if you are in Da Lat you must visit.BUT, its under construction at the moment so you can't see all the sections. When I was there, busloads of Russian tourists inundated the place and behaved as if they were the only people there, pushing you out of their way and yelling if you were in a place they happened to want to take a photo of. People did photo shoots for 5 minutes in one location, which would be OK if it wasn't the only way to pass on a narrow high up walkway. The level of congestion made it hard to enjoy in its entirety.Still, its worth a visit. Just don't expect it to be relaxing.

First off it is 50000 vnd to enter, so not even 2 gbp  (bargain). Initially, it was like stepping inside Alice in Wonderland/ The Labyrinth. The lady who designed this place has a real creative flair about her. I have never seen anything like it and for this reason it is worth a visit, although don't plan a full day trip here as you are in and out within about 30 minutes. I won't give too much away but if you do go make sure you see the 3d under water style cafe/ dance hall. When we went in we were informed we could book one of the rooms and stay there if we wanted to. They range from  $35-$100 a night. I don't know about anyone else but I certainly wouldn't want to be woken up to countless numbers of tourists peering through my window taking pictures at 8am in the morning!
Funny house. Nice to see once. Quite touristy though. There is a lovely and trendy little restaurant across the street.

It was a must-see if you're in Dalat, but I think it's worth it. It looks a bit like a surreal playground, a mix of different styles, but it's fun. Plus, it's a hotel, so spending a night there isn't a bad idea. By the way, they're still expanding it, and the new area looks promising.
Too expensive for what is actually is. Its interesting but thats all, only a building with plenty of people doing photos
It is just like the photos, but I left wondering why someone would want to build a 3-dimensional maze and call it a house.  Children probably enjoy running up and down the steps.

Crazy House is one of the Top Sights in Da Lat. It's like entering Disneyland or another little Fanatsy World. The Architecture is weird and you'll find yourself walking in cicrles and up and down over rooftops and through windows. Unfortunately it was a rainy day and the view over the city wasn't that good as usual. Furthermore everything was wet outside. It is a fun place but it was very touristic and crowded. There were lots of small paths and steps where I was quequing behind lots of people and had to wait to get past them, because they stopped for a photo. I went there on my own and left after an hour. It would have been a better experience with friends or Kids.

We were amazed at the unique design and architecture of this house which adds to the beauty of Da Lat. Got to say that we could not find the same architecture in anywhere else. The crazy house also offers room rental for those who wish to experience the building. However the room is pretty old and I believe the whole facility has to be renovated after years of opening to the public.
Worth a visit but nothing that impressive compared to other attractions that Da Lat has to offer. Just a funny looking hotel.
That is just fantastic and artifical place. Stairs are nerrow and complicated. I felt that is not safety. But Children are very happy.
The building is really beautiful but it is not a must-see attraction.

This place was advertised much more pompously than it actually was. I was there once with my boyfriend and was completely unimpressed. Yes, it's unusual, but it's probably not worth wasting your time on. Dalat is an incredible city, with many places worth visiting, but I wouldn't count Crazy House among them.
Artistic designed! But don't come on holiday entrance fee is higher than regular days.It's like kiddie adventure.

We treated ourselves and stayed in the eagle room for a night in Da Lat. The place is amazing and the craziness of the hotel does not disappoint. However at 8am the construction work started which the hotel did not warn us about it honestly sounded like someone was drilling into our wall. It was so loud I couldn't even hear my boyfriend talking.
