Crazy House: A Surreal Walk Through a Living Art Piece
Calling it a "hotel" feels like an understatement. Walking through the Crazy House (Hằng Nga Guesthouse) feels less like checking into accommodation and more like stepping inside a Salvador Dalí painting or a whimsical, organic dreamscape.
The Experience: Why It’s Famous
The Architecture: Designed by architect Đặng Việt Nga, there are almost no right angles here. It’s all about flowing, organic curves, tree-stump structures, and towering "cobweb" bridges. It’s an architectural marvel that feels like a giant, concrete treehouse.
The "Explore" Factor: It is a maze. You will find yourself climbing narrow, winding staircases that lead to unexpected rooftops, secret gardens, and viewpoints overlooking Da Lat. Every turn offers a new, bizarre detail—it’s genuinely fun to just wander.
The Themed Rooms: If you stay here, you’re not just getting a bed; you’re sleeping in a giant concrete gourd, a bear’s den, or an eagle’s nest. It’s a bold, immersive choice.
The "Real Talk" (What the Guidebooks Might Not Say)
The Privacy Paradox: This is a working hotel and a top tourist attraction. If you stay here as a guest, be prepared for visitors to be walking right past your window or peering into your common areas during the day. It’s definitely not a "secluded romantic getaway" during daylight hours.
It’s Not for Everyone (Mobility-Wise): This is not an accessible building. The stairs are steep, narrow, and often uneven. If you have any mobility issues or are carrying heavy luggage, you might find the "adventure" to be more of a workout than you bargained for.
It’s a "Work in Progress": The architect is still adding to it. You will likely see scaffolding or ongoing construction during your visit. It’s part of the charm, but it means parts of the "art" are always being refined.
Timing Matters: It is a major tourist magnet. If you show up in the middle of the day with the tour groups, it can feel cramped. Go as early as possible to actually enjoy the atmosphere without being jostled on the bridges.
My Verdict
Is it worth it? Yes.
It’s not a polished, luxury resort, and it doesn't pretend to be. It’s a creative, bold, and slightly chaotic labor of love. Even if you don’t stay the night, it’s worth the entrance ticket just to say you’ve walked through one of the most unique buildings in the world. It’s a must-do for anyone who appreciates weird, wonderful, and imaginative design.

I stayed overnight in one of the hotel rooms  — and that completely changed the experience.
At night, when the crowds leave, Crazy House becomes quiet, mysterious, and almost dreamlike. Corridors twist, staircases curve unexpectedly, and every window looks like it belongs to a fairy tale… or a dream you’re not sure you’re awake from.
 A Place You Keep Discovering Again
Even on my second visit, I kept finding new details:
Hidden stairways
Sculptures I missed before
New angles, new feelings
Crazy House is not meant to be “understood” in one visit.
It’s meant to be rediscovered.
 The Vision Behind Crazy House
What makes this place truly special is its creator — Dang Viet Nga, a Vietnamese architect who dared to imagine architecture without straight lines, without rules, without limits.
Inspired by nature, dreams, and surrealism, she created a space that feels alive.
And what makes me especially happy: the Crazy House is still beautifully maintained, full of warmth, creativity, and a welcoming atmosphere.
This isn’t an abandoned experiment.
It’s a living, breathing piece of art.
✨ Why Staying Overnight Is Worth It
If you’re considering it — do it.
Staying inside Crazy House allows you to:
Experience it without crowds
Feel its atmosphere after dark
Wake up inside a piece of art
It’s not just accommodation.
It’s an experience.

studied in Moscow from 1959 to 1972, at the Moscow Architectural Institute (MARCHI) and in graduate school there, where she defended her PhD thesis in architecture.

Crazy House – Da Lat, Vietnam
My visit to Crazy House in Da Lat was one of the most memorable travel experiences I’ve ever had! This incredible architectural masterpiece feels like stepping into a fairy-tale world — its winding staircases, organic shapes, and surreal design make every corner a surprise. The buildings resemble something out of a dream, with curves and forms inspired by nature, creating an atmosphere that is both whimsical and inspiring.  ￼

Walking through its labyrinth-like corridors, I was constantly amazed by the creativity and imagination behind every detail. It’s more than just a building — it’s a living piece of art. Whether you’re exploring as a guest, staying overnight in one of the themed rooms, or simply taking photos, Crazy House offers a unique blend of artistic expression, fun, and wonder that you won’t find anywhere else.  ￼

The staff were welcoming and helpful, and I loved discovering new surprises around every corner. It’s truly a must-visit when in Da Lat, and I’ll definitely carry the memories of this magical place with me for years to come!

The first impression is like stepping into a portal that transports you to an inside-out world. It's like falling down a rabbit hole, where everything is upside down and inside out. It's either a fairytale forest or a group hallucination, a collective madness. I've never seen anything like it, even though I've been actively exploring the sites built by Gaudí and Montaner over the past couple of years, and they were quite the entertainers too.

In short, if you like this kind of thing, you'll be walking around with your head up and your jaw hanging open. Endless passages from building to building, up and down, zigzagging and twisting. Fantastic plants, vines, burrows, and aerial staircases. And despite the fact that most of the buildings, including the decor, are made of concrete, it feels like you're in a natural setting, built into the mountains and cliffs, entwined with wild vegetation. There are also living plants in the courtyards, by the way. It's sometimes hard to tell where the entrance and exit are. One corridor gives way to another. Climb the airy staircase and you'll see Dalat immersed in flowers and greenery, and then, turning the corner, you'll find yourself on the other side of the building, with creepy claws on the walls. Walking around there is pure pleasure for crazy travelers like us.

And what's more, it's also a hotel. This time, our trip was spontaneous, and we couldn't stay there, but next time I return, I'll definitely make sure to book in advance to stay there. I'm sure that at dusk, when the crowds of tourists have ebbed away and the mysterious house is ablaze with lights and shrouded in mysterious sounds, it will be extremely interesting there. Although it might be a little creepy to spend the night there, it's definitely worth it ☝️

Hằng Nga Villa, also known as the Crazy House, is one of the most unique buildings in Da Lat, a place where the nickname "Strange Building" is by no means an exaggeration. Designed by architect Dang Viet Nga, this building is a kind of "Da Lat version of Gaudí style" architecture that combines nature, surrealism, and organic curves, giving the impression of a blend of a quirky forest mansion and a labyrinthine fantasy space.

Upon entering, one is immediately drawn to the staircases and windows intertwined like giant tree roots, the slanted rooms, and the animal-shaped guest rooms. The circulation and structure are so free and experimental that it is momentarily confusing to distinguish between walls and ceilings. As you cross narrow bridges and climb the stairs, you discover hidden observation decks and rooms throughout; the view from the upper observation deck was particularly impressive, offering a panoramic view of downtown Da Lat beyond the building's organic curves.

It is a space that is better described as "interesting" rather than "pretty." It was not just a simple tourist attraction, but a case where the architecture itself became a work of art and a theme park; it was Da Lat's most unique attraction, worth experiencing firsthand at least once during your trip.

This is incredible!
I recommend visiting it, or better yet, staying overnight there so you can stroll around the entire grounds before 8:30 in the morning.
This is a work of art, and I realized that nothing is impossible. Thanks to the owner, who is also an architect!
Only visited and it was lovely. Not sure id stay here with so many tourists going through

This place was one on my favorite spot in Vietnam, clearly they have a culture of sculptures (by the religion, art,etc) and this place I found it really unique.
Be prepared to walk a lot and has so many stairs, so we spent a lot of time there.
Even was turistic it felt really unique and was so cool the arquitect was a woman :)
My friend and I enjoyed a lot our time there.
This is a must if you’re in Dalat !
Probably I’ll get super early to avoid a large crowd.
The only downside the staff were quite dismissive, you get use to the good hospitality in general in Vietnam and this place seems they are tired of tourist people, but we will give 5 stars anyway.

The house is so big, you should definitely set aside plenty of time. This stop was truly a highlight of our trip.

A must-see!!

The name is on the lid, not the whole room, but ALL HOUSE WAS CRAZY!!!

Amazing architecture done here and its a great experience to be able to wake up first thing in the morning and experience this while the sun is rising, without the crowd of tourists that arrive during the day.

All staff were amazingly friendly/helpful/loved a chat. The only downside I can say is that the area gets swarmed by tourists during the day, but if you get out and explore the surroundings, it wont be an issue for you at all! 10/10.
The site visit is extraordinary; it's completely offbeat!
Exciting
It is a place that children will like. The prices are not cheap, but it is definitely worth visiting.

A crazy house designed by the president's daughter
Every corner has a surprise
Stairs that go up with strange leaves
Cool and beautiful place
That maybe tells you that it can be designed differently
With a lot of imagination
We didn't smoke in the place, just visited

Some salesperson are rude and disrespect Asian customers. Especially one of the postcard sales who wearing glasses. She was Very impatiently moving the items around when we were choose the stamps and even asked us in Mandarin ““You buy or not ？！” ”Later she even took entire basket of items from a Korea girl who was picking postcards inside the counter. It was really rude and unprofessional. After we bought two sets of stamps She laughed with other staff that Asians are poor and can only afford two. The service was incredible.
Nothing special.The building is full of stalls trying to sell you overpriced junk. Not worth going unless you're already passing by.

I hailed a green taxi in front of the post office. The driver, who looked to be around 30, and we agreed on a price of 51,000. Unexpectedly, after turning just one corner, he asked if we had any small bills, saying he didn't accept large bills. I said I didn't have any large bills, and then took out a 50,000 bill. Then, for some reason, he acted as if he didn't understand what I was saying, and then, pointing to a sign on the back window, he reached out and tried to take the bill from my hand when we turned around. My friend, for some reason, told me to show him the money so he could see if he had any smaller bills. Then, the driver actually took all the money, pretending to check for smaller bills, then gave it back to me, and told us to get out, saying he couldn't take us because we didn't have any smaller bills. I felt something was off, but I couldn't quite put my finger on it, because we had been watching his hand the whole time, worried something was wrong. But after getting out of the taxi, I still felt uneasy. Ignoring the risk of being robbed, I sat down on the roadside and counted the money. Sure enough, five 500,000 VND bills were missing, totaling 2.5 million VND. It was truly appalling. Our local friend said calling the police was useless, especially since we hadn't noted the license plate number. I'm writing this not to seek sympathy, and please don't leave comments calling me stupid; I just want to warn everyone that while taxis are cheap in Vietnam, there are quite a few unscrupulous drivers.
My 4-year-old daughter got fussy because she was sleepy, and a security guard immediately forced us to leave.
Good afternoon!

Interesting for those who like architecture. Aside from that, there's nothing particularly special about this visit. The interesting thing is that it's located right in front of the cathedral, another tourist attraction in the city.
I don't think there was anything special to see. Luckily we were just passing by so we stopped but after only a minute or so we moved on.

There are 2 souvenir shops inside that sell the same stuff as every where else. The building isn’t that impressive if you’ve travelled a lot and not the nicest building in the city, in comparison to the temples. The notre dame next door is not special either especially if you’ve been to the real thing in France! There is a nice cafe next door however for when you’ve done the 2 minute glance around the post office.
For Asians who live in big cities and only know concrete and skyscrapers, it might be worth a visit, but absolutely nothing about this place impressed me.
Take a photo outside and walk on as nothing but cheap tourist tat.  There is a lovely side street next to the GPO with nice book & coffee shops in a shaded alley to rest wary feet.
I can't really find a reason to visit the place again, let alone recommend it to all other visitors. Local post office, go there to buy stamps.
The functional post office sounded like little tacky from the inside with so many souvenir shops. The architecture itself was OK.

Only visit this building if you like architecture.  It is still a working post office, but basically has just been turned into a souvenir shop.  The building in nice but there is so much more to see in Ho Chi Minh City I would not make a special trip to see it.  The tour we were on visited it, I think more because it is next to the Notre Dame Cathedral.  However because the cathedral is closed for renovations it was a waste of an hour of my time.
There is nothing to see ther a tourist trap, but hey it is free, so to go in for a few minutes in centran Ho Chi Min is not a waste, you will walk by it for sure. Just dont expect much
This place is advertised as the main attraction, but we found it completely boring. Beautiful architecture, but uninteresting to us.
A large central hall with counters around the perimiter. Not much else to say really. Pop in if youre passing but dont make a special trip.

A place that didn't give me anything special. Quite a few people who wanted to post their cards. A place that wasn't anything special either. There's so much more to see when you just walk around town and look
Personally not impressed with the central post office .It is just across the street from Notre-Dame , I just stepped in given the proximity !
It doesn't amount to much to me. It's just that it was included in the tour. The inside is mainly a souvenir shop.
Saigon's central post office is magnificent, but why has it been transformed into a souvenir market?

A good spot for taking photos; old French-style architecture, just look around and that's about it. Lots of tourists buying stamps and postcards. For those with a nostalgic heart, it might evoke a sense of nostalgia, but it's just so-so.
I think it might have been different if I had gone knowing more about the architecture or information, but
I don’t understand why it’s in the guide books. Nothing special. It’s a post office that looks like a market.
We simply wanted to put the letter we were sending in a waterproof envelope. This was refused rudely, even though we had always been able to do this at other post offices in Vietnam.
Fancy looking post office with items for sale but nothing special. It was super clean and huge for a post office but that’s about it.
There's nothing to see or do for tourism, and vendors in front are aggressively trying to lure foreign tourists; it's unsafe to take photos.
Just another tourist trap with over priced souvenirs. Its very boring and there’s nothing to do. It’s not worth visiting.
This building is unsophisticated and not well maintained. No idea why anyone would think this is a key attraction for HCM.
Unfortunately this attraction was a big lemon. Not much to offer aside from the architecture but tacky tourist shop and too many people

Not much going on there, we had nothing to post and nothing to buy, boring sightseeing places. There is a money exchange store at the right side of the entrance which was suggested by some tourists that they offer great rate, but the reality is low rate, less type of currency exchange, for example: they don't change Taiwanese money at all, plus they charge 1%commission without warning you before they take your money. They just deducted and gave you the rest! I have been to few countries in South East Asia, even my own country in Australia, I was never charged commission from an exchange store (excluded bank). Airport offers the best rate so far during my trip.
What exactly is there to see here? Well, it's a post office. With souvenir vendors. Wow. I have no idea why this is supposed to be a tourist attraction.
I actually wouldn't recommend visiting this place, it's always mentioned in tours but honestly there's nothing really special to see.
I went there to get a feel for the atmosphere.
Charming building located in the centre of district 1. You can post letters and parcels. Vietnam’s post has reduced this place to a poor excuse to sell useless souvenirs.

Visited the post office w my family. Nice architecture n fun for the children posting the post card. **warning** we boarded a taxi waiting outside n driver claimed meter not working n demanded to pay cash 1st even before set off. We obliged but he keep trying to tell me 25000vnd is 250000vnd. Obviously we did not pay n move to the next accomplice taxi. This round he stopped about 100meteres on receiving a call n claimed car spoilt n wanted us pay but we denied n get out. Soon after....he drove off. Writing this to warn other tourist. Vietnam people are generally nice so during our visited.

Hello?! This is a post office! In the digital age of WhatsApp and email, taking a tourist trap to a post office with physical postcards/stamps and old phone booths converted into ATMs is breathtakingly (no air conditioning, very hot and stuffy) boring. No need to do it.
I do not recommend it . You'd be better off shopping or
The building is rather spectacular but not worth a visit on its own, in my opinion. If part of multi place tour, then fine.
I visited this building because it is mentioned in all the guides and blogs I read...well what can I say, even if it is dated, it is still ONLY a post office....

So, on the way, we enter....not really interesting or impressive...because it's mentioned in the guide, so lots of people stand and take pictures by it. (By the way, the post office in Vietnam is considered a particularly bad thing.)
Recommended everywhere, but when we first arrived, apart from the side mosaics and the English telephone booths, there was nothing special. What a shame.
I dont get why this is a tourist attraction.  Its just an old post office with nothing to see inside except hordes of tourists. Very boring.

People raved about this building, but after having visited I can't really understand what the fuss is about. It's a pretty basic building, some decorative detailing outside, but not anything to warrant a separate trip.
Well, it is a post office, period. Actually, there is nothing more to say except I have to fill 100 signs here.

The post office itself is nothing special. I was abit annoyed about what happened when I was purchasing souvenirs at the main store (right in the middle inside the post office). I gave the girl 500k dong, think I spent 230k, she only gave me back 70.000 change. I enquired straight away about the rest of the change. She put up a puzzle face but didn't ask much and just gave me 200k. Just check the change before you leave.
Boring, souvenirs are very expensive. There is Nothing to see. Exhanges are very bad. Very crowded with a lot of studends
I agree that this post offce has a certain historical value. However what it is today is just a tourist trap.
I'm still waiting to be impressed. This is just an old building with a good paint job. It's not a must see piece of architecture.
This place is overated. There is a lot of peddlers outside asking you to buy stamps, and souvenir shops inside which are small and crammed.
Other than some information on their history this place was rather dull. I am sure there are other interesting places I could have gone to

While i have been here twice, i assure you it was not for any level of fun. I simply used it as a functioning post office. But hey, if you get aroused by incoming and outgoing mail then by all means check out the post office. It's great for those who live here and trying to conduct business while tourist are standing around taking photos.

Literally spent about 5 minutes here. It's actually just a post office. I wouldn't go out of your way to visit it, but if you're in the vicinity have a look inside. Good place if you want to post goods/exchange currency.
Well worth a visit, plus it's close to everywhere in district 1. Lots of character & a bit of a timewarp.
It's a waste of time.... It's a building where Saigon's main post office is located...

I visited the Post Office, but for the life of me I can not even understand why is is thought to be something someone needs to see. While there is no charge to take a stroll through the place I can not see why someone would want to unless you just need to use one of their services.
That's all it is a working post office the building is OK as well not anything massively attractive spent two minutes here tops

As we were exploring the central part of the city with our guide, we ended up at this place. I really don't understand what the point of this visit was; there was absolutely nothing to see. Cancel this visit.
Just another building, lot of people, and always people trying to sell you something. Some of the architecture was interesting but not my cup of tea.
Ok to see, but that's all... Nice that its still operating. Don't expect much to see, combine it with the church and/or opera house.
I posted some postcards back hom and never made it.  Wouldn't waste your time.  Besides they go through the mail u post

Not worth your time visiting. Just a building where they do business, sending money, letters etc. just a big postal building. But you can see it and the church next to it. Maybe just drive by or stay for 5 minutes

After reading that this was one of the top things to do and see in the city my friend and I walked the half an hour to the post office and got drenched on our way.I can honestly say that once there we were disappointed we'd taken the time to go there.The previous photos we'd seen definitely sold it well, but in real life we felt it was nothing to write home about - we didn't even take any photographs. If I'm honest I don't know why it was listed as an attraction.I'd have been even more disappointed if we'd visited this as part of a tour we'd paid for.

There was nothing impressive about this building. The interior was run down not impressive. It's good enough to pass and look from the outside. Inside, there are a lot of crowded vendor stalls selling souvenirs and not enough room for people to walk around freely
So beautiful.  Still a working post office.  Posted myself a post card but still waiting after 2 weeks for it to arrive home.
Ok historical architecture but inside it is a larger scale souvenir shop opposite of the Notre Dame church.  Do iris you must as little else to do in this City.
historic decor, with numerous souvenir shops. they still have the little cubicles that used to house the public telephones. they are now converted to bank ATMs.
No idea whatsoever how this makes trip advisor or lonely planets to do list, it's a post office! Reasonably nice exterior at best, feel free to use the postal service too, it might work.
It's a post office, useful for sending mail. Gift shops and McDonald's are attached to it. The architecture is quite interesting to see, but it's a functional post office useful for its services.
It's located next to a church, was bustling, and the building was lovely. I only looked at it from the outside and didn't go inside. I recommend it because they sell commemorative stamps.
If you have no business to be there then just stay away or if you want to visit the place it is ok but don't ask staffs thats working there for any information, all you will get is a rude response.
No one even uses Post offices anymore so you can get a look at the architecture from a passing taxi. Don't waste your time going inside.
Nothing special here, just only take a picture for the outlook of building. Opposite with Dame. Beside the post office has a book street, very good environment.
It's not particularly interesting; there's no discernible value, neither architectural nor artistic. The Madrid Post Office is far superior.
Just popped in to see what it was like. Not sure what the fuss is about. It's only a post office. If you have a spare 5 minutes and are in the area otherwise not worth going out of your way to visit.

Don't allow much time for this you literally pop your head in take a photo or 2 then your out it does have a few shops you can look at but they are just the same old market goods you can get around the corner
Its a post office. People post stuff. End of story. Theyve even gone and attached a mcdonalds to the side of it.
Very ho hum. Nothing special except it's dated interior.  Near cathedral and and new upmarket shopping centre.
If this is among the best of the Saigon, the city is not worth the visit. The architecture is not very outstanding or fascinating. In addition, the staff is very unfriendly.
It is a nice building and all but that is it. Unless you are very mich into architecture and the history of  the building.
This is a nice building but it is a post office. If I was walking around the city I would stop to look but i definitely wouldn't go on an organised trip.

Oh my God, a post office. Completely oversized, completely overrated. It's enough to put you off just looking at the shops in the immediate vicinity (Starbucks, etc.), but I don't understand why anyone would go here for sightseeing. It's perfectly fine to buy your stamps here (13,500 dong for an air postcard to Europe), look up, let your gaze wander, and tick it off your list. But to make a trip here as a "must-see," as recommended in practically every guidebook: No, thank you. Notre Dame Cathedral is right next door, by the way, so take a look at that too and then get back to the finer things in life ;)
It's a large post office, nothing too spectacular. I guess if your maybe into your architecture go see it but for me it just wasn't very interesting. Its a post office for crying out loud.

No one able to provide any correct information at the international office !The deeper you have to enter in that Buildind, the deeper they will try to enter your wallet ! No official price, no garanty for any "service"...I will let experience talk :First shipment : arrived 3 months after expected, broken.Second : arrived and got back to sender because NOTHING in the tracking system was respected.Third (express): arrived 3 weeks after expected, broken.Fourth shipment attempt : Item send from China to Tokyo in two weeks for 20 $ max,from Tokyo to hcmc in 3 days for less that 60 $ with EMSsame item from hcmc to France, still with EMS : - We can not send this - Ok we can but go buy a bigger carton box to wrap it. (I did)- We can not send it anymore, it's over sized with the box- Ok we can send it for over 760 $ with the same EMSConsidering the trash they identify as a post service, how do they manage to be almost15 TIMES MORE EXPENSIVE than PERFECT JAPANESE Services ???I didn't complain in any way for years, thinking I had to nderstand...I understood.I will keep insults away from that review as a respect for readers and the website.The building ?  " lol "Is that the best western influence could do ??

Ok I will acknowledge that it is a large building and is probably prettier and better made than most in Vietnam, but it is just a post office that sells tourist junk.  Not a lot to see inside  wouldn't make a special trip unless you're a postal nut.  No entry fee . Right beside the cathedral.
I expected something more impressive, but this is really just a big hall with higher-than-normal ceiling height. Look in if you're passing by anyway, otherwise forget about it.

Not sure why it's a tourist destination.  Lot of tacky shops and crowds of tourists looking upwards. If you don't stumble onto it by accident there are probably better uses for the five minutes this takes to look at
There's nothing much to see, it's just a regular post office where you can buy postcards and send it to whomever you want with a souvenir shop on the side.
Although being free, the only interesting part was the architecture, which even then was not that exciting, if your around visit, if not i wouldn't bother

Yes the architecture is splendid and it is across from the cathedral as well as a plethora of Western fast food restaurants. Go of you need stamps. There are far more stunning edifices in this historic city.
It's a normal, active post office. I don't need to go to Vietnam to visit one – Bremen, for example, has one too.
Really just a post office with a bit of granduer .Watch out for stamp sellersjust go into postoffice yourself.

Whilst this is quite a beautiful building this could be any random building in major European city. It's got no particular history except the franchise built it. It's got a a few tacky souvenirs shops. Don't believe the hype.
The building itself is beautiful, but inside only one thing matters: selling souvenirs, selling souvenirs, selling souvenirs.

Very boring and run down post office,  a few small tourist shops inside with very expensive prices, money exchange shop located inside also trying to rip you off, pictures outside are nice to take if you like many tourists in your pictures

We went here only because we'd been to Notre Dame and it was just over the road. There isn't much to see though really, it does have the old feel and nice architecture inside with all the post office booths, but other than that it's full of touts outside trying to sell the same tourist junk that you see all over. I wouldn't recommend going out of your way to see this.
I do not under stand why it is a tourist attraction. Nothing to see. It is old and hot inside. Why would a post office be a tourist hot spot?
We went to see it because it's next to Notre Dame Cathedral. There's simply nothing to see.

So we were heading to a tour and needed to change so more USA, we found this place just inside the front door of the post office building... However the exchange rates given was terrible (a load less than the hotels had been) when challenged he increase by a very small number and said it was commission... It was over 5 USD lower than anywhere else...Don't use, there are better money change rates in HSBC which is over the road (looking away from the post office to your left) or even down near the opera house).
There really isn't anything to see here. It's painted yellow and look cool from the outside but ultimately it's a post office. Do you really want to visit a post office? Very underwhelming
Not very interesting at all. I walked in, snapped a few photographs, and walked out. There are better sites than this one.
Wouldn't have mattered to me whether we came here or not bit guess it's a good idea to have a peak inside while checking out notra Dame Cathedral.
This is absolutly one of the pathetic places i spent my time to be. nothing to see. waste of time. It close to few other places of interest but nothing to put in the list of place of interest.

We decided on our day off ship in Vietnam to explore Ho Chi Minh City alone!  OMG never seen so many scooters weaving in and out of traffic and pedestrians, oil spilling everywhere and fumes to make you choke.  We found the City depressing and were very aware of the poverty so held on tight to our bags and wallets (we had received a lecture on board the cruise ship warning us of pickpockets).  After walking and seeing very little, we asked various people the way to the museum but nobody either did not speak English or thought we were American and would not acknowledge us, we found the beautiful building of the old Colonial style Post Office!  Wow just like we had them many years ago, beautiful wooden clocks adorned the walls indicating the time zones around the world.  Little booths housed helpful cashiers, there were tasteful reasonably priced gifts, no haggling, and what a relief, air conditioning!  Just before entering we were giving up the will to live, never again do we want to set foot in such a city, big disappointment but had wanted to go as I am a big fan of the musical Miss Saigon!  It was a city of contrasts, one minute the locals sat with primus stoves and filthy woks cooking lunch then there would appear a modern shopping mall with very expensive shops and eateries.  Our advice, don't bother with Ho Chi Minh!
check out the cars in the carpark..... post office workers get very good salaries here!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

like a lot of buliding it is nice, but that is it. Al guided tours take you by the post office and everyone go in. For what? You can bye suvenirs and postvards inside. But they dont have anny postcards whit pictures of the nr 1 attraction in Ho Chi Minh. The tunnels.

I was writing out postcards when some other 'tourists' came over and 'pretended' to have their photo taken with me. I thought it was bizarre then another couple came up and did the same thing. The first ones must have been opening my bag.. The second ones stole my purse. Beware. Beware..beware..!!!!!!!

From an architectural point of view, the building is pleasant enough, but in my opinion, has been ruined by layers of horrible peach colored paint.  The once magnificent building now houses same old tourist shops, selling same old plastic junk.  I would only come here again to post a letter.
Cozy, but totally uninteresting. Nice building, fun contrast between the French colonialist building and Ho Chi Minh on the end wall. But otherwise - hm

This is a nice looking building but there is nothing special about it, except some history you read in the Lonely Planet guide. If you're from Europe you've seen better architecture. There just seems to be crowds around it which attracts people. It's nothing to make a special trip for, but if you're in the area pop in.
This is an attraction? It's nice enough but don't go out of your way to visit, there's nothing interesting going on here
An ordinarily Railway Station Basic and not an architectural Masterpiece. Traffic in and outside the station is interesting to watch.

The Old Post Office is one attraction in a full day of touring. I just couldn't let it go. The building is impressive in architecture and the conversion of the phone boxes into ATM's is clever. The one thing I must say that I was glad to see the Post Office workers of the world have one thing in common...they hate thier job! Asking for stamps to send the old fashioned Post Card home was met with a grimace that is universal. I should have asked for the workers right arm to be severed, it would have been less painful for her. Oh, and yes, I sent the post cards and only 2 of 8 arrived at their destination!
You can visit this place in 5 minutes and get a feel for it, pretty lame. Check out the plaques on the columns outside, Descartes and Franklin get their due.
Located close to the R.C.Cathedral and not too far from the Renaissance Hotel. It is quite different to the Post Offices here in Australia .
Fun to poke your head inside, but no reason to go out of your way.

A beautiful building from the colonial era, with a spacious hall, high windows, and a beautiful floor. It still functions as a post office, but the souvenir shops have encroached upon the building and occupy a central place. Culture has to make way for commerce. Sad.
Building is old but it has been revamped internally and has lost its historical value. You will spend less than an hour here.
It was ok, very busy when we went and chaotic. The huge photo of Ho CHi Min and the old Vietnam maps make it interesting. MacDonalds is also right outside too 😀
We walked past the front and went in. And it's literally just a post office. Nothing remarkable about it.
Nothing special not sure why this is a turist destination. Just a post office like any other you have in your town.  They have gift shop inside....
This place is part of most City Tours. I did not find anything interesting here...just felt it was a time waste...however I did see a beautiful wedding photo shoot here! :)

Well the building looked vintage from the front, but on the inside, i didn't know what to do, there's nothing to see.Just an ordinary post office with vintage building.They also selling souvenirs, but the price were so high.I only go in because i was visiting the cathedral and it was nearby the cathedral.But really, in my opinion, just take photo from outside.Don't bother going in, coz it's just a waste of time.

It's an old building - still in use as a post office. Worth popping your head inside and looking at it for a few minutes from inside. You're likely to cross it at some point if you keep walking through district one. Some interesting maps of French Indochina painted on the walls/ceilings inside. Having said that, nothing spectacular.

The post office is classic french architecture from the early 20th century. It is still a functional post office  and u can buy post cards and other  souvenirs there.To me the best part was the line up of old fashion telephone booths that look like they came out of an old Jimmy Cagney movie.Since it is a tourist attraction, there are  many street vendors around and you can pick up some nice inexpensive souvenirs there.
Packed building with stalls inside selling stuff.Go and see it take a few snaps of the inside and ho chi min then go , allow 10mins to look around, nothing really inside to do
It's nothing but a nice building on the outside and the the only reason for going there must be to buy stamps.
a post office. i really dont know what exactly you can see in a post office. it's like everywhere i think. it includes atm and exchange money. thats all.

We are having a great time in Saigon, but places of beauty to visit are slim, so all of a suddent, this Post Office building, which is nice, dont get me wrong, becomes a place to see when you are a tourist.  There is nothing there to see, I actually feel bad for the people trying to use the post office with all of the tourists and souveneir hawkers crawling around the place. They even lease space in the post office to booths of the same stuff you see all over the town.  Nice to stop in if you are walking by, but don't go out of your way for it.
Don't understand the hype around this one. It's a beautiful building, but it's just a post office. Was in and out again in minutes.
Apparently unmissable as a hub of a certain part of the country's economy, but apart from the exterior photos, the building itself is nothing exceptional, keep walking!
Not alot here; couple of very expensive tourist tat shops and some post office clerks. Very nice building but thats about it.

Really, it's a post office. It also has some tourist information desks, bureau de change, cash machines and tat stalls. If you want to tick it off your list, feel free. Just don't go out of your way because there's really nothing to see.
The old post office still retains some of its charm, but the security guards are unfriendly.
A regular post office with HCM picture on the back. Just another building to take pictures and continue your walk through the city. Not a must see.
We visited the central post office, not much to see, just lots of souvenir shops inside.

Adjacent to other landmarks, this visitor area highlights three outstanding buildings not common to Vietnam generally. The Post Office feels like an old government building and looks like a railway station from the outside, but railways were never really well developed in Vietnam. Worth a look for sure given the other neighbouring features.
Beautiful from the outside but lacks a bit inside. A couple of shops filled with tatt and an actual post office.
Colonial era post office is now a tourist stop...  Still a functioning P O but packed with tourists and souvenir shoppers..Not worth a stop.
Nothing to see or do. Pure waste of time. I could not understand what is so great here. It's a part of the tourism circuit,  so one does it. But a waste of time. Avoidable. Bhagwan
Nice architecture, didn't like the souvenir shops at the front, I don't think it is a must see but if you have spare time, 5 to 10 minutes should do.
This was the first tourist attraction I visited where I didnt take a single photo. Enough said. Go to the pub and have a beer, or find a cafe and have another Vietnamese Coffee.

The architecture was very French colonial and is free to visit.  They have painted murals on the wall of old maps they posted to and you can still  use the post office today. Situated next to the cathedral.
It is an actual post office where business is conducted. However, it holds little significance for tourists, although the souvenir shop is fairly well-stocked.

It is a landmark, alright. We went and had our picture taken.  There is nothing to see or do.  After the entire stay and visited various souvenir shops, I must say those at the Post office were the best.  Many others marked up quite a bit.
The post office is a beautiful old building, but that's about it. I have no idea why it's a must-see.

The only thing worth seeing in there is the ceiling. Otherwise it's just a post office like any other. There are a couple of souvenir shops in there, and a huge square outside where tourists flock to be photographed. I wouldn't say it's a must see.

Ok stop. Beautiful old building, but not much to see once inside. Make it a quick stop if you do stop. Full of typical tourist shopping.  Surrounded by American fast food restaurants such as KFC, Dunken Doughnuts, Puzza Hut, and Popeyes.

It's located opposite the equally crumbling Notre Dame de Saigon. It's a colonial building with a depressing appearance. There's nothing interesting inside—a million souvenir stalls with outrageous prices and crowds of tourists. Antique lovers might like it, but I didn't like it at all.
They still process mail, and have lots of vendors - getting an eye on the place takes about 2 minutes - at least its next to the cathedral so you can just pop in -
A nice old post office that's still in operation.
This was worth a short visit, but would have rather spent more time in the Chinese market.i didnt take any pictures here but I did send a postcard.
If you are walking past or need some stamps etc do pop in and enjoy the impressive architecture but dont make a detour.
Nothing to see actually. Like any other posg office in any other place in the world.Not recommended to lose your time here.
I didn't think there would be anything special about a post office, but it was spacious and grand, which was fascinating.
Nothing special at all. It is just an old office but If you have not much time to spend in Ho Chi minh, go somewhere else.
If I am being brutally honest I was pretty underwhelmed by my visit here. Perhaps I am being an architecture Neanderthal though!
The building itself is beautiful but the inside is a dump.  A convenient place to exchange money at not the best rates.  I found all of the goods to be of low quality and high prices.
It's a regular post office with high ceilings.  If you're in the neighborhood stop by, but not special enough for a special stop.
A building of little architectural interest, it is seen on the way to the copy of Notre Dame church, which is worth seeing.
A normally operating post office offers little beyond its old-fashioned feel; there's no way to learn about its history or unique characteristics.

It's a post office we're forced to visit because Eiffel designed it. So what? I understand it's exotic to see one of his creations in Vietnam, but it's nothing more than a post office turned into a tourist trap. Three souvenir shops. Two jewelry stores. The central rotunda sells more souvenirs, and in one corner, there's a window where you can send a postcard to your family. Don't make a point of going there. If you happen to be passing by, go in (it's easy because it's centrally located), but don't expect anything special.
Worth a visit and a photo op.
It's built in a French style, but it's not a building that would catch the eye of a European; I'd skip it if I didn't have time.

It’s worth a visit, though there isn’t much to see... You can just take a quick photo at the spot where a picture of an older gentleman is hanging inside and then leave haha... Also, there’s a cathedral right in front, so if you go to the post office, you’ll have seen two tourist attractions right away~
A quaint old world building of beautiful architecture. If you miss it don't stress just Google the pictures
From the outside, the building is quite nice to look at, but the interior isn't particularly spectacular. The souvenir shops inside sell the usual items at inflated prices.
There aren't many old buildings in HCMC, so an old post office is immediately special. Just walk inside, take a look around, and you're all set!
The building is nice but nothing special if you are from Europe. Not worth the visit if you dont have time. It was included in our TNK tour so that was alright.
Worth a visit only if you've already seen every other corner of Saigon; nothing special and completely uninteresting – except for sending letters.
Those interested in architecture or postal services will likely enjoy it. Otherwise, you can visit it as an afterthought while you're in the area for Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral.
A beautiful building worth seeing, but if you missed it, it's no problem at all.
The title says it all, no need to waste your time visiting this site as there's nothing exceptional about it.

It's not worth making a special detour. However, it's right next to the cathedral, which, while huge, isn't exactly a highlight. Given the lack of other sights in Saigon, you'll probably end up spending half an hour on both.

This is a beautiful old building that is worth a peak inside. Interestingly for an American visitor it has the feel of a train station more than a post office. That said, the clocks and wooden decor is certainly beautiful and harken to days gone by.

On a walk around the city we ended up at the post office which according to the local tourist office is a sight to see. Yes, the building has history with colonial style, some small souvenir stalls inside, old phone booths, and a big picture of HCM on the back wall.
Nothing special for western people, as we have thousands of building like that in our countries. Anyway, I took advantage of my visit to send a letter back to Catalonia
This is an absolutely beautiful building, but it was so crowded with large tours when I visited that it was not an enjoyable experience.

Everyone told me that we had to see the Central Post Office, but I'm not sure what the purpose was.  Yes, the architecture was lovely, as it is has the obvious French colonial influence, but going in there was just a tourist mad show.  People were hawking tours, tourist junk, western restaurants.  Seriously, take a picture and admire the architecture from across the street.  I wouldn't bother to go in.
I don't know why this place are famous in the tourist. In the building, just looks like normal post office. And I can not find any historical point.

The post office is a lovely looking building and located just outside of the cathedral so both are worth walking too, although inside there is literally only a small post office. I guess I turned up expecting a grander building with something to look at inside
pop in for 2 minutes when you swing by the Notre Dame cathedral.  frankly, we were more excited by the newly opened Dunkin' Donuts next door!

Perhaps this would be good to see if you new some of the history behind the location. We added it to our stroll around Hanoi. It was nothing special to look at. If you want to buy post cards or pop up cards buy them inside, and not from the vendors outside - or better yet - bargain at a different market. Its not a tourist trap you need  to find yourself at unless you are actually going to send some mail.
It felt a bit boring; everyone just strolled around and left.
The architecture is beautiful, but that's all. There's not much to see except for one interesting possibility: you can send a postcard, something from the last century that still works.

Lovely building. Really busy inside. I was planning on sending some postcards but gave up. Yes, if you're in saigon you will definitely see this place, even by accident. Everything seems to be centered around district 1.

The post office is famous for its architecture, but the current restoration is very shoddy. Inside, the reliefs on the column capitals are painted very crudely and sloppily. Apart from souvenir shops, there are no displays related to the postal service or the city's history in the form of postcards or stamps. I am completely disappointed with how the city's tourism department is treating this famous landmark.

Although this may fulfill someone's interest, it certainly didn't fulfill mine. It is simply a post office. They have a souvenir shop and some phones and that's about it. Not the best! That's one simple opinion though. Easy and quick to check out yourself.

I didn't find this attraction particularly interesting. The building is beautiful on the outside, but inside there are souvenir shops on both sides selling the same things. It is directly across from Notre Dame, so it is no trouble to walk there.
I don't know why this is a tourist attraction. Its a large central post office with souvenir shops inside.
Just dropped in to take a few photos, quite busy and the architecture is quite different from the reds of the city, but obviously quite like a big French government building!
Was really looking forward to going here, but the architecture was really nothing special, given the reputation of the architectural firm. Sadly disappointing. :(

Just go there to see the architecture. I feel this can be skipped. Spend more time at the cathedral next door. Also there are many souvenir shops. there were some items reasonably priced but dont bother just go to the night market.
I went in because I heard it was famous, but I didn't get a particularly good feeling from it.

It is located next to the cathedral. It is beautiful old architecture. However there is no air conditioners, so it was so hot and so crowded. There are many gift shop vendors in side. To me this place is just ok...
I was underwhelmed. Other than u can still post some postcards if u want to, I was lost what I should do there.
Not worth the stop, quite frankly.  I didnt find it overly interesting, but it was pretty inside. Not sure why its listed as a highlight.
This could possibly be something for those interested in architecture. Otherwise it's just boring with salespeople peddling a lot of junk.

The central post office in Vietnam is quite crowded area/public place. When i was there i had to wait 20 mins just to get into the post office. I really only wanted to post 2 postcards back home to Aus and it toke a GOOD 2 hours to do so. Not good enough. Pfft.
Nice architecture - fun phone booths and shops for souvenirs, but not worth spending too much time.  Can't remember but think the PO is still functioning.
This building is located in downtown, but it is nothing special....just regular post office. If you around. Have a look, but not a destination place.
If you want to send a postcard or make internationa phonecall, it is right place to do it. But nothing special do see or add
It's a post office, so there isn't much to be said. It lies near other attractions so it's easy to pass by. There are cool maps from the 1800s of the city and old telephone booths.
Confusing at first to figure out what is going on... And what counter you are supposed to do business at. Anyway my package for to its destination fast.

I know most tours stop by here for sightseeing and shopping since it is opposite of the Notre Dame cathedral. I suggest you enter the building, buy stamps for your post cards, take a photo of Uncle Ho and leave. Shopping is better and cheaper elsewhere.
Booked CuChi tunnels here for £17 . Older vehicle, no lunch but great guide and only 4 of us! Included hotel pick up. As a building not much to say! It is a Post Office.
The visit, if you can call it that, consists of taking a look inside.
Nice building - but ultimately it's a working post office. That said, as a photographer I did find it an interesting place to photograph

The outside of the building is perhaps more interesting than the inside of it. There's parts that has been converted into selling lousy touristy stuff. Interesting phone booth to look at but you'll probably find the hawkers outside more interesting. Went in for 5 mins for a short rest at the bench and left.

There is not much to see.  It would not be worth it as part of a tour that I had to pay for.... but as a place to walk to for free, it was okay.  It is right across from the cathedral, and walking distance from many other places in District one, get a map and enjoy the walk, don't get ripped off by taxi drivers.
It doesn't seem to be a tourist attraction. It's pretty, but doesn't warrant special mention. Don't go there specifically for that. If you're passing by, visit it.

Ok. I saw it when I went to see Notre Dame as they are opposite each other which is handy. It's nice to look at, the inside is full of shops and there's lots of hawkers outside. I wouldn't say you were missing out on anything by not going inside.
Meh. It's a large post office with some history and a lot of people trying to sell you stuff. No big deal really.
Only the facade holds any interest for those feeling nostalgic.
Just a colonial building, go see it if you're near, if not, don't bother. It's close to the Note Dame, which is the same type.
Nothing special about it at all except overpriced and crawl knock knock yours   Some old time phone booths and clocks   Not sure why it is a thing

This is a remodeled big city post office with lot of shops selling tourist stuff.  I'm not much of a shopper and already had plenty of Vietnam tourist souvenirs so I wasn't interested in that.  Plus if you do like shopping there are other, better places to do it.The building is nice enough but there are many better ways to spend your time.

Unfortunately there was not much to see on this excursion. Fortunately it was part of a day trip, because if it was a trip by itself it would have been totally disappointing. There are a couple of souvenir shops in the station that sell exactly the same items. The shops are very slim, and crowded. You can obviously buy stamps  at the venue, but as I said not much else.

Good for a few mins, so you can make some pics and tell your friends at home that this building was designed by Eifel. Other than that, you are done in 5 mins. Unless you want to buy souvenirs, thats also possible here.
Nothing much to see here, just a typical very busy general post office.  It is so busy and full of people taking pictures
I saw this on a solo outing, so I didn't hear any history behind it so maybe that's why I was unimpressed. But honestly, didn't see the big deal.
The post office is a must-see according to guide books for Ho Chi Minh city. It is interesting to have a look at it but I didn't find the visit of the inside of this building thrilling nor a "must".
The Central Post Office is (quite obviously) central and probably worth a look, but it's very much a tourist trap. Get out of the tourist areas and see the real Vietnam is my recommendation.

As with many locations on tripadviser, this is a feature worth stopping to pop in and see but I wouldn't dream of recommending people to go out of their was to see.  And this,  why is it on the list of things to do.  Saying that,  I certainly do not know of the history which may well give it further kudos of you take a tour involving this as you may actually find something about the history.
Nice French colonial architecture but no much to see overall.  I loved the placement of Ho Chi Minh's picture - can't miss it.
Have a look if you are walking past the post office....dont go out of your way though. Plenty of other more interesting places to see in HCMC
Not even really sure why this is a tourist attraction. The outside of the building looks okay? Overall a boring experience and not worth the trip at all. Spend your time elsewhere.
Just a building that you can send letters.  Save your time and go somewhere else.  Seriously don't waste your time and money.  And if you do go just walk in don't take a tour.
It seems like it's obligatory, but there's nothing interesting about it. Good shops with cheap souvenirs.
Not that impressive. I don't regret walking a few minutes to get there but it is not much of a destination. Just a couple tourist shops and a post office...not that exciting.

we were walking and came across the notre dame cathedral and the post office is right across the street so we went in since we had heard of people going there and it wasn't anything special, yes it is pretty but we were in there for maybe 1 minute and then walked out. go if you are by the cathedral but don't make a special trip there for it.
A completely ordinary post office in the city center) Three million tourists per square meter, two million sellers of all sorts of unnecessary trifles and food, and a one-minute tourist attraction)

You'll probably end up near this at some point during your time in Saigon but I don't get the big fuss. The building is nice enough but there are shops inside the actual post office selling the same tourist junk you see everywhere. What could be nice photogenic hallways are now cramped market type stalls. See if you pass by but I wouldn't walk here expecting to be stunned.

I mean the building is beautiful on the outside but so is the opera and people committee and other French influenced buildings. I wouldn't go out of your way to see the post office. I mean if you have post to send- I guess do it here? There are tourist shops inside the post office which kind of ruins the atmosphere and a McDonald's cafe next door...
But since it is next to Norte Dame Cathedral you can have two photo opportunities in one trip! Lots of restaurants and of course coffee shops in the area.
Attractive French colonial architecture, but that is about it.  Do not knock yourself out if you miss it.

On every tour but it must be because its close to Notre Dame which is also a low B grade attraction. Architecture is interesting in a time capsule sort of way . But it its hard to spend more than a few minutes there
French architecture (Eiffel?) and a huge portrait of Ho Chi Minh inside create a striking and interesting contrast.
It's just a Western-style building, and not really worth spending a lot of time there. It seems to be included in tourist tours, but I didn't think it was that important.
It is a somewhat classic Western building. Not special at all. There isn't much to see in the center of Ho Chi Minh City anyway in terms of buildings or interesting architecture.
Admittedly, the facade is beautiful and the interior has a nice "train station" feel, but to rank this place 8th out of everything there is to see here... I just don't understand.

I mean, its a post office in an old attractive building, but it's worth approximately 30 seconds of your time and no more. Worth a look if you're walking by, but by no means is it worth going out of your way for.
A bit disappointing. There isn't much to see or do. The building is interesting, but we were let down after reading the descriptions of the place, which promised much more.
The place is a witness of the french colonial influence in the Country! If you are a fan of post cards like meee, this place is heaven for you!
Although it is an old building, it is mostly filled with shopping inside, so it falls short of being called a tourist attraction.

This is a normal old post office in Colonial style. By European standards there is nothing special about the place, but by Asian history it is fine. My recommendation is to drop by, spend 5 minutes and then move on.

I know this post office since 13 years ago and the older it gets the less attractive it is. Instead of keeping it a nice old post office it became a flee market for souvenirs. Can go there to take a picture and thats about it. You wont spend more than 30-45 minutes there.

A fully functional Posy Office.  Not much to see here other than the beautiful old building.  You step back in time to a French Post Office of early 20th century. "If" you are in the area it would be a nice photo stop.
Some oldish architecture not particularly well maintained. A busy working post office. Nice old telephone booths. Free
It still functions as the Central Post Office today. It has a vaulted ceiling made of iron and glass. Very beautiful and frequently visited.
Just a post office, nothing much. Only the architecture is worth visiting for admiration, but even then, if you are well-travelled, this isn't a place you would want to visit.

Unlike some, this place not  really wasn’t for us, fairly small, architecture was ok, couple of gift stores, that was about it, watch out for the stairs up to the gift stores as they are steep and wouldn’t think to a standard.
It is just humongous gift shop. Very crowded and noisy. Really not much to see. Waste of time. It is a good place to send a postcard from Vietnam.
Boring at bat s---Just a boring old building can't see the fuss at all, Unless you like boring old buildings Waste of time
Sorry but this place is not worthy to be so popular. Sure its a nice building for a photo but i was there for about 5 minutes. Saw it, took a photo and moved on. Nothing special.
A nice building, but very underwhelming and just a tourist trap. Not worth visiting unless actually sending a postcard.
An expensive tourist trap at best, with really nothing of interest to see here. Across the street from the supposed Notre Dam Cathedral - also not impressive, which is under construction.
A nice building but nothing really much to see. If you are in the neighborhood just walk by, it takes a few minutes to check out.
I don't think it's really necessary to go.
It wasn't that big of a deal. The old phone booths look pretty cool and that's about it. Too many people walking around.
No one should have to spend more than five minutes in a post office unless you are sending a letter.
Nice building, but actually it's just a big souvenir shop.
Not worth it. Crowded and not much to see. Wouldn’t recommend. Ok to walk in if right there but wouldn’t make a special trip.
I don’t know why they consider this place to visit , it is just includes many gift shops with highly priced items.
A short stay is more than enough. The post office is open and there are plenty of shops. In our opinion, there's much more to see in Saigon...
I've seen better post offices. Obviously, you can see why, and they're next to the Cathedral. All stalls selling items.

We spent about 5 minutes walking around before we left. It’s still a working Post Office which was cool to see, but is swamped with tourist shops and vendors outside. I couldn’t see any signage on history or any information, so was a shame.
the construction from outside does look good but beyond that there is nothing to see here. given its quite close to another site you can spend a few minutes there but nothing major to talk about
Beautiful building inside and out, but it's now really an overcrowded market place selling overpriced nic nacs

I was disappointed that there was no exhibit or narrative to guide visitors to the sight. Instead there were shops selling souvenirs of Vietnam and Saigon filling up the space of the Post Office. Why would we buy any thing there as we can find the same stuff elsewhere at a more reasonable price.
If you are in the area go and take a look but don't go out of your way.We were led to believe this was an awesome structure but go and see for yourself.
In my idea central post office nothing have special point and its a store only , you can check better place on the city

Among the many uninteresting sights to see in Saigon, the central post office is a rather baffling sight. It's a French-style building, with an air of antiquity both inside and out, and is lined with cheap souvenir shops. Skip it.
More for souvenirs shopping than anything. Was in and out in 15 minutes. Not good  ventilation in building how ever I did find decent price for some items.
The building looks very nice. However, it's very crowded and noisy.

We came here because we were in the area and the reviews were reasonable. It’s a giant post office, how entertaining could it be? There are souvenirs for purchase inside and it was overall really underwhelming.
A commercial place full of people selling souvenir. It has broken the traditional feel of a post office. Nothing fantastic of the place.
Arrived to find it was closed for refurbishment !! It was part of tour so tour guide should have known this prior to our visit !

I’m not sure why this is such a “must see” for the various tours offered. The French Colonial architecture was the only thing remotely interesting about this building. Inside is a large picture of Ho Chi Minh, large country maps and old phone booths which now house ATM machines.  Across the street is the Notre Dame of Ho Chi Minh City.  A McDonald’s is next door to the Post Office and to the right is one of the buildings used as an extraction point for South Vietnamese who helped the US during the war.
Nothing to write home about. It was crowded of tourists and nothing was special (for me). Not recommend to go.

hot & humid didn't help. If like aged post offices then go along otherwise not much else. architecture was good but not to impressive. nice retro touch to actually send a postcard.surrounding area is popular for weddings and the notra dame building next door( closed for renos)

It's like and of the other colonial buildings in Vietnam. You don't have to go at all. The only cool thing is that it's right beside the embassy where they took that famous picture of the last Americans leaving Vietnam on a helicopter from a rooftop.
Maybe they brought us here because there is little to see in Saigon, but this place doesn't add anything to the visit to the city, it's just a normal post!!

Beautiful building, lovely ambiance.  When we were there it was full of people, lots outside too as it is right opposite the cathedral.  The outside of the building is beautiful, which attracts a lot of people taking photographs.  Inside it is impressive and calm.  Shops selling the usual tourist items, very cramped in these parts.

The highlights of this early 19th-century French building are its arched ceilings, the telephone booth near the entrance, and the old maps displayed on the walls. Also noteworthy is the clock on the front of the building.

It looks like a typical French post office, but it doesn't function as a post office or shop. The shops inside are overpriced; if you really want to buy postcards, head to the adjacent bookstore area—you'll find some good ones at cheaper prices! I don't recommend spending too much time here.
A special one above the rest, a nice building that has more or less been converted into a souvenir shop. Don't spend too much time here. Take the obligatory photo as well.
Just a glorified large post office selling trinkets! Not really a place too visit,a waste of time really.
Only went there for the sake of ticking it off the list of things to do and attractions to see whilst in Ho Chi Minh City
Good to see as a “place” in Saigon. But you wouldn’t be missing much not to go. More of a spot to tick off the list
An old building and if you need to mail something then go.  Price is right, free.  Not much to see, a few vendors inside.
Apart from taking photos of the building, there's not much else interesting about this place. The souvenirs look ordinary and expensive. The inside of the building is quite dark.
Nice building, but if you don’t have much time, I’d recommend a visit to the war remnants museum. Post office is just a big hall full of people selling junk to tourists.
I wasn't impressed. It does look like a train station. But nothing to write home about or even go out of my way to visit. It just gave vendors a chance to display goods.

Beautiful and still well preserved building .. visited in afternoon ... masses of Chinese tourist groups yelling and jostling each other ... McDonald’s adjacent and a small street of book shops selling English books too ... cafes and hang out area ... suggest visit early to avoid the mobs
Beautiful building, great architecture.

I maybe didn't understand the significance of this building, but it literally just seemed like a regular large post office. If you want to pick up souvenirs though, this may be a good place to go as there were many merchants.

A beautiful building (take two or three photos, no more), and inside there's a shop. Fans, fidget spinners, flatbreads, magnets, crocodile skin. I recommend visiting only this way: when entering the Saigon Cathedral of Our Lady of Sorrows, turn right, take two photos, and move on. Don't waste your time!!!
Really  very little to see.You can mail your post cards here. Close to the Cathedral. Not really a must see.
A beautiful building that has unfortunately been converted into a huge souvenir shop. I find that very sad, because it completely loses its charm.
The main post office is a really beautiful building from the outside.
Save your time. It’s a building with no particular interest other than being 100 years plus old. Don’t go if you don’t have to.
Nice to have seen but done that in about 10 minutes. Was there for an hour during a tour. Not my specific highlight. Was okay for a 15 minutes but that's it
Hot and full of souvenir stores. No restroom facilities. Impressive design. Souvenirs not over priced

The building is beautiful and the working concept of different desks for each postal area is quant. Pity about the masses of souvenir sellers as it turned it into a market.If time is tight, forget about going there!!!

Maybe because we went there by cyclo on a very rainy day or because it was really crowded with people escaping the weather but I couldn’t wait to leave.It was boring. I know it’s a piece of history but it ain’t no colosseum. If you’re on a tour do a drive by otherwise my advise is give this one a miss.

It did not change much when I visited it 10 years ago, a place crammed with expensive souvenir vendors that I may have found abroad (meters less than half the price) !? Not worth more than 30 minutes.

Old French colonial building, however not super spectacular and not authentic due to a huge souvenir shop inside. Have a quick look when you visit Notre Dame, the Opera and District 1 in general (you‘ll stroll by anyways).
Very nice building from the outside,however, we were disappointed when we entered and were greeted two large souvenir stores either side of the entrance,
A French colonial-style building in the same square as the Notre Dame Church, it's truly uninteresting.
old building with tourist stuff for sale inside. not much to see here imo. the post office is actually a working post office though if you need to mail something.
The attraction is so-so... well, the post office... it's strange that this needs to be seen at all. A large building, telephone booths inside. You can send a postcard.

This building is not really impressive. The outside is plan and the main lobby inside is not as impressive as most post office and railway stations that are on tourist destinations. At least it's free.

After visiting Temples, Churches,Museums, the Reunification Palace, Notre Dame cathédrale, our guide insisted to visit the Post Office as we were not too keen for this visit.  Same as in Europe Plus hundred of kiosks souvenirs.  Absolutely nothing of interest ,for us anyway.  A useless visit unless you need a souvenir ... Part of our program , replied our guide when asked. The purpose of visiting a Post office...Not a nice visit

The building has a charming exterior that evokes the feeling of European rule. The interior was also lovely and retro. It appeared to still be in use. As a tourist spot, there were also many souvenirs for sale.

With seemingly hundreds of tourist mingling with the local populate going about their busy in this central Post Office coupled with small businesses in every possible corner, no ever where! Interesting but busy!

I'd been curious about it because I often saw it in guidebooks. When I actually went there, it wasn't as big as I imagined. There are souvenir shops lined up on both sides of the post office, but the passageway is narrow, making it difficult to browse.

I personally would not bother with this,  it nice building but it’s not that impressive having been to grand central station this is no where near the grandeur of that.  I can say I’ve been and seen it though
I didn't find it particularly interesting. I felt that simply passing by and taking a picture was sufficient. Those interested in architecture might find it interesting to visit.
Nice building but again a filler when there is no other tourist attractions to see. Probably another 30 minutes of your life that you have wasted and won't get back

Nice to look at from the outside and a quick look inside is all you need. This is mostly a tourist trap for shopping the vendors inside. What you need to know is the more historical place of interest is just in view, from the street. The CIA headquarters and where helicopters took US citizens out of Vietnam during the fall of Saigon . There is video footage of this and you can see the building where this happened.
Nothing special.Interesting maybe for locals.Big hall with some souvenier shops.Use time elsewhere.
This is an overcrowded touristy place. Architecture-wise not very interesting. But if you are looking for a way to pass time, go and have a look on the way around the city.

there is nothing exciting to see in this post office. you can see the old phone booth turned into ATM machines... and a lot of stalls selling souvenirs and that's it. it's extremely hot outside even when we visited during dec time. just feel like a tourist trap spot for selling you souvenirs. we went because it's part of the city tour we joined. can be skipped.
It’s funny to see colonial style buildings in Asia, but this place doesn’t have any added value other than the building itself. It houses a few souvenir shops and that’s it.

Other than taking picture with Ho Chi Minh's portrait, there isn't much interesting here. Definitely not an ideal place for forex exchange as their rate are so low.Try going to the tour agents shop to exchange instead.

Tour groups everywhere, so you can't really stop and take in the splendour of this building. Beautiful floor though - very ornate. And the book market nearby is a fascinating place to spend some time people watching.
Walk in if you are passing by.  Not much to see, but a nice colonial addition to the city.  Smaller than the pictures ilustrate.

I had no idea if I missed out something.I saw a lot of tourists in this pkace so I entered.It was a post office with operating for sending post, selling stamps, packing.There are many small souvenir shops inside this place.I could not see anything interesting much, but I had no tourguide with me so I might miss out something important for its history.

The French era post office is supposedly built like a train station and that is supposed to be it's main reason for attraction. However, the inside is like a bargain souvenir market. Not very impressive.

i start my first day here, 20 mins walk from my hotel (Sofitel). The walking trip was great, but for the post office...I'm still not quite sure what all the hype is/was about. It is just a building and tourist trap, yes done by Eiffel but nothing impressive. A lot of shops around selling over priced merchandise. If you are limited on time, skip this, this town have a lot more to offer.

The building has certain historic and sentimental appeal, but being honest tyere is nothing special to see or do there. If you are nearby maybe you can go and send a letter to a family memnber or friend and thats all.
Just across the Notre Dame, but actualy not worth visiting. Tourist shops are inside, but no real special details.
Apart from the colonial architecture of the building, there is nothing interesting of the place. There's a large souvenir shop right at the middle and it was kind of waste vising here.
I don't understand why people come here as a tourist activity; I can't imagine doing that anywhere else. The building is average, bordering on nice, but that's about it.
A beautiful fully functioning post office spoiled with so much tourist stuff inside. A shame since it is a great old building with a functioning post office.
It's unclear why it's included in the city tour. An early twentieth-century post office, in the French colonial style, still in operation. And that's it.
I was disappointed in this - the building is amazing and well worth seeing but the low end tourist shopping inside is worthless to me - look from outside
If you've toured places like Italy or France, and have seen the "old architecture" there, you will not be impressed with the Central Post Office.

The architecture from the outside is beautiful. The inside has been turned into a small scale of market. You can find souvenirs found at any other markets throughout the city.It locates opposite the Notre Dame (which was still under maintenance as of Nov 2018). If you’re interested in historical stuff or culture or architecture, it is a good place to visit.Now that I think of it I might have appreciated the place more if I had read about its history before visiting the place. As far as I know there’re not many signs or reading in English explaining how important the place is on-site. So be prepared. And you’ll enjoy it more. I guess..
It's a large old post office you can post things here. However it has a number of gift shops in it for buying souvenirs
We just spend 5 minutes over there. Nice building but nothing really more to say about it... you can skip it if you are short of time
Can skip if you have not much time in HCM. The souvenirs prices are higher as targeted at tourists. Not much to see inside the building. Took some photographs and left.
Nice building, but that's about it.
Whilst the building is nice if your into this type of holiday, their are gift shops, ATMs and exchange services inside only.
Very touristy and really nothing special.
