I wasn't quite sure why this is considered a tourist stop.  Maybe because it is beside the Catholic Church.  It's a working post office, so why visit it??  The building is interesting, but I think one can find more interesting places to visit in Saigon.  If you are wanting to see the Catholic Church, it is under renovation, so it has even more limited access than before.  Before, you were only allowed inside the entrance, now I don't think they even allow that.  So my recommendation would be to bypass both the church and post office.
Was interesting....beautiful exterior....interior was nothing special to me....enjoyed but nothing really spectacular
Disappointing to enter the beautiful building. I would have thought the market was more than two narrow corridors. The hall itself had an original interior, and that was nice.

On the internet, people often say this is one of the famous places in Saigon. However, my actual experience was completely different. The architecture isn't particularly outstanding, and repeated renovations have diminished its original antique charm.
The building is certainly nice but it's not worth going in, there's nothing inside, just a lot of people moving around hastily.

Not much to see. If you’re interested in French architecture (or post offices) then go for it. Otherwise it’s just a functioning post office with a few cramped gift stores in the wings. The fact that it was designed by Gustav Eiffel peaked my interest for a second, but that quickly evaporated.
Nice architecture but not much to see. There are some tourist shops inside but besides that a standard operating post office.
Unfortunately we didn't know how to appreciate. Just a building for us. We went in 3mins and came out. Can go as a tourist spot. Can skip too.
Was a very important place for the vietnamese but not really a place of interest for anyone else. It's only real interest might be the architecture and the maps on the walls inside the building.

It's a much-talked-about building, but frankly, very disappointing. When you see similar buildings in old Europe or even other, much more elaborate Vietnamese architecture, this building falls so incredibly short... I wouldn't bother visiting it again.
OK, it was planned to be a train station, but they turned it into a Post Office instead (I think) and that's about all I can say about it . . .

There's not much to see: a post office, similar to some Italian train stations, and elsewhere, with a large clock on the front. Right next to the Basilica of Notre-Dame. The basilica is advertised as something special: if you happen to go there and manage to get inside, it's under construction, so judge for yourself!
The Central Post Office is listed as a “must see” in many guides & though the building is of historical importance, I wouldn’t consider it a “Must”.

We were fortunate to hire a personal tour guide from a social enterprise called travelearn. They curated our tour to fit to what we were interested in. The highlight of the excursion was this piece of living history. The crown jewel was meeting and interacting with the last notary writer in Saigon! Got his to translate our English postcard into French and Vietnamese. Had a pleasant chat with him to know a little bit about him. Many stories in the 88 year old octogenarian.

We saw the Central Post Office and Cathedral as part of a visit around the city on our first day and honestly i wouldn't bother... I'm not sure if there is meant to be more significance around the buildings but there is nothing around to explain that to you if there is... its basically just got two souvenir shops and a lot of postcards, key chains etc for purchase
The building is impressive, but inside, apart from the post office, there's only a souvenir shop. Combining it with Notre Dame doesn't add any extra effort.

The building is stunning, I definitely appreciate that.  However it has been turned into a tacky touristy place, nothing about it feels authentic or interesting other than the architecture. If you’re in the area check it out - but it isn’t worth the making a special trip for. Is right next the Notre dame cathedral but it is currently under renovation.  Beware of the various scam artists outside, and hold onto your bags or keep your hand over your wallet inside and out. They’re very good at what they do... I would give it 2.5 stars if it was an option

Not a special building, but nice to pop in for a quick look if you're in the neighborhood anyway (for Notre Dame across the street, for example). There are plenty of ATMs, so if you need to withdraw cash, it is safer this way.

The post office was part of my walking tour and my Jeep night tour. I can’t say it’s a must go. It’s a post office built with French architecture and there’s a bunch of junk souvenirs you can buy there. See it if you must but it’s not a big deal, in my opinion.

From the outside, the building is very inviting but soon as you stepped in, not a single element of surprise that you will find in there. It could have been good if they’ve added more historical elements like, old photos, old stamps, people who visited the place etc. just to add something interesting and /or educational about the place.
The post office itself is a beautiful building from the outside, but inside it is a disappointment.
Sure, it's a nice old building, but you probably have a dozen or more of these in whatever City you're in right now.  Wouldn't recommend unless you're a fan of the work of Alfred Foulhoux.
Plentiful of cooler things around the area that are just so much cooler. We are young dudes and maybe we didn't appreciate it as much but it just wasn't that cool.
or need to be aggressively persistent to secure where you are in the line, otherwise some people try to cut in and it will take you forever to get your parcel(s) delivered
Wasnt great. A couple of souvenir shops and a post office. Was underwhelming experience. Wouldn't have cared if I missed seeing this.
It's a nice and pretty building, we took some pictures, went inside to look at the souvenirs, but there's not much else to do.
You can go here and take the photo. I sent postcards from this place in Jan for my friend in Vietnam but only after a half of year did they receive the card. So fasst!!!!
The Post Office is generally a post office. However, vendors have stalls inside. Lots of souvenir items but more expensive than outside stores.

Nothing remarkable really.  There is an old "colonial" map of old "French" Vietnam that the communist regime has let be where it is.  For the rest you can come here to buy stamps: the staff is very friendly and English speaking

The architecture is impressive, but beyond that it’s a tourist trap. The place was extremely busy and the shopping areas were tiny with a ton of people. The souvenirs sold are the same that can be purchased anywhere, so I would recommend taking pictures and moving on.
This is pretty boring, I understand it's same architect as Eiffel Tower, however there's not really much to see inside. Recommend to skip this one.
Not really special.
This place is polluted by too many souvenirs shops...nothing special...i was expecting something different. Visiting this place is a waste of time

C'mon people, five stars.for this place. It has to be a joke.I can admit, that I wasted my time and money coming here, do the same, you will feel better.It's a nice building hence the two stars. Although it is likely you have been to more remarkable buildings in your time.It's busy, a massive tourist trap. It is surrounded by over priced restaurants, people hassling you to buy something or get you shoes cleaned or go somewhere in an overpriced taxi.There is really not enough reason to come here unless maybe you are an architect.

Really didn’t see the point of this being such a tourist attraction. It is a working post office!?!? Beautiful building though. Then once inside there is the typical tourist markets along the front corridors and post office counters in the middle.
Post office recently renovated, nice and clean.
The building's architecture is certainly very beautiful. However, we felt it lacked a certain something... It is, and remains, just a post office.

I don’t understand why this place is advertised as a tourist destination. Yes, the building is rather nice but it’s basically full of ‘Made in China’ junky items. Really. Not worth the effort. One good thing, behind the Post Office there was a really interesting avenue that was full of bookstores. It was refreshing to see stores selling something a little bit different.
Nothing special - a building and a really really hot one. You can buy stamps but new spent about 1 minute in here and escaped.
If you are compeled to tick out all that is recommended -  go and see this place; otherwise do not bother. You may buy a postcard and  a stamp and send it from there, thats the whole fun of the place
I was expecting a spectacular place, we were disappointed with the result. Worth a visit if you're just passing through.
Well we went to visit the war museum how confronting and very sad.could not stay longer as felt compelled to leave due to how sad it was
I popped by the post office this evening to check it out and frankly don’t see what the hype is about. Nothing special and the staff were rather rude.
Well....it’s really just a post office. Very hot and crowded inside. Staff is not friendly at all. The outlook of the post office is ok.

It's centrally located, so stop in if you happen to walk by, but there is really nothing to see inside other than the ceiling. The hordes of tourists make it difficult to enjoy. There really isn't anything to experience. I wouldn't bother.

We stopped here for 15 minutes in a tour. Yes it is a big European building in the middle of Saigon, attractive but very very busy and side rooms of things for sale directed at tourists. You can send a post card/letter and take some pictures but to Europeans it is not more grand than Post Offices in home provincial cities.

The building opposite the Notre Dame de Saigon Cathedral doesn't boast any remarkable architecture. It used to be the main post office, but now houses a souvenir market and a small amen shop. Prices are almost the same as at the market. Of interest is a map of southern Vietnam on the wall inside, along with some interesting postcards you can send by mail. You can also see wedding couples having their photoshoots.

I did this as part of a city tour. I found it pointless and a bit gimicky. I don't this after the independence palace which was equally as boring. The city has lots to offer but this is pretty boring.
Can’t really see the point of going there - yes its a great building and yes the interior is okay including the huge picture of ‘Uncle Ho’ but its overcrowded and not really very interesting.
While a beautiful style of architecture the place really doesn’t know what it should be. A haven for the recently rich Chinese me thinks as I was grabbed and photographed!

Been there 8-9 years ago. This time it was a bit of a disappointment, as the whole thing had been turned into a big tourist circus. It was all incredibly messy and you had no sense of the grandeur and original function of the place. So sad.
There is so little worth seeing in Saigon that they show you a big Post Office from the colonial era.  Save your time, don't bother.
It's a nice building to see, if you're there. It is next to the Notre Dam. Inside there are a couple of souvenir shops.
This building is French colonial and nice architecture but is more of a tourist trap.Spend no more than 10 minutes looking inside
I'm not sure why so many tourists go there. Is the interior a historical building as well? I'm a bit skeptical. It's fine for a walk if your hotel is nearby, but I don't think it

Do the effort to go here, for a great selfie, or photo opportunity - the building is really great. There is a working post office inside, if you can stomach the tourist stampede and overpriced trinkets.The location and architecture has a real charm to it, pity it has to be spoiled with a heavy dose of capitalism (mind you: make that a double shot).Not an essential visit, but great if you have 5-7 minutes to spare.
If I wanted to see a French or European building I would have gone to Europe. Just a post office, nothing crazy

If you like French colonial architecture, this is good to see.If not interested it is not a big deal.  Interesting that they turned the phone booths into ATM’s. But that is about it.It is not inconvenient as it is across from Norte Dame church and a few blocks away from main hotels and consulates.
If one like to visit a monuments with good architecture then they would like it at the first glance. Otherwise it's an ordinary market with many many vendors selling nick necks thing.

I'm not really sure what the draw to this place is other than its relative location to other attractions. The building is marginally interesting to look at, but, on the inside, it is super crowded and oppressively hot and humid even in December. Save your time (and perspiration) and admire the building from outside as you walk by on your way to see the other attractions in the area.
I visited this place during a trip with adventures around the world...they say it's a must-see...but I honestly don't see anything special about it.
When I asked at the information desk about buying stamps, they very reluctantly told me to buy them at the souvenir shop.

On January 14, 2018, I visited this old post office, which was packed with onlookers. It is indeed an old building, with old-fashioned counters and an interesting ceiling. Visitors are mainly confronted by a large number of vendors selling old maps, souvenirs, and vintage stamps, who cleverly took advantage of the crowds being drawn in.
Built during the French colonial era, this place still serves as the Ho Chi Minh City Central Post Office.
If you're just passing by, it's good to take some nice photos. Nothing special, just something for a postcard...
I don't understand how this can be a tourist attraction. The architecture outside is interesting and beautiful, but going inside is not worth the time.
Nice to see if you're passing by, but don't bother unless you are particularly into seeing different architecture/buildings.
I wasn’t really sure why this was included in our tour.  Was pretty to look at but didn’t add much educational value.  That’s my opinion anyway.
It's just a building. Nothing to see here. The history of Vietnam should be more recognised in this city not the oppression of it.
Don't know why it is even a tourist attraction!?!?!There is not much to see or admire... just an old post office like a million that would exist world over
Dont get why its an attraction...its just a building. Pretty yes but not worth going out of the way to see!
Basically it’s just a post office with nice architecture. Most people will just take some photos and then side tracked by the souvenir stores on the left and right side of post office.

We stopped by here on a walking tour with Saigon Lovers. Unless you're interested in French colonial architecture or post offices it's not worth battling the tourists to get inside. Our guides stayed outside while we snapped one photo. Skip it.

I've been going to Saigon for several years now, and the post office has unfortunately become a tourist shop... before, you could write your postcards and stick your stamps in the jars... they've gotten rid of everything!! Disappointing... the exterior is fine...
It's just an old post office with Souvenir shops everywhere. Next to it is a MacDonald joint if you like a western meal after all Asian food.
A glimpse of colonial past, centrally located. Stop by for 5 mins to take a pic & absorb the atmosphere. Guide highlighted the Asian influence in architecture, the lotuses!

A visit right next to Notre Dame. It's worth a visit, but there's nothing spectacular to see. We went on a Sunday, which is packed with students, and they were all taking photos, so we couldn't really appreciate the building properly.
It's a post-office that looks like a train station with overpriced southerners. Worth a picture and to send a postcard if that's your thing but not much to see.
The place is ok good thing its opposite the Notre Dame Cathedral.  There are a couple gift shops but they don't offer anything different. I wouldn't go outvof the way to visit it

This post office is supposed to be famous, but it is nothing more than a 'beautiful building' with a history that is, in my opinion, uninteresting. It is swarming with tourists whom they are eager to exploit.
It's a beautiful huge post office with European architecture. It's too much crowded inside. A lot of people want to send a postcard. And a souvenir is more expensive than outside shop.
It's just a post office. Nothing to see. Move on. On the plus side it's free and has a large hanging picture of Ho Chi Minh but basically just a post office. Worth 10 mins of your time and no more.
Chaos and not very helpful people. Took me ages to work out where to post my postcards, no letter boxes anywhere.
Well, a post office is a post office. There's no air conditioning, so it's hot.

It's a post office. It is old and the architecture is cool. The old phone booths are there. If you have a guide for this you can get a little info about why one booth was used for other purposes, and learn that the 90+year old scribe has retired but still goes to the post office to work because he loves it. It's a quick stop. There is a gift shop.

I love colonial architecture and this is an impressive building- but nothing to really make it a "must see" - but worth a quick look.  Like many of the old colonial buildings in Saigon - they would benefit from  some written history boards to tell their story and give them a sense of place in the history of the city.   The little pedestrian street running down from McDonalds next door was pleasantly cool and calm and some funky little book stores.
As the title suggests, it's just an office, nothing more. You can visit it, though, just to tick it off your list of sights, since it's near Notre Dame Cathedral and Ben Tham Market.
The city tour includes a visit to this building, but for me it has no greater significance than a building from the colonial era.
WhEn you have been there you feel little empty- really: its built as a central train station but it is an post office- hmmmm what to say. Little waste of time.
Lots of sellers to hound you with things. A good stop off for 5 mins at the most. Not a highlight of the city. But good to see the building.
This post office was apparently designed by the same person who designed the Eiffel Tower in Paris.

Nothing special it wasn't a must see to me. The area surrounding it was lively and had a lot going on. There are souvenir shops inside with a few things to buy. The post office is open for use which I didn't realize.
Maybe just me but I found this pretty underwhelming. If you're passing maybe check it out but I wouldnt go out of your way
Nice building. Can be seen in 5 minutes. There is not much to see, but give it the 5 minutes it takes to walk around in there

The building itself is a symbol of the French era and as such is worth a visit. It is best seen from the outside, and if you are short on time there is no need to visit the inside. Spend your time visiting the French church directly opposite.
This place has nothing to offer except McDonalds is next door.I think the tourist companies take you here to fill in time only and visit Maccas.

We took a walking tour and the guides took us there. It's a beautiful building, but the visit was absolutely unnecessary. Saigon is such a vibrant and incredible city that I don't think it's worth visiting such a bland (although beautiful) building like that.

If you've got a packed Ho Chi Minh itinerary, don't bother squeezing this in. Its nothing special at all. A walk in, photo, walk out kind of place. We didn't really engage in looking at the post cards and stamps and what not. There are however, a few ATM's and a money exchange office inside.
Interesting piece of history but don't worry if you miss it! Driving past would almost be enough:/ it does look like it should be in the middle of old London though:)
Great on the outside and if you're lucky you might be able to snap a half decent photo without too many people in it. The inside is just an overrated souvenir shop.

The CATAI tour offers you the chance to see this building inside, which is unlike any other post office in the world. All 20 clocks they have are broken and out of time. I think the CATAI company has been doing these kinds of tours for a while now, and they're not worth much.
If you like buying tourist souvenirs, this is a great place to pay too much for them. Otherwise, it's also an old post office. The architecture was mildly interesting.
Except for the French architecture, there's nothing exceptional about this place. After all, it's a post office! It's about as exciting as the McDonald's next door.

Nothing to see.Just a place that has a lot things to sell with a high price for tourists.Nothing really to do in this place If you are here just come as its free ... but nothing to do or see really at the end.
A tourist trap like nothing I've ever seen.

There isnt much to do or see here at all. The outside is nice to look at and take a photo of but thats about it. Inside it is still a functionning post office so thats handy if you need to send something internationally. Other than that, theres some overpriced tourist souvenirs being sold and the visit is over in about 15 minutes.
Just an old post office, comparable to the post office in Rotterdam back in the day. Nothing spectacular.

It's a post office building with a nice exterior, but nothing special inside. Postal services are still being carried out, and it's full of tables selling souvenirs for tourists. You can avoid it, but it's next to the Cathedral of Notre Dame de Paris.
The architecture is pretty cool and it's a working post office.  That's about it.   Add on a tacky gift shop and lots of sweaty tourists.  Would't be near the top of my list at HCMC.
Cool building from the outside but pretty boring inside. Worth taking a look...but not missing much.

The post office is a fabulous building. Lots of hustle and bustle, do not change money here, rip off, 3% commission and a bad rate. Good to see the workings of the old building, every space has been sub let and used in order to sell, sell, sell to the thronging tourists.Walk around it, take a couple of snaps and be on your way. Notre Dam is over the way, so both dove tail, down within 90 mins.
Thought it was a bit boring, it's an old post office that looks like a railway station. Can't see behind the scenes or anything interesting just touristy gift shops.
It's a post office, not much more to see here. Yes it is historically significant but not much more than this. Read the Wikipedia page and move along.
When you enter the central, it feels a bit like going back in time. The design and scenery is very likely and beautiful. Cheap to send something and the help is very nice
Really not much to see here. Looks nice from outside. Worth going in to take a photo that's about it.
A nice old building but I really feel I missed something because that's it. The space inside is packed with the typical souvenir stalls and I was underwhelmed.

Just a building that now houses different stalls selling Vietnamese knick-knacks.I only enjoyed it as it was a scene from my childhood with wooden pay phone booths  otherwise I cant understand why this space isnt put to better use
I have been to many Central post offices in my travels and The Central Post Office is just another postal office expanded to offer souvenirs for sale. Be there to see it if you are in the area.
Not really much to see here. We had 5 mins in the main entrance wth our city guide. Free to get in and have a quick peek.

A beautiful building dating from 1891. Inside, a glass roof with an iron frame by the Gustave Effel workshop. Two souvenir shops on either side. The telephone booths have been converted into ATMs for several banks.
This building is worth a view, situated next to Notre dame, great architecture and history to it. It is still a post office.
Nice building that is currently in use but a five minute visit is enough, lots of tourists and souvenir shops. Absolutely not a must in this city!
It's one big tourist trap,  perfect only to walk by and take a picture then move on. Just like most of Ho Chi minh city "siagon " could be alot better but missing it's own charisma.

I'm not sure I understand the fuss here...It's a nice building but this seems to be something of a created tourist trap, and is heaving. However to be fair, I have not researched the building so may be missing some historic significance! I'd advise you pop your head in if you are passing, but don't make a special trip.
Like stepping back in time, lovely building and opportunity to buy souvenirs or book tickets if you wish.
It's a nice building but not much more to it.  Post office with a few tourist shops selling the usual trinkets.
I started to feel like it was getting in the way of post office business.

Not sure why this is a tourist attraction. We got to see it on one of the local tours. We feigned interest until we couldn't pretend anymore then moved on. It's a nice looking building, but that's it. Feel free to walk by it and keep moving. Yes it's a post office. Yes it's a building. Yes that's all there is to it.

This is a functional post office and you can get special service like translations - from the guy on the bench. He should be the attraction, not the post office itself. Beside being a normal post office it's just a tourist trap.
I thought it would feel like a museum... but

The post office was a mighty fine post office, but that is all it was. Thought there was going to be more to see, I understand the history of the post office but that is not really displayed. Good place to exchange money though
It was OK, nothing spectacular, nice to take a couple of pictures and continue with your  adventure!!!
Went there right after visiting Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral. Its just right beside the church. Visit it. But dont expect much.
I mean really? What all the hype about? Just walk past the place and take a photo. I mean really it's just a post office
Not much to say here. It's a post office. Admire the architecture...but it really is just a post office.
It didn't particularly impress us, and it was too crowded. I wouldn't go back; I wouldn't waste my time visiting this place.
I went there to send some postcards to my home country. The stamp was very cheap but took more than a month to reach...
Nice architecture from the outside but really it's just a post office, and a working post office at that.
Huge, historical and unconventional office for sure. However not a great place to visit. If you have spare time can visit.
Small ex post office from days of the French now a tourist attraction with a number of souvenir shops.
Apparently one of the most important sights to see.  Undoubtedly a beautiful building but when we went inside it was nothing special at all. Just a post office!
Very busy place, lots of people and hawkers out front. Great look if you like architecture, the money changer inside was so rude thou so get money elsewhere

Visited the central post office in 2016. It was really beautiful with Europe style architectural but had worst experience ever during the trip. My friend and I were trying to buy the postcard which comes in a package of 10. When we were about to pay the money, the staff counted again and took one piece of postcard out telling us it only comes in 9 per package. But then we tried to count another package and found that they all come in 10 pieces per package. We told the staff properly that she might be wrong about this. But she rudely tall back to us and told us we can only have 9 if we want or we can just leave. She put back our postcard and simply walk away. Hope they can do something on their enployee morale.
Bice building from the outside but very expensive souvenirs inside. Very touristy. Not my cup of tea.
From the outside, this is a magnificent building in a very beautiful location. Inside, there is nothing to see except two souvenir shops selling exactly the same thing.
Sure, it's nice here, but there wasn't much to see, I think. I don't know what to say.....
Not much to see here don't bother as there are much other interesting places to visit in Ho Chi Minh

Its a post office. The building is large and old but the architecture isn't anything that blew my mind especially with the Cathedral across the street. If you already want to see the cathedral it is nothing to cross the street and peak inside.

It's like the Merlion, a place people take commemorative photos with as proof they've been to Ho Chi Minh City. It's still a post office, after all. Located in the heart of Ho Chi Minh City's landmarks, it's worth a visit. However, since it's still a post office, don't have overly high expectations.
This is a cool historic building, but it's like a ten minute stop, tops. Don't spend a lot of time here. Not a destination and not a place you should feel bad if you miss.
It took all of ten minutes to go through here and they had vendors trying to sell you stuff.  It had old telephone booths and it was a nice building, but I thought it was a waste of my time.
The outside of the building is beautiful, Inside it is difficult to find the actual post office among all of the stalls selling tourist knickknacks. It's basically just become another tourist market.
Large, somewhat older building in the city. A working post office. And many tourists taking a look. You'll be back outside in about 15 minutes, because there really isn't much to see.

The facade is the most interesting thing about this building. Although of historical significance, it is still a functioning post office , and also contains therein a large souvenir shop selling wares found stodg other shops and markets in the city.  A good photo opportunity on the wish to other attractions, I guess.
It's definitely a nice building but not a must see. However, it's close to the "cluster" of other attractions so worth a visit when you're nearby.

Still operating as a post office, and a stunning building in its own right. Renovated around 2014 it is a major landmark. Very close to the Notre Dame and Book Street. Touristy area to the front of the building but an intriguing history.Plenty of souvenirs if that is your thing.
This is the historic post office building. Typical colonial architecture. Inside now only has a small postal counter but it is still worth popping to have a look at.

From outside, it looks like a railway station. Interiorly it looks like a Post Office with appropriate counters around the interior walls. Filling the central court, is a mass of souvenir stalls from which to pick what you may wish to post.
Lovely architecture, but it’s hard to see because of how crowded it is inside and out.Many stores inside, wasn’t what I was expecting.

Saigon Central Post Office is worth a quick stop for its impressive colonial architecture. The interior is airy and elegant, but the numerous souvenir stalls detract from the experience and feel fairly generic. Not a top must-see in Ho Chi Minh City, but if you’re already nearby, it’s worth popping in for a short visit.
Impressive building but insanely busy and a bit of a tourist trap. Would put a visit here on the maybe pile. Unless you really want to send a postcard, then it's a must.
This is a working post office but also a tourist attraction. Nice old building, was busy with tourists getting pictures done, gift shops and hawkers. Not quite sure what else it offers

A symbol of the city, in the French colonial style, is a reminder of bygone times when it was used as a communications center. The interior is similar to a railway station with the barrel vault and many iron decorative elements. Today, although still in operation, it hosts many commercial activities for tourists.

A French colonial beauty designed by Gustave Eiffel (yes, that Eiffel), where you can still send a postcard under its grand arched ceilings—or just snap a selfie pretending you’ve time-traveled to 1880s Paris. Don't expect much more than architecture, postage and a souvenir shop. Across the street is the Notre-Dame Cathedral of Saigon, though it’s currently closed to visitors for renovations until 2027.
Nice building dating from the French colonialist era. Fun to see.Telegraph booths of the time are still in place.
There isn't actually a lot to see here unless you know a lot about architecture.  The place is full of small vendors selling postcards for you to post and tourists
It's just about looking at the buildings; upon closer inspection, they're not particularly exquisite, and some are even dilapidated. There
Tourist post where many come to send a postcard to their loved ones , here you can also find various things to buy

The post office was extremely busy.  They have card of all kinds to buy. I purchased one and mailed it to the U.S.A. it is February  now and the recipient never received it. It was very costly to mail it. Very disappointed  😞

Is very nice buiding, but not organised, you must ask and inform haw to proceed to send a post card, In my opinion you must go and visit itTry to raad something about it on google before you go there!!Shame for very very bad way to organise the place
Architecture quite interesting and you can send a map to your loved ones. Stay 10min nothing special in all, food nothing when you come by you can enter.

It is considered a site of interest in the city, so it deserves a quick pass. They are very proud of this central office that serves the whole country, sorting the correspondence.Old French building keeps inside old clocks and telephone boxes of the past. The presence of souvenir shops suggests that it is a very touristic place, but it is really unusual to visit it while the employees are at work.
Very touristy, beautiful architecture, nothing special. Notre Dame Cathedral closed for renovations, so could not visit 2 together. Hardly worth the effort.

A beautiful building in Saigon but too many tourists, and way too many social medial personalities, like everywhere in SE Asia."Why so many girls or young couples dressing up?""...they'll post it on Facebook and social media..."Across the street, is their Notre Dame but is under renovation, as well.Next to it (northwest) is the lovely Book Street, and the yellow building locates at 22 Gia Long Street, that is southeast of the post office, was where the last American helicopter left.
A publib building you will find in the tousands n France. The only point worth mentioning is the fact that this one has been build in Saigon. Then there is a picture of Ho Chi Minh. That is all.
Imposing building that presages an intense history, but today is in decline, with few points of interest and very touristic.
Particular structure for a post office but that post office no longer has anything. There are only shops with souvenirs
Nice old building full of souvenir shops. Worth 10 minutes to look around inside but the fact that this is the #3  attraction in HCMC explains why this is a place that I would not want to see again.

Im going to be honest, I dont really see what the hype was about.... It was nice, French architecture was nice.  It was certainly busy!   Dont go to the tourist shops there, more expensive and super busy. We did send a postcard, it took about 3 weeks to arrive to the UK.  Happy to say that I have been there and ticket it off

There was a bit of a queue at the entrance. Apparently people love coming here. There are also many nice stores here. Those who want to buy souvenirs should definitely check it out. I loved it so much that I bought a few gifts for myself.
Did enjoy being able to purchase some postcards and send them back home.  Other than that just a quick look around.  The people were friendly.

I have never really been to a post office so it was nice. I went and sat on the wooden bench while my mom looked around. nothing special tbh. just a tickmark since everyone visits this place and its close to the palace.
The French Colonial building is worth a look at for its architecture, murals, and old telephone booths (used before phones in homes became ubiquitous).

Apparently it came 2nd in the Most Beautiful Post Offices of the World (Algiers won).No doubt it is a nice building but didn't have the wow factor for me.  Always groups posing outside so hard to get your own photo and even busier inside - there are now lots of little tourist souvenir shops each side as you go in and it is still a working post office.Nice to see it, especially loved the old-fashioned telephone booths, and you can also see Notre Dame Cathedral being restored across the street, but not worth making a special trip to here.
When visiting Ho Chi Minh City, if it is open you can visit the Post Office. French colonial style. Interesting building.Still working todayWorth a quick visit but nothing specia
nice enough building, but not much more that a 10-15mins pit stop whilst in the city. I wouldn't make a big effort to seek this place out

Saigon's Central Post Office is said to be a famous landmark in the city. It is located to the right of the city's Notre Dame Cathedral. The 19th century colonial building, designed by Gustave Eiffel, is interesting to look at and why not visit. You can buy, write and send postcards right there. Personally, I found the souvenir stands inside the building more interesting. There is a huge selection of souvenirs and at reasonable prices.
It's pretty but invaded by souvenir shops made in China, the old telephone booths remain, it will take you 10 minutes!!!!

It is indeed a beautiful building from the colonial era, but it has become very touristy. Next to the post office and the old telephone booths there are only souvenir shops at overpriced prices. Opposite, the cathedral is under renovation, therefore “packed”.

10 minutes will suffice here. Yes some nice wood etc inside but otherwise mainly dedicated to souvenir selling. Surprised at its ranking to be honest. I would put the independence palace the Ao line show at the Opera House and the fine arts museum way ahead of this. So if short on time prioritise those. You will understand Vietnam better for doing so

It's in the same location as Notre Dame Cathedral. While looking at the cathedral, I looked to the right and saw the Central Post Office.At that time, while walking, I only saw the outside of the Central Post Office.
I understand that the building was built by an important architect but nothing specialA quick visit is more than enough
It is an interesting building and spectacular as working post offices go . Not sure why it is so popular though. Very crowded.
Great to have seen. You can just walk in. There are some shops with souvenirs, we didn't look there.
Since the post office is still working, it’s an interesting visit as most buildings of this type are museums. Worth a look

Definitely historic building where inside you will find the old telephone booths.Now full of stalls.The nice thing you can do is send a postcard from counter 3 to a nostalgic person who will be happy to receive it.Outside you will find the Notre Dame Cathedral under restoration and closed during this period.
Still a functioning post office, nice stop of photo outside and inside the building, lots of vendors selling postcards and other souvenirs.
You should go in the morning from 6:00 a.m. to 7:00 a.m. to have an empty space to take beautiful pictures. The remaining times are very crowded and stuffy.
Beautiful building that was once a post office. It now houses souvenir shops. You can see everything inside in about 5 minutes. From the outside, it looks impressive. Nice if you walk past.

We stopped here twice, the first time because it was a “must do” and the second time to try to understand why. I understand the appeal of the architecture, but that’s really about it. If you are in the area checking by other attractions, stop by, but don’t have too high of expectations.

Formerly a post office, this building is now simply a tourist attraction. Well-preserved in the French colonial style and retaining its original colors, it's attractive but lacks much appeal except for the numerous tourist shops inside. It stands directly opposite Notre Dame Cathedral (currently under restoration and shrouded in scaffolding).

It is beautiful on the inside and is a working post office. Many tourists buy old stamps and even send themselves postcards. If you like that you could try it. I didn't see the charm in that. I mostly took photos and we saw some souvenirs. The pricing and authenticity was not clear if it was right. But they look pretty.It is a bustling place that you may want to experience, esp if you are not from an otherwise populous place.
I came here to buy postcards because they're cheapest here.

I would like to hire whatever PR person got this marketed as a tourist stop on every list. There is nothing wrong with it, but the architecture isn't impressive, and in essence it's a place to send postcards and have the options to buy some souvenirs if you didn't manage it somewhere else.  Still, it's easy enough to visit, literally right beside the cathedral - and it is a really easy place to send your postcards. Also, bring your own pen, none are provided!Vietnam postcard selection overall is weak, but I wouldn't wait to buy them here like I did.  They have a quite poor selection, and pricier than most.

Was wondering ever since this place came up on the  list what’s so great about this place. On entering we realised it is a steel curated European architecture on display which one can size up in 5 min in desperate photography.After that you are shopping stamps, coins, some decorated lacquerware and if in a generous mood to splurge silver jewellery interlaced with mother of pearl and cut American diamonds.So this can be a max 5 min stop or 1 hr depending on your shopping appetite or maybe a skip from the schedule as Notre Dame next to it is also on a restoration till 2027.

When France invaded Vietnam and later colonized the region as Indochina, their architecture influence went into building this in the 1800’s. This French structure has a beautiful ceiling. Tourists can mail a postcard from here. It’s actually a functioning post office. Additionally Vietnamese citizens went here to pick up pension as well as filing other governmental applications. Free admission. Located across from the Notre Dame Cathedral of Saigon. Unfortunately the church is under construction covered with scaffolding.
Interesting if you have somebody with you telling you the history and the stories. Looks like a tourist place, but still a working post office.

A visit to the Post Office as part of a quick stop, after the War Remnants Museum. Interesting architecture and it is still a working post office. Visit if you have some time to fill your itinerary otherwise you can give it a miss.

The architecture of the building is beautiful, but there isn't much to see here besides that. I stopped here as part of a tour and it only took a few minutes to walk around the outside and inside. Worth a stop but don't plan to spend any extended amount of time here since there simply isn't much here.

It is a stop you should do while in HCM but if you miss it or have other more important things to do in your itinerary…you are not missing too much.  The place is smaller of what you can imagine by the pictures.  They sell souvenirs and there is a booth to book the hop on hop off.I was able to buy my Vietnamese hat…. Bit overpriced at $130k but… I got it her 😎

Nice to walk past quickly, but otherwise not very special. The colorful building is nice to take a quick photo of, but inside it is mainly a combination of souvenir shops and a post office that is still operational.
Maybe I've missed something, it looks nice from the outside and it's ok in the inside, nothing too amazing.
This post office is still active with remarkable architecture both inside and out.  Located across the street from the Notre Dam Cathedral has gift shops inside and is very busy.
Went as part of a tour, interesting place but would not go out of my way to see it. The cathedral is right next to it but was closed due to renovation.
Worth popping in if you’re passing. There’s a lot of souvenirs for sale here if that’s what you’re looking for. Still being used as a post office.

I went here because of this post office is between Notre Dame Cathedral of Saigon and Ho Chi Minh City’s Book street. Lovely building is Western style. Also I was lucky because of I saw Miss universe 2022 she went there too. lol
One of the few remaining French era buildings still standing in HCMC, vaguely interesting but not worth the trip.
The reason the post office ended up here as a tourist attraction is probably due to its older architectural style. It's nice, but you're done there very quickly.
Not really much going on here, just lots of tourists taking photos and grabbing post cards or souvenirs
A beautiful building. Inside, the old-time atmosphere is preserved, including telephone booths and a beautiful French mosaic floor. Lots of tourists.

I bought 10 bookmarks and 3 postcards , but the cashier refuse to give me extra bags as those are gifts to people. She insisted only one when i bought so many? then i ask her, if someone else buy 1 bookmark, will you give 1 bag for that? then she unwilling gave me 5 bags only.
An okay looking building. Inside is okay to look at and has a big picture of HCM. Nothing special though and only worth a quick look if it’s on your way

What am I not seeing ? This needs 5 minutes. Take a photo of the exterior - very impressively designed building. Go inside and take a photo of the roof. Avoid the souvenir shops and get out. Job done. Was so disappointed.

Yes it’s famous , yes lots of tourists visit there - a nice architectural hall that is pleasant to eyes, not worth more than 10 min spending here.We bought some magnets and postcards here- reasonable price
This is a place to have a look at when in the area but I would not make a special journey. There are some souvenir shops here but prices seemed high.
Its an old post office that for us was not of any real interest, some goods on sale outside were of more interest to my wife

This is our second visit to Vietnam and the decline in postage use is noticable. The Central post office is a mere shadow of its former glory, many of the counters now closed and much space taken over by souvenier sellars.

This is one of those sites where if you bring your family, after about 10 minutes everyone is standing around asking what's next.  This functioning post office with gift shop is no doubt a great work of architecture. But it you are not into architecture, then not sure of the point for you. Now that being said, as you are sending around outside in the square, off to the left you will notice the building in the iconic US evacuation photo where people of trying to get on a US helicopter.  The CIA building is still standing and well that is pretty interesting to see from the square of the post office.

This place is one of popular photo spot in Ho chi minh. Better come in the afternoon. When you got lucky you'd see some pretty vietnamese women dressing in ao dai (traditional dress). They'd happily have some pictures with you :)
I have called this location a minimum of 15 times TODAY. There is no reason staff should be ignoring calls this often. No one is that busy.
Interesting building, but really only good for souvenirs. Notre Dame, across the street, was covered in scaffolding.
Nice building, still in use. You can see local people using the post office while visiting the small market inside
A beautiful building centrally located in District 1 in downtown Ho Chi Minh City. Beautiful architecture and worth a visit when you are nearby!

The colonial building is wonderfully positioned near the famous church - the exterior of the building us truly beautiful to look at, but there is not much to really see or do inside, its all about he exterior and ten mins of photos can cover that. some of the city tour buses start off at this location and its proximity to the hotel Intercontinental makes it an easy walk, also close to a lot of embassies.

Normally packaged with crowds, locals and tourist, today during Covid time almost deserted like nobody knows it. Notre-Dame church ⛪️ in scaffolding suit next by and square just few people. Interesting 🤔 time
Just stopped by to catch a glimpse of the architecture. There were shops with souvenirs on either sides.

I came here because we want to visit the Cathedral which is under construction on Feb 2020. It mostly souvenir shops, post card, travel agent. The telephone box is empty and we just take some good picture in there.
Lovely to see this beautiful building is so well used! Well worth a visit when staying in Ho Chi Min City

Still a working post office, but it offered that inspirational colonial feel. I would recommend visiting if you are in the area, but I wouldn't go out of your way to come here. Allot of people write a post card and then post it on.

The Saigon Central Post Office is located slightly east of the city center in Ho Chi Minh City. It's a colonial-style building situated east of Saigon Cathedral. A legacy of the French colonial era, it remains operational and is also a popular tourist attraction. The interior design and decorations are magnificent.

We went into the Saigon central post office (Bun Dien) during our walk downtown. It is right across the street from the Cathedral. It is a colonial architecture yellow building with a few brass looking statues outside. The grounds have been well maintained. The building was constructed in the 19th century when Vietnam was part of French Indochina.The inside is still a functioning post office but has a bunch of small vendors selling small things. Nothing crazy. It was free to go in. We spent about 15mins at most here. I personally wouldn’t say it’s a must-see but if you are in the neighbourhood it is a good short stop.
While it is one of Ho Chi Minh City's representative colonial buildings, it's nothing particularly outstanding or distinctive; it's quite ordinary. If I had
One of the must-see tourist attractions in Ho Chi Minh City,

If you're visiting the sites of Ho Chi Minh City, you'll probably come to this area. Next to the Central Post Office is Notre Dame Cathedral and nearby is the Opera House. So it's worth going into the Post Office for a quick visit. Built by the French in the 19th century, it is "architecturally" interesting. Inside are a variety of gift shops, and there is also a the large picture of Ho Chi Minh on the back wall. Take a quick look.
Was disappointed in the post office which was situated in a train station. It was a tourist trap and sold a lot of touristy memorabilia.However, the building was worth a quick picture.
Worth a look if you are passing.  Great building but not much more to look at.  Just full of tacky gift shops.
The building is superb but filled with shops selling tourist trinkets, which spoils the monument.
More attractive on the outside than the inside.

A visit to the Post Office is a must, as it retains much of its old character. It is of course a must visit building so it is full of tourists, as we were, so taking photos was a challenge.We were advised to eat at the restaurant there, and had planned to take dinner, but on arrival noted that it was a best a hamburger restaurant, not bad if that's what you want but we wanted a more authentic local dinner.We did take some refreshment which was nicely served, by interested and helpful staff
Apart from the exterior, nothing particularly remarkable. The
Beautiful place, but the vendors are sly, even those selling stamps... Be careful. The interior of the building is worth seeing.

We went here to send post cards to friends.  It is a historic post office with stores inside re:kiosk. Just a little noisy. People are friendly.  You will old paintings and sculptures dating back before the war. Interesting map of old Saigon (Ho Chi Minh City) can be found. History buffs - a good place to visit.
It’s just a big post office.  The building is mildly interesting but not so much.  Cathedral opposite is more interesting.
This isn't something that will take long.  You go in to see the building and can shop the many little stalls selling souvenirs .
Spent time on a guided tour visiting Post Office. Impressive building but not a visit that will stay in memory for long. Been there done it. Move on.
We were here during our stay in Saigon. It's nice to see, like the unique phone booths and writing desks. But here too, cheap plastic souvenir shops are thriving. It's not fun anymore.
The exterior of the building reflects the colonial period. The interior gives a glimpse of the atmosphere that must have prevailed at the time.
At first glance, it's clear that whoever designed this structure loved iron; in fact, it was designed by Eiffel.

The main post office is definitely worth a visit if you have a few hours to spare in Saigon. However, it's rather unspectacular. Based on the pictures we saw, we expected much more. It was also very crowded.

An old post office still in operation, where guides take you, but you can do without them. Notable features include the structure and the maps stuck to the walls, which are barely visible due to the chaos. The place has shops full of tourist paraphernalia.
Expected 'wow' having been told this place was something special but was really disappointed....it's just a building with an arched ceiling. Can't really see what the fuss is about.
Nothing exceptional, just lots of people and souvenirs to buy. I admit I wasted my time...

This century-old building, with its golden exterior walls, is a remnant of the French colonial era. Stepping inside, the high ceilings create a grand atmosphere, and importantly—this century-old post office is still in operation! Visitors can buy postcards and send them home. The corridors on either side of the lobby on the first floor house souvenir shops, convenient for those looking to buy souvenirs. However, the abundance of souvenir counters gives it a strong touristy feel, which doesn't quite match the century-old architecture. That said, the Central Post Office is right next to the Notre Dame Cathedral and can be visited at the same time!
It is worth a quick look inside but is full of tourist cheap stuff. Most interesting is outside where the statue of the revolutionary heroes is right next to the McDonalds.
Maybe I didn't read about the history of this place and that's why it just didn't get to me. Nothing special. But many tourists!
Opposite the Notre Dame Cathedral. Nice architecture and a selection of tourist gift shops. Still operational.

Impressive colonial building and operating as a post office with tourist information and gift stalls. A lovely place to spend 30 minutes looking around and to send a few postcards.It is next to the cathedral which is currently closed.

I wouldn't make a special visit but if your passing call into see this still operational post office. It is beautiful but you won't be there for more than 5 minutes(apparently there is a guide there normally who will give you some background information but he wasn't there when we visited so can't t comment if this is true.)

This grand building, which could easily stand in one of France's prefectures, is a relic of the colonial era and an iconic symbol of Saigon. It's a must-see when visiting the city, but you don't need to spend much time there.
Overrated but worth a quick look. Can mail postcards, etc quite easily . Two historical maps on the walls are interesting.

Sadly the place has become a tourist shop more than a working post office. Yes it is still a working post office however it is teaming with tourist who come into the main area buy trinkets, take their selfies and leave.
The oldest building in Saigon (1886) is not particularly exciting for a European. The murals and the market inside are interesting.
Interesting architecture from the French colonial period, still a post office but now also filled with garish souvenir stalls
A pleasant, timeless place. Stop by and take the opportunity to send a postcard to your friends.
As a Brit we have so many historic buildings and so we didn't really think the Post Office was anything special but it was interesting and free to visit

It's supposed to be a colonial-style building, but I find it strange that it's a tourist spot. It felt like they made it a tourist attraction simply because there wasn't much else to see. (Sorry for the bluntness.)
The main post office built by the French. A fine building worth a look if you happen to be around the corner. Nowadays, Uncle Ho greets visitors.

The post office is situated across the road from Norte Dame so, if you visit one, you'll visit the other.It's exterior is pure French colonial and the interior interesting enough. Our guide said it was designed by Gustav Eiffel's company rather than the man himself but hey......

The largest and most beautiful post office in the heart of District 1, next to Notre Dame Cathedral, however, there's nothing particularly special about it except for sending letters, postcards, taking photos, and buying souvenirs.
Colonial-style post office, where there are people who obviously send mail and write postcards but it is more of a tourist market than anything else.
This might have more value if you took a guided tour, I don’t know. I went in by myself: the architecture is impressive but there’s nothing but souvenirs stalls.
We posted some cards, ok place for 15 minutes stay. Postcards inside the building is much cheaper than outside.
Yes, the architecture is beautiful and it's an enjoyable experience. Unfortunately, this is marred by the many souvenir shops.
Not much to see inside the building. No entrance fee. You could spend up to 30 minutes there. It's across the road from the cathedral

In this hectic city, the building is particularly attractive from the outside and has been renovated. Inside, it houses a post office and a few tourist souvenir shops. Overall, it's a worthwhile stop on any city tour or walk.

The building itself looks amazing, very french, very regal. However u are only in the building for around 20 minutes and then you run out if stuff to look at, good for a picture but not alot to it other than that!
It remains a 'not to be missed' building, but I remember the time when this was still a real post office; now the 'commercialization' mars the nostalgic atmosphere.
It is indeed a post office, but most of the visitors were tourists. There are shops on either side of the entrance, so you can buy souvenirs. Please be careful of pickpockets while you are visiting.

Across the street from Notre Dame Cathedral and beside book street, you don’t loose anything coming into the post office. Nice looking from the outside, with interesting details inside, it seemed not so well preserved. I was missing the charm what I believe it had in the past...

Didn't spend too much time here, was on way to museum so popped in. interesting to see the old architecture together with the old man hand writing the postcards/letters. Interesting but wouldn't travel far to see it but if in vicinity or on way to war museum it is worth a quick stop
Nice architecture but not that amazing. It was nice to be able to send a post card from there. I would not spend more than 30 minutes there
Convenient location for also visiting Notre Dame but it only takes a couple minutes for an outside picture and an inside picture.  Nothing else worth seeing.

The Central post office was designed by Gustave Eiffel - the same architect of the Eiffel tower. It was built during the French colonial period and is probably close to a century old.  Enter the post office and you'll notice the immense ceilings, giving you the feeling of a train station and you can't miss the portrait of Ho Chi Minh and the nice phone booths have been turned into ATM machine booths. They sell postcards, stamps and many souvenirs very cheap or at least not overpriced.

Interesting visit - crowded with tourists! There are opportunities to buy postcards and stamps and these were delivered to destinations in U.K. and Canada. When I posted from UK to Vietnam cards did not arrive! - so the post office seems reliable.Also some stalls from which to buy the typical souvenirs, which were similar prices to other places/markets.

I didn’t really get why this was a tourist destination. I think maybe due to limited other options. You walk past loads when walking round the city so by all means bob in but don’t make a special effort.

This is a beautiful building! Strong French influence. It's still operational which for visitors from the UK is an added bonus as ours are being closed on a daily basis. The old telephone booths are lovely to see and the detail in the food structure is glorious, well worth a visit on any tour of the city!
The building itself is a landmark. However within is full of touristic tat. There does however remain a working post office, but for how much longer?

I took photos of the exterior and interior of the historic building. It was interesting to see what post offices in other countries are like while they're open for business. Then I took a stroll around the crowded souvenir shops. But that's about it, typical tourist spot experience (lol).
If you are near the place it would be nice to visit it, nothing special! A nice post office with souvenir shops, you can even send a letter if you want!

Looks like an old European train station. Even has phone booths left. Nice to see to complement the overall HCMC picture but I didn't spend more than 5 min. It is close to Notre Dame which  can make a decent combination.

Just across the busy road from Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral is the Central Post Office,  A lovely stop on a tour of Saigon as it gave a bit of insight into the everyday life of the Saigonese people.  When we visited there were a few wedding parties having their wedding photos taken (apparently this is a favourite spot for this and pictures are taken in advance of the wedding).  Lovely to see everyone in their finest.  The architecture of the building itself is interesting and there are some shops for souvenirs.

The building has it's very own charm and is worth a visit for all architecture interested people. Located conveniently next to the church and just a stone throw away from the opera and all other main sights in town it's worth stopping by on the way to the book street or in between some other inner city visits. That said, the inside and inhabitants aint living up to it's great looks. While the post is still using the building and a few counters are used as intended the majority of the building is packed with souvenir shops and stalls selling the usual products apart from old stamps and collectives. Some of the old features are not in use, up keeping inside is a bit mediocre - overall the full potential isn't used at all and the building suffers from all the vendors. Still, when in the area stop by for a look before going ahead to other places

Worth visiting once. However, there are limited things to do there. Mainly just snapping a photo and sending a postcard internationally. I had to use google to find out more about the significance of the place.
This post office was built during the French colonial era. It's located across the street from Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral.
Looks great from the outside, but awful inside! Beautiful architecture from the outside, but inside it's basically just a souvenir shop! What a shame.

What a beautiful building designed by the same architecture who designed Eiffel tower. From mosaic tiles to arch roof its a classic masterpiece of a french asian styled building. A must visit in Ho Chi Minh city.
Not much to see, it’s a lovely building and free to go inside. Take a look if it’s on your way somewhere but I wouldn’t make a special trip.
Nothing exciting, just another crowded post office.  Have some souvenirs to purchase apart from that nothing else.
I liked everything except the huge number of tourists. I don't understand how the postal workers manage to work in such conditions.
It's a nice building, but nothing special. If you're already visiting Notre Dame Cathedral, you can also see the main post office. It's not worth coming here just for this building, though.

The architecture makes it well worth a visit if you are in the area. It's quite interesting inside with all the hustle and bustle of the post office counters and tourist trinkets for sale.I liked the row of old phone boxes, looked like something out of a cold war film.15 minute tops.
A really nice old building from when Vietnam was under .. French rule.
Nothing much to look at inside. All souvenirs shops occupy a major portion of the central hall. Can just click a few pics nothing else. Rather visit the books stores lane adjacent to it.
The Post Office is a beautiful building, inside and out and is worth a visit.  Lots of souvenirs can be bought there - including Vietnamese stamps, coins and other novelty items.
A tourist spot to see the old Saigon map and the new ho chi minh city map on the two sides of the wall at the main hall. Used as a place for tourists to buy souvenirs.
beautiful old building, but now used as a 'upscale' market.  All the items you could buy in the market are for sale here at slightly higher prices.  Nothing special but worth stopping in for a visit.

Old style post office with spectacular preserved ceiling and front. Still functioning as a post office, and we were privileged to meet the last public writer from pre-revolution times. Worth seeing if you're in the area, a link to the Saigon of the past.
Not particularly interesting.  Better sights to visit in my opinion.  War Remnants, Chi Chi tunnels, and Independence Palace for example.
Nothing much to do around here other than a taste of history and taking some photos which took less than 30 minutes
Interesting french styled architecture. This is really a shopping arcade now. Close to the cathedral and the former US Embassy.
The place has interesting decor, but nothing special. The exchange rate there is terrible, and the little shops sell everything at very inflated prices.
A working post office with Interesting architecture and small, tourist market. Not disabled friendly.

The building is very interesting architecturally and I was able to mail my postcard. I wouldn’t come over to see this building by itself unless there was something that brought you to this side of town.
As expected of a famous spot, it was quite crowded in the evening. I just took some pictures and left as soon as possible.

A good place to visit during a day walking, full of interesting bits and pieces. Good if you have an interest in the postal service in Vietnam.As usual the cheap trinkets for sale along with some interesting pieces.
Was a bit disappointed to see, it is basically a big souvenir shop. The building is nice, but it is also crowded.
Popped in for a quick look.  Architecture is really interesting and there are souvenir shops on both sides of the building.  We only stayed 15 min which was enough to see the highlights.
There's not much going on here, but it seems to be a central thing to see when you are on a tour of Ho Chi Minh City.

The building is OK, French style nothing special. The queues to buy stamps are infernal. And if you try taking photos inside there's gonna be a guy coming to you and scolding you for some reason. Well, it is next to the Cathedral so it is easy to visit but a little overrated even if designed by the Eiffel guy.
It's a pretty post office, but to call it a monument worth visiting?

This place is well located and not difficult to find. It is still functioning as a post office and there isn't much explanation of the history of the place.There are a few souvenir shops within the place, however the prices are not attractive, there are better places to buy souvenirs...

The structure highlights French architecture which is very predominant in the entirety of Ho Chi Minh, or even Vietnam itself. It could be a bit boring due to the reason that you won't do anything there besides taking pictures and buying souvenirs. The aren't any tours provided by the administration that we know of either. The best thing ought to do is purchase post cards.
It's a nice place to stop by, but it was pretty full of counters selling all sorts of souvenirs, so the original purpose of the place has become a bit secondary.
Great example of architecture.  It is interesting to see.Lots of tourist shops too.Lots of people there.
Many small shops are now located inside the prior post office but one can still admire its architecture and paintings

We came here as part of a tour at the end of a long day.  It’s exactly as it looks, a busy post office with a lovely roof and a great building across the road.  If you are into quiet activities and or architecture seek it out.  Otherwise it would be a good stop in a walking tour but if short on time I wouldn’t stress about missing it.  My younger two teens stayed in the car for this one.

It's an impressive building that attracts many tourists. Right in front of it is Notre Dame Cathedral, another famous tourist attraction. I was staying at the nearby InterContinental Saigon, so I passed by it many times, and it was always packed with tourists. The interior of the building is also beautiful and well worth seeing. Many tourists were sending postcards from here.
Interesting visit, nice building from Colonial times but extreme tourism.  Shame about all the little stalls selling souvenirs.
Another great architecture building and its decoration inside the post office. Mild steel structure and high ceiling with motifs floor tiles could be found all around the building.
Nice architecture and worth seeing, but not considerable as a top tourist destination - there are no real explanations as to the significance of the site

But aside from the small shops, there isn't much to see or do. Of course, if you appreciate the architecture and design of the building, you'll surely have an interesting time. If, like me, you're ignorant of the subject, then there's not much to do or appreciate.

We stopped by this post office today. All elderly foreigners. We're standing there taking a photo of a call booth with a Moscow clock. An English-speaking tourist emerges from the booth and tells his companion that when he called from such a booth during the Soviet era, not only the person he was calling was listening, but also the KGB. His companion rolls her eyes in surprise and indignation. I lean toward her and clearly pronounce in English: "The KGB is still listening to you." And then I walk away. A silent scene with gaping mouths. Curtain.

It's an iconic building in the city that's definitely worth a visit, even if just a quick one. And while you're there, you can also take the opportunity to send a postcard to your family from Vietnam.
Unfortunately there are alot of vendors inside which takes away from the atmosphere - the outside is pretty.  If you don't add this to your itinerary I don't think you are missing anything.

It stands out as a distinctive yellow French-style building compared to the surrounding buildings. Inside, there are many souvenirs for sale; besides the central sales area, there are other options along the side walkways. Sending postcards from here is also more reassuring than dropping them in a mailbox.
Other than taking photos & admiring some french architecture, there isn't much to do. I literally spent 10 minutes max here.

Since the Notre Dame Cathedral was closed for renovations, they brought us to the Post Office...while it is an interesting place of history; I felt like we were killing time there and did not need to spend more than a few minutes to see the interior.

Went here for photos. A historical building architecture. It still operated as a post office but since the building is one of the tourist attractions, a little bit crowd with tourists. There's a souvenir shop inside too.
Fascinating old building, in the style of a railway station. We'll worth a visit if in the area. Opposite Notre Sam Church.

Highlights for me were architecture, outdoor statues celebrating both men and women, and central interior. After that it is mostly souvenir shops. Is across the street from the also French colonial Notre Dame cathedral.

This is a really attractive piece of architecture and it does conjure images of a bygone era. The outside was far more attractive to me than the inside but the interior is very pleasant as well. There is not much to see though. Maybe stamp collectors will be interested in the exhibits but although it seems to operate as a regular post office, much of the space is given over to souvenirs. I hate to be cynical but although it’s a very pleasant building, I can’t justify giving it four or five stars. If so, how would you rate the truly great sites of the planet?

The central Post Office has been taken over by sellers of cheap and overpriced souvenirs. Except for the central area still selling a range of stamps the rest is tacky items that you can get cheaper elsewhere. Definitely go there to take a few photos but it’s lost the chalk it once had.
A very pretty building, an Aladdin's cave of temple merchants.
Saigon is not a city to conquer you.Its predominant issue is the traffic chaos.Central Post office is just a nice building in the same square with the catholic cathedral.

Right next to Notre Dame so worth a look whole you're there. Nice ceiling. Small selection of tourist stuff inside as well as vendors hawking outside. Postcards, pop up cards etc etc stamps and mailing box inside. Bit crowded but has to be done to send letters.

Looks like a train station entrance but inside is very good. Again tourist overload with stalls etc but the building itself is good to see. The surrounding area is worth a stroll especially the cathedral and book stalls.
Nothing special to be visited here. The coolest thing it's probably Ho standing up there over the wall looking at you and the architecture of the place. Thaaat's about it
I understand that it was built by an important architect but nothing special.
I don't quite understand the hype surrounding the post office. It's just an old colonial building. With a little imagination, you can picture what it was like back then.

This is a beautiful building.  We were with our guide, and he introduced us to a gentleman who retired from the post office many years ago, but he still goes in every day to help illiterate people write letters.  The building is very well preserved.  The architecture is quite stunning.

It's hot inside the post office and there's no air conditioning. If you're not paying attention, you'll be targeted by pickpockets. Elderly people in particular should be careful. The post office service is poor. Well, I didn't expect much in Vietnam anyway.
There are lots of souvenir shops and currency exchange offices, and it's very spacious.
Interesting building with some souvenir shops, worth popping in if passing by or wanting to send postcards home
When you are here, you will certainly take a look at it. It gives a good impression of the positive contribution of the French in Indochina.
Designed by Eiffel, it's a beautiful structure worth a quick visit.
A must-see in Ho Chi Minh City is the Central Post Office, which, while still serving as a post office, has become a tourist attraction. Perhaps too touristy, with so many souvenir shops inside.
Saigon doesn't offer many attractions to visit. In other places, this structure wouldn't even be considered worthy of being part of the tourist circuit.
the architecture of this building blew my mind.  it was amazing, the people were interactive and informative.
This colonial building is a peace of colonial architecture. Is is till today used and the missing od air conditioning is not mitigated by the big windows and fan.
There are so many tourists, it's hard to believe it's a post office.
the building design is great but no need to spend time here shopping around, tourist stores and nothing special
The old post office, which is still a post office, is more of a place selling tourist trinkets than a historical site. It's pretty both inside and out, if you ignore the small vendors.

After seeing the cathedral was closed, we walked to the post office.There were lots of people standing/sitting outside the post office. When we walked inside, it was packed with tourists.It was packed like a shopping mall. We didn’t bother walking around and just left.I felt safe in Ho Chi Minh City. Although HCMC is a poor nation, the street peddlers never approached us.

I visited the post office after a recommendation from my tour guide. You can plan to visit this place for 30-45 mins. Not much you can do honestly, but it's a great place to shop some local vietnamese stuffs.Ps: next to this building you will find a hidden street with lots of book stores. If you re into bookz and cozy coffee shop to enjoy, its a Must place to go!!
We stopped by the post office when we were strolling through the city. Nothing special but you can have a look if you are close by. If you want to send letters or postcards - they got you covered...
Nice building but not sure why you have to go here, lots of souvenirs and travel agencies but really not so interesting
Genuinely old and has some character, worth calling in for a look around. Cathedral next door is also popular
Unlike the opera house and Notre dame church you can enter this building. Quite amazing inside and worth a stop.

The building itself, and especially the architecture, is very beautiful and certainly nice to visit if you are in the area, but I wouldn't come specifically for it. The interior is very touristy with souvenirs at relatively high prices.
Not impressed. Nice architecture, but nothing extraordinary. It is not worth travelling for. Been there, done that, bought that t-shirt
feels a bit overrated. normal post office.can buy souvenirs here, or send a postcardplace to escape from the heat

It's located right next to Notre Dame Cathedral, making it easily accessible for tourists. While not particularly spectacular, its cute yellow exterior makes it a good spot for souvenir photos. Souvenirs are available, but the quality is only average. It's probably best visited as a casual visitor rather than a major tourist attraction.

visited here - and it was wonderful to see. A lot of people around however DO NOT get money exchanged here as you will not get as much as you would if you went somewhere else (plus they took commission)definitely worth a look

Read reviews about this place so I decided to check it out for myself. Very hot inside mostly filled souvenirs that u can purchase everywhere. Title says it all. DO NOT BOOK A TOUR U WILL HATE URSELF FOR IT

Outward appears lovely but was dissapointed with the interior as a circular stall selling tourist tat seemed to be the vocal point in the middle. Its still a working post-office around the sides of the interior.
Well, it's a colonial building, interesting but nothing special. It's not the best thing in Saigon, but it's worth a visit, and it's free.
