Five minutes flat, just enough to say we saw the Saigon post office... but it hardly deserves any more attention. And to think the tourist guides rave about this place! It's still a colonial building, but the cheap shops piled high with mountains of cheap Chinese souvenirs, no thanks...

I went here because it was part of my tour itinerary. It's the biggest post office in Vietnam. This place is more suited to homesick backpackers who want to send a 'Wish you were here.' postcard to loved ones. I was there between 11-14 February which was basically the lunar new year for Vietnam, and the PO was closed. Unless you want to mail something, I think you can skip this place.
It's a nice building but essentially its just a post office. From here you can post letters and parcels all around the world, plus there are some ATMs and souvenier shops.
Nothig special! An old monuments where inside you can see....a souvenir shop,a stamp shop,a book shop with high price... but nothing more!
but besides an European like building with post inside, there's nothing to see. unless you are a post freak or architecture fan- you can skip it.
An old post office. A connoisseur might still find some architectural value in it, but I am no connoisseur and was back outside after three minutes.

nice architecture but as far as service from the post office this place is the worst.  Lots of waiting and no culture of making a queue.  people just pushing their way in font of you.  certainly the management could make little rope barriers so people would wait in a civilized line

Really not worth the visit unless you actually have mail to send. The building is very plain and just has two tourist shops in it, these being the most interesting part of the post office alongside some old telephone booths. The post office is large but the architecture is nothing special. If your visiting the cathedral then a ten minute visit to the post office makes sense but if your not going to the cathedral, this attraction is not worth the drive.
Well worth a stop inside if only for the spectacle.  If you plan on posting anything bring it with you.  I couldn't find anywhere to buy a postcard.

Is this really a top attraction in Ho Chi Minh or is trip advisor going to the dogs?? I I went there and sent a package, nice building, but very hot and a little stressful. Crowded with people taking pictures and obviously raving about it on TA. Enjoy your stay.

The only thing cool about this site is the fact that's it was designed by Eifel,  as in Eifel tower.  A quick picture of the outside, and a quick peak inside is all you need if this stop. I bought some postcards inside but its just a really large, and old post office still in use.Right next to the post office is the famous cathedral. This was also just a quick photo from outside and a quick look inside. Our toot gave us 15 minutes for both these buildings and it was just enough time there not to get bored.

If you are having a look at the cathedral, you can also have a quick look at the post office. The inside is really nice, but that's it. So if you dont have time or you are not a designaddict, there is no reason to go to this place...
Not sure what all the hype was about this place. Just a normal old building that every city has, nothing special at all i cant believe this is a tourist attraction!

A fine example of colonial French architecture. Still in use and therefore chaotic. Note the ATMs built into the old telephone booths. In the square in front of the post office, you have a view of the old CIA building, known from the photos at the end of the Vietnam War.
The building is interesting but be careful posting things! Postal charges sem to depend on which teller you get. Question everything.
We employed an official guide and it is clear that the post office is on the "must do";list. I would not bother.
It will take up 1 min to see this place. 5 ,I'm if u wish to post smthg. No harm dropping by if you are gg to Norte dame
Nice building, but really only worth a photo and a quick walk in
It is a shame that the inside of the Post Office is nothing but a tourist trap - nothing but souveniors shops.  I would recommend one to just stand outside and snap a few pictures and then move on.

The building itself is a bit uncommon for this city, as it is (obviously) constructed by Gustave Eiffel. Today it is a bit difficult to observe that the building houses a post office, as it definetly gives the impression of facilitating a souvenir shop. The ceiling is though very nice to look at.

The post office is great for mailing stuff.  Efficient. Fine, Things get where they are going.  The staff is lovely.BUT BEWARE the money changer counter as you enter the PO on the right side.  These people are thieves.  Don't change your money there.  They gave me a criminally high rate, without making it clear.  I went back after counting my money and figuring out that there must have been a mistake.  It was too late.  When they told me the rate (I was buying, not selling, so it wasn't the posted rate), it was too late for me to undo the transaction.  Thieves.
Its sort of impressive as its large and French colonial but it is just a big post office with a portrait of Ho on the wall. You can buy touristy stuff there too.
There are interesting maps on the wall. Walk in, look around, and walk out.
Not much difference than your local post office. If you need post cards or other trinkets to buy this may be your place. I would not make it a priority.
nothing much to see except the lovely architecture...

I am not sure why people rave about this building - I was expecting the inside to be more architecturally interesting. There were a lot of tourists here.

Inside:  counters to buy stamps, a tourist shop and a centre counter where you can purchase post cards. And some old telephone boxes where you can take a picture.

Completely underwhelming.

My opinion is you don’t need to come here if you stay for a short term in Hochiminh.
The inside of building is souvenir shops and was not attractive for me.

The post office is a historical spot in the area. While it still functions as an actual post office, there are lots of shops around—some a bit overpriced, while others offer good deals. It can get crowded even on regular days, but it’s still a nice place to visit, especially since it’s close to other tourist landmarks.
Beware of the cashiers in the middle of the post office. They may say you gave them $20k instead of $200k and show you CCTV footage of herself acting confused after taking the cash.

I’m not sure what I was expecting, but the experience was definitely underwhelming. The postcards are sold by independent vendors and are much more expensive than those at any other shop nearby (Book Street is just around the corner). Not only do you have to queue to buy the postcards, but then you have to queue again to find a spot to write them, and then queue a third time to buy stamps at the post office. Overall, it's something you can skip if you're short on time.

I don’t understand why this is even an attraction. It’s still a working post office and it’s full of overpriced pop up shops. It’s a nice building but I wouldn’t bother visiting. Especially if your own city has amazing architecture. Seriously underwhelming.
A nice old post office. I had a very nice 10 min tour.

Easy to find, beautiful building. They offer posting services and sell cards and souvenirs.

I was going to post a card back to my country and sadly a postal staff member was having a rude attitude towards me and my girlfriend when trying ask questions about posting.
We didn't do anything wrong other than asking for help. This is not acceptable, this woman doesn't fit to work with public service.

This is the reason for the low stars, otherwise its a good and affordable place and I would give it a 5 star if it wouldn't be for this rude woman.

I found it funny that this has become a turistic attraction. The place is beautiful, yes, but it is full of shops with souvenirs...only..
maybe just pass by if you are in the area, but definitely it is not a must go ;)

Construction of the Saigon Central Post Office began in 1886 during the French colonial period and was completed in 1891. It is still in operation today.
The design team was Gustave Eiffel, who also worked on the Eiffel Tower (Eiffel's company designed it, and Eiffel was one of the team members), and it's said to have been inspired by Paris's Gare d'Orsay (now the Musée d'Orsay).
The architecture itself is stunning, and as a working post office, many tourists visit to send postcards from this post office.
Unfortunately, there are so many souvenir shops lined up here that it's easy to forget that this is a historic building. The souvenirs they sell are also the same things you can buy anywhere, and they're priced high. The old stamps are also souvenirs and not real (look at the stamps; they're all in the same place and at the same angle).
To promote the historical value of this post office, I think they should consider removing these souvenir shops and charging an admission fee (and setting up a free counter to allow postal services to continue).

The Central Post Office is a colonial-style building, also built by the French colonial government between 1886 and 1891.
It's known to have been designed by Gustave Eiffel, the architect of the Eiffel Tower in Paris.
Its striking yellow arched exterior, and the large clock above the main entrance, give it the appearance of a train station.
It's currently the largest post office in Vietnam. Inside, a large portrait of Ho Chi Minh hangs in the center, but I didn't go inside, so that's just my guess.
There's a souvenir shop inside, which is nice to look around.

It's a completely commercial facility. It's a working post office and you can send postcards, but I think roughly 70% of the people here are engaged in souvenir sales. In terms of the hall's floor space, it feels like half of it is taken up by commercial facilities. As you enter, there are public telephone booths lined up on both sides, and above them are world clocks, but they're not functioning at all, including the public telephone booths. For example, Tokyo is off by more than 5 hours, and the time difference between each city is generally measured in hours, so it's strange that the minute hand is so out of sync. Everything is out of sync. Since it's a public facility like a post office, I thought that if they were going to exhibit it, they should do it properly.

Nice to have seen, but it's not a disaster if you skip it. Next to the two book stalls on the corner, to the left is a nice, cozy little street with bookshops. Pleasant to walk through.
I think it's the most touristy place in Vietnam. Every tourist who visits Vietnam stops by to take at least one photo at the Central Post Office.

A combination of a bunch of stuffy souvenir shops and a functioning post office. I bought a standard and unusual Star as a gift... some pretty pictures on plaques and... that's all... Ho Chi Minh City doesn't spoil you with sights, so that's a good thing.
It is a beautiful building that has been transformed into a large souvenir shop. Take many photo's from the outside and a few from the inside.

I found many buildings in Saigon having a colonial feel and when I followed the crowd into this particular building not even knowing what it was I came to my conclusion of it's purpose. I walked near a guided group to learn what it was and my previous guess was spot on.We didn't stick around long because it was OTT packed with tourists shuffling and probing.
This post office is located in the heart of Ho Chi Minh City.

It's an old building, perhaps with some history, a structure left by the French? It's right next to the cathedral, so you can stop by if you're passing by. But there's really not much to see; it's a post office that's still in operation. There are some shops in the middle selling unremarkable tourist souvenirs.

Since it's a tourist spot, there were a lot of tourists, and I couldn't leisurely browse the souvenir shops. However, it's recommended because you can visit it together with the cathedral and other attractions.
The architecture outside is nice and bright but the inside is just that -- a post office and a souvenir shop. Pass through, check out the domed ceiling and feeling nothing afterwards.

Certainly one can see the French influence in the architecture of the building, but after about two minutes, there's not much else to see or do, unless you are a stamp collector or want to buy cheap souvenirs.The post office is a good landmark to get one's bearings, but not really a tourist destination, in my opinion.
This is an attractive building to go into and full of tourist shops, however it probably won't detain you more than twenty minutes
Nothing really to write home about (pardon the pun). There is no charge so it’s easy enough to pop in I guess!
Big tourist stop and many tourists and lots of touristy trinkets to buy.  Near Notre Dame Square. Often entertainment just outside at the garden area.
Too crowded with people! because notre cathedral is closed currently, everybody went there instead, not very unique, go there only if you got nothing else to do.

We visited the Post Office as part of a tour. The architecture of the building is quite lovely, but outside of that, there's not much to do unless you want to send a postcard or buy some trinkets. Our tour had scheduled our visit at the PO for around 30 mins, but honestly you could do it in 10. Nice place for photos.

Nothing interesting. But the facade looks good for photos. Opposite is the Catholic church (Notre Dame Cathedral) which is under renovation. So you are not allowed in unless you are attending mass at the church. It's a beautiful church and would be even more beautiful after the renovation.
If you're strolling in that area already, which you most likely are as a tourist, it's worth a quick stop, maybe mail yourself a postcard, but it's nothing spectacular.
A little piece of history that's well worth a quick stop at this heritage site in the historic city. A short visit and a few souvenir vendors.

Whats good about it is that it is a lovely old building being used as a post office! However would have preferred less vendors inside. The main entrance hall and the outside of the building is what you should absorb.

This is the former Post Office located in Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam. It retains its original French architectural style. The building was constructed using French steel identical to the steel used to build the Eiffel Tower in Paris. However, it is a shame that it has been converted into a huge souvenir shop catering to tourists.
A beautiful building for sure but inside, as usual, there are souvenir shops... I expected better.

A classic Eiffel construction with extensive use of metal details from the designer's catalog. The post office is currently operational, so one finds oneself in a kind of bazaar that sells souvenirs while also performing its normal functions.

We are fairly sure that the main reason to visit would be to study the building, because of the architect. Even then it might be a stretch.This is mostly a tourist store, with multiple vendors selling tourist items.  You actually can mail things from here, but the draw is from the architect's name who designed the building.  Good luck with either decision you make to visit.Mahalo

You only see the building from the outside and the entrance. There's not much to see. It's still used as a post office today, and you can see people working there. The sides are lined with typical shops.
A post office reminiscent of a Parisian gare, but on a smaller scale. The exterior is simple and elegant. Inside, the commercial aspect is predominant and distracting.

You should be prepared for it to be quite crowded. The building looks like a train station at first glance, with a shop in the middle of the first-floor hall and long, narrow shops on either side of the main entrance.
Stopped by here on my sightseeing tour today. Nice, but not very special. Lots of people selling dill dall outside.
Of architectural and historical interest.
It's a building with a real sense of history.

it has a very interesting design and a very cool building. but its just a post office innit, a post office is quite interesting for a bit and then it gets quite boring in a while. if you get hungry there is a MacDonalds very close by and also a pretty cool gift shop.

Saigon is very hot, but it feels cool inside the Central Post Office. The post office sells many Vietnamese souvenirs, so you can browse and buy some. However, you can't bargain because the clerks said it's a government office, so the prices are fixed.
I expected way more to see instead is just a lot o touristic nic nacs and not a great deal of info about the place its a nice building anyway but yeah not extraordinary!!
It is a beautiful post office, inside is pretty much normal, not too much going on. Quick stop type of place to check out, still cool.
I took a few photos of the interior and a few of the exterior, and

An arc building well maintained,  that actually works also. The central room is always full of tourists that they send  postcards...and look at the souvenir shops that are actually expensive...its a must seen building since it's near to cathedral ..in a walk in the town you passed through  both of them. Maximum one hour visits..
It was incredibly crowded, both inside and out.
Another sightseeing place to visit. It was a quick visit as too crowded with bus tour passengers. Okay to miss if you don’t have time.
You don't need long to look around here. The building is attractive but today mainly houses gift shops

This historic post office is still in operation, and sending postcards from here is a popular activity among tourists. The area is lively and crowded with visitors and tour vendors, with souvenir shops nearby. It is a must-see attraction when visiting Ho Chi Minh City.

A well-maintained, rustic building preserved from the Vietnam War era, the Saigon Central Post Office is more monument than post office today. Architecturally impressive and rich in history, it’s worth a quick visit to appreciate the design and heritage.

That said, the experience is diluted by the crowds. The interior feels more like a gift-shop complex than a functioning civic space, with souvenir stalls dominating the floor and heavy tourist traffic making it cramped and noisy.

Overall, a visually striking landmark with cultural value, but the over-commercialisation and overcrowding take away from what could have been a more meaningful experience.

Grand looking building from the outside and beautiful architect interior of the roof and the wall inside. However it was mostly being overshadowed by the tourist shopping trapped inside and out.
It was super packed on the weekend as well so you can't really see all the space that clearly.
But as it close to the book street and Notre Dame cathedral it was nice and easy to stop by!

Not as impressive as I wanted it to be.
Disorganized, and unfortunately as with many historical sights in Vietnam, everything has to be turned into a shopping mall for maximum profits. Still, not enough ground to skip this magnificent building from a bygone age.

beautiful building, but feels more like a big shop.
30k Postcard. They not even that nice I suggest buying one somewhere else. You still can bring it there and send it.

A true tourist Hotspot. Hot and sticky hall with a crowd of tourists that are screening postcards and queuing long lines to send them home.
If you really like to enjoy the beauty of the city, skip this place ;-)

The Saigon Central Post Office in Ho Chi Minh City is a renowned 19th-century, French-colonial landmark designed by Gustave Eiffel. Located in District 1 near Notre-Dame Cathedral, it features neoclassical, Renaissance, and Gothic architecture with a functioning, historic interior. It is a top tourist attraction with free admission. You can post letters from here. Give it at least 45 days by the time you get the letter

Situated in the heart of Ho Chi Minh City is the Central Post Office. this is a must see experience if this is your first or even your second visit to HCM. this is a working post office where locals can pay their bills, process mail, make phone call in old fashion phone booths and browse the many booths for trinkets. The post office is housed in a historical building that is a popular stop for tours and locals who wish to have their photos taken (many wedding photos here). The interior includes many market stalls with a variety of gifts and souvenirs at reasonable prices.

The post office is one of the top tourist attractions in the city and can be visited at the same time as the Notre-Dame Cathedral across the street and the Opera House. Adorning the main wall is a huge portrait of Ho Chi Minh. Admission is free and well worth having a look inside if visiting central Saigon.

Only visit this building if you like architecture. It is still a working post office, but basically has just been turned into a souvenir shop. The building in nice but there is so much more to see in Ho Chi Minh City I would not make a special trip to see it. The tour we were on visited it, I think more because it is next to the Notre Dame Cathedral. However because the cathedral is closed for renovations it was a waste of an hour of my time.

Really crowded
Just post office and souvenir shops

A beautiful building in Saigon but too many tourists, and way too many social medial personalities, like everywhere in SE Asia.
"Why so many girls or young couples dressing up?"
"...they'll post it on Facebook and social media..."

Across the street, is their Notre Dame but is under renovation, as well.

Next to it (northwest) is the lovely Book Street, and the yellow building locates at 22 Gia Long Street, that is southeast of the post office, was where the last American helicopter left.

This is a very nice and interesting place. Still functioning post office and the tourists may even buy post cards or cards to send to their loved ones back home because apparently, slow mail is still very popular here.
The souvenir shops also sell many interesting trinkets and tokens for you to buy home.
Beautiful. One of the places to come to if you are in the area.

We wanted to sent an international package by sea but the staff told us the sea is closed for shipment only over the air is possible ( I assume because its 2x more expensive by air) we asked why and he said he doesnt know why.
Sending off normal postcards was easy and quick!

This is an okay place if you want souvenirs. I got a refrigerator magnet for 25k. In general I wouldn’t go out of my way to come here because it’s not much but it was crowded around 2pm on Saturday. Also, it’s really hot inside.

I guess one has to check it out as it gets a lot of publicity. Having said that, it’s interesting, in a way, but it’s just a working Post Office with lots of boxes and then they’ve added a bunch of gift stores in the centre & around the perimeter (but yes, we did buy a few souvenirs). So yes, worth a visit, especially since it’s close to other tourist sites; but only plan on a short stay).
It is a great building but i think they could have made it more of an historical site instead of a shopping store. Worth a look from outside
Full of tourists, buying postcards and souvenirs…lady at counter selling stamps was unfriendly. Too hot and couldn’t wait to get out of there..

Another historical place to visit and see the architecture of the building, it’s still well-maintained and not much changes compare to my last 10 yrs of visit.

However, the service here is very slow and not well managed.  I went to buy the stamps for postcards which has to queuing up for more than 20 mins while others foreigners who in line also just buy stamps and sending postal.

It's a post office filled with tourists and overpriced souvenirs.

Very hot inside.

It's a really nice building on the outside and on the inside there's this magic atmosphere where you can imagine what happened in the past in this very place. Only the hall is available to people plus two small galleries to buy souvenirs. 3 stars only because there's not much to see but it makes sense if you'll send some postcards home from.this beautiful place. It's part of this city's history so make sure to read enough about it.

Saigon Central Post Office, a blend of functionality and heritage, has transformed into both a working post office and a tourist attraction. The vintage architecture captivates visitors, offering an ideal backdrop for photos and a chance to purchase souvenirs. However, prices may be on the higher side. Conveniently located and free to enter, the post office attracts crowds, so be prepared for potential congestion. Despite the occasional crowds and pricey souvenirs, the historical significance and architectural charm make Saigon Central Post Office a notable stop for those exploring Ho Chi Minh City.

Weird place ,the lady in the photo at counter 3 charged 330k for a postcard to Taiwan.The first time she just showed me the number on the calculator,I asked her again and she said clearly “thirty three hundred “.
My friend asked again she also showed him the calculator but when he asked “isn’t it 33k”she changed the numbers immediately.

An impromptu visit to the post office. Not really much to view but was amused that it was an post office as well as a tourist place. You can purchase post cards and send back to your loved ones! Loads of souvenirs to choose from as well.

We have been to HCMC many times and have visited this place on several occasions though not every time. It is one of the iconic landmarks in District 1. But in our recent visit, we have dropped by this place again as my cousins were visiting the city for the first time.

It was Sunday and the GPO was very crowded and packed with visitors, both locals and tourists. Many were queuing to buy and send postcards and parcels while others were shopping for local souvenirs. Inside the building was very warm and humid (it was drizzling outside but windy) and even for a short while, we were all perspiring and so we went outside. Hope they can improve the air ventilation here, maybe installing more fans, aircoolers or aircon.

But it's nice and one can feel the historical aspect of this building as if time has stopped with many vintage and old setups, decors and furniture.

Beautiful old French colonialism building,
we posted a few postcards to family from here. You can buy your chosen card, sit at the desks, and write out your card (TAKE A PEN), then go to the post office counter who will sell you the stamps you need to send your cards.
It felt quite special to do this from here.

Good sightseeing spot for picture taking and to send someone a greeting post card. There are tables and a postcard counter for visitors to buy and send post cards. For once surprise your love ones with a hand written postcard.
When were the last time you did that? Lol!

I guess this is a landmark that is commonly visited and entrance is free; hence the strong crowd.

There are postcards available along with souvenirs and stamps.

But the architecture is just classic, not a thing for me.

Nice to see the building from outside and the vintage decor inside. But nothing really special ,only a huge amount of souvenir shops and ambulant dealers who want to sell you nonsense for high prices. The hop-on/-off tour service only provides expensive City-Tours as well as the tour agent has only limited and expensive day tour packages.
Lots of tourists and soviniers.  The outside of the building is nice.  Across the street from Notre Dame

Giving it 3-stars, mainly because it's a fully operational historic post-office with cool temperatures.  Otherwise, if it were no longer in operations, it would be much lower.

For me, the tacky and tad expensive souvenirs take up the entirely too much space in the front floor-room.  It would be nice to be able to walk around and not feel cramped by nic-nacs all around you.

I would say, if you are in the area of the cathedral, then it pays to stop-by.  But do yourself a favor and mail someone you love a postcard.  We don't do this as often as we should, but a postcard from someone you care about miles away goes a long way.  Yup, that's about all there is to do there; so you might as well.....

Total time needed, 10 minutes tops and that's only if you decide to send a postcard; as when I arrived all of the reps were ready and available to assist.
It’s cool, but very touristy definitely worth just going in and seeing.
The architecture is nice, and it’s cool that you can send a postcard from there—but overall, there’s nothing particularly special about it.

This place is one of popular photo spot in Ho chi minh. Better come in the afternoon. When you got lucky you'd see some pretty vietnamese women dressing in ao dai (traditional dress). They'd happily have some pictures with you :)
The place are well maintained, we went during Sunday and are very crowded. No entry fee. Activities to do here would be sending post card back to your home country
Crowded place. Nice selection of postcards but more in the pricier side. Building is beautiful and old.
Well preserved tourist attraction. There is market in the building, cheaper than anywhere for souvenirs and little things.

Really cool place to check out! The post office actually opens at 8AM (not 7:30 as Google is suggesting). Their are a ton of postcards for sale inside, they make great souvenirs. Don't bother wasting your money "sending" your postcards. They will most likely not actually be sent and never arrive at the destination. It's best to just send them locally when you get back home

Aside from the nice building, there's not much to do here except buy some tacky souvenirs. Come by the area though because it's all part of the package : notre dame, book cafe Street and palace not too far away
Nothing touristy here, its the postal office and some souvenir shops that were overpriced

even tho it’s a very famous tourist spot there’s nothing written in english 🥲
it’s not necessarily a bad thing it just would’ve helped me what to do if i wanted to send a post card which i did but because of the lack of information i didn’t
it was also pretty warm in there and there’s a lot of people

Entertaining but overpriced. The experience to write and send letters was fun, but the prices were really expensive for Vietnam and it was very crowded. Many gift shops and a lot of choice dough. Probably less crowded in the morning

It was a very nice looking building that shows the history of the city. There was a lot going on inside which may have been what made it less appealing. I didn’t look at all the stuff being sold, but what I did see was more pricy than it should have.
Nice for some instagram pic, you can book tour tickets here and they sell small nick nacks
Beautiful architecture, but there are too many souvenir shops inside that distract from the view.
Great building architecture but stuff/goods inside are insanely overpriced.
Great Architecture and it is also a fully functional post office! Something to do if you have time, for there isn’t much to explore here.
Bought some coins here, can’t complain.

The building is beautiful and there are some souvenir shops where you can buy postcards, but the queue was long and the service quite disorganized — we waited once, then the clerk suddenly stopped without notice, so we had to queue again elsewhere
It is a great building to look at but that is about it, you wouldn't spend very long in there but worth a visit if you are already there looking at the Cathedral next to it
Over rated place. Nothing much to do aside from sending postcard and being a tourist trap.

Cool building, some of my favorite souvenirs, but holy cow was it crowded and full of some of the rudest and dumbest tourists I’ve ever seen. We went back early the next morning and there were still a fair amount of people, but it wasn’t nearly as bad. Also, if you want to actually mail anything the counter to buy stamps is super confusing, we kind of just stood back for a while until we were able to force our way up to the counter, it was less of a line and more of a mass of people. Anyway, just go in the morning, it’ll be better.

If you want to see French Colonialism at its finest then this is a cool place to go. The building is quite impressive but the number of tourists there take some of the charm out of it. It is also interesting to see how much the French wanted to show their wealth and power to the Vietnamese whith these kind of buildings that do not look like they belong here.
Nice building, old post office. Very romantic to send post cards to your loved ones but in reality it’s just a big (overpriced) souvenir shop inside

Don’t get me wrong, it’s a pretty building but for me it wasn’t wow. It may be in the heart of the city and be quite old but is small-ish, has a few curio stalls etc but we were out in 5 minutes.
The times also wrong on the booth showing Pretoria.

We sent 6 postcards to 3 different countries. NONE of our friends and family received a single postcard.

Update 1: Three postcards arrived. 3 months late, but they arrived

Of you want to go just for visit I don't think not is worth not if you don't have way around. Yes it has nice architecture but besides that and two souvenirs shops notning special. We was here to sent twelve postcard, which we did to Europe, and it was 252VND. It is reasonable price and your loved ones will have smile on their faces. For that good to go but better buy postcard on the local market.
Building is nice, but a little too pricy. Also, they only accept cash as payment. Overrated in my opinion
Nice looking building but ridiculously over crowded. Book Street just round the corner has a much more chilled vibe

The architecture looks pretty cool but still not sure how this is a "must visit" in HCMC. It's an old post office that sells a load of cheap souvenirs to tourists. Good if you have 5 minutes to spare or want to buy a magnet.

I have never really been to a post office so it was nice. I went and sat on the wooden bench while my mom looked around. nothing special tbh. just a tickmark since everyone visits this place and its close to the palace.
Not much to see here. You only spend a few minutes looking around except if you write a postcard or browse through the gift shops.

It's a large post office, so it was perfect for a meeting.
There are also shops where you can buy souvenirs.

There are a lot of pigeons, so people who are not good at it may want to be careful.

Fascinating architecture, I particularly loved the  colonial period phone booths which has been converted to ATM booths. There's also some stalls selling knick knacks on both sides after the entrance. Well maintained to draw tourists, 10 minutes the most, I was out already. Still functioning though as a post office till this day.
Busy place  with tourists but historical architecture. Some small souvenirs  to buy inside  the post office.
Beautiful building, packed with souvenirs. Designed by Alfred Foulhoux – not Gustave Eiffel. Worth a quick visit if you're in the neighbourhood, but wouldn't hike across town for it.
Hot take: this is just a somewhat fancy post office. Three stars because the crowds make it hard to send mail.

I enjoyed the green colors and the big Ho Chi Minh painting inside the post office. Everything looks relatively new since it has been renovated recently.
It gets crowded when tour groups arrive.

The interior and exterior of Central Post Office, both of them are exceptional and unique, maybe could be considered as one of many symbols of HCMC. But... Nothing is really special about it, except for its signature view of front hall. You should come just to sightsee this place and you could add your visit by looking on the shops inside.

Beautiful place,  just by the cathedral,  which is stunning.

If you plan to send some things home,  be sure to have google translate or a way to talk with the people who work there.

The coolest thing about this building is that it was designed by the same architect that designed the Eiffel Tower. Other than that,its currently a functioning post office with many gift shops attached.

Salespeople unenthusiastic about making sales...a bit surprising to someone coming from a non-socialist country. Mediocre service qhen it comes to the salespeople. However, this is probably the place to get souvenirs. The same products cost 2 to 3 times more at the airport.
A bit overrated but nice place to send a postal to your family!
Best thing is right next to the Notre Dame Cathedral Of Saigon, too many tour as well, if you have no tour guide to tell you the story behind the building, you will only need to spend 5 mins here
It's a non-airconditioned post office with french colonial architecture. That's all you need to know if you're planning to visit.
Historical place with French architecture, nice and cute visit for a few minutes, but that’s about it. In my humble opinion a little bit overrated.

Unique post office, worth the visit. Would recommend that if you're in Saigon to go see it. Be prepared for a massive crowd.and yes you can get postcards, stamps there but also be prepared to pay through the nose.

You can meet much more people, and experience what "people mountain, people sea" is here.

Most of the post cards sold here are lack of style, I strongly suggest those who want to buy post card to buy one that you like at other souvenior shop, then mail it here.
Nice building right by the Notre Dame cathedral. Nothing special if you have been to or are from Europe. A bit of a tourist trap..
I like designs of the post office. Especially its flooring.
so touristy....many people ,if wanna go here ..suggest came early..
Pretty building and it's nice to send a postcard on the inside. It's very warm on the inside and gets crowded easily.
As a tourist attraction it was ok for a quick visit. It looks grand with a spectacular ceiling. Crammed with tourists and souvenir shops.
Historical building. With many tourists who buy the handcrafts and gifts. It is beautiful place to walk around.
Came here July 2015. It was free and worth the quick walk inside to browse the place. It still has a historic feel.
Open daily 8 am until 7 pm, located beside saigon catedral church, one of the saigon historical architectural building.
Still an active post office, good place to take structural ancient photos of this marvelous building but quite a tourist trap inside with a horrible currency exchange.
Nothing much to do except souvenir shop and stamps. Good for a visit to take pic of the building structure.
Nice ideal for a quick visit. The nice part is that is an actual post office so you can send your postcards from here
Tourist shops and a great hall. Not much more than a quick look. Don't go out of your way to see this
Overrated, nice from outside becouse colonial style, from the inside only if you want to buy Souvenirs. 🙄 …
It is not simply a post office but a building to visit. At No. 2, Public Works of Paris, District 1, this is a tourist destination in Saigon District 1 for those nearby
Not sure what the hype is, but it's fine. You can buy and send some cards, it's straightforward and reasonably priced.

Beautiful place to see.
You can’t use credit card inside the post office.
If you’re in the area anyway, then sure visit for a few minutes. It’s just a post office with a lot of overpriced souvenir shops.
Nothing much but interesting
can send post cards here
I was like "oh, that's it?"
Go there only to take picture. Hot-non aircon. The best picture is the facade-outside of the building
Very old building and still offering post service, good to visit!
Fine of it's not too far out of your way. Otherwise, you're not missing much in my opinion.
I've been sending letters from here to my country since mid-August but until the end of October I haven't received them.
Nice to look at and basically a large post office inside (complete with a huge Ho Chi Minh portrait), worth a look
Nice looking building.  Not much to do inside besides sending mail.  Not sure what else I was expecting.
Former General Post Office. Now converted to a centre with numerous tourist souvenirs shop.
Nice quick visit and a photo. That about it.
Very crowded by tourist taking look around souvenir shop inside building.
Pretty building but it is just a post office with a couple shops
Historic post office with some souvenier shops and a tour booking company inside.
Enjoyed the architecture and maps, not the shops
An old building with some history, located in the heart of the city near notre dame cathedral
Quite a historic place but not much to do though.
A building which is over a century old. Nice nostalgic architectural.
Only thing i love here is the architecture of the house.

Nice to take a look if you’re around...
There is no much to see
Nice spacious
I don’t know why it’s one of main attraction
Cool place to visit once. Nothing too special.
Just passed through. But it seemed to draw a lot of tourists
Nice from the outside. Bit of a tourist trap inside.
Some little post cards
Colonial type building with architectural importance
Don’t accept card and very slow
A must-visit place for travellers to Ho Chi Minh city
If you’re around you can have a visit.
Amazing preservation of culture and vietnamese style
It’s fine…
Well preserved facility
Should have more shops
Not much to see
Could take a look
Expensive
Worth a look.
Very classic
Best place

My boyfriend sent me a postcard from here in November 2025, and I never received it; pure gamble, potential money waste. That said, when I was in Vietnam my postcard to CA, USA took 1.5 months to reach my friend. The building is nice but gets crowded.

The architecture is very beautiful, especially the interior design. You can buy and send postcards and stamps. You can also buy souvenirs such as miscellaneous goods and coffee, but they're not particularly cheap or unique.
Take a photo, check out the spot, and you can leave. Even though it's a weekday/Friday, it's packed with tourists, it feels like you're browsing a Chinese New Year market!

I went there as a tourist spot in the center of Ho Chi Minh City. As expected, it was crowded, so I walked around the store without stopping. As it is a historic building influenced by France from 140 years ago, it was worth visiting just to feel the atmosphere.

It's extremely crowded there. Inside, you buy postcards amidst the hustle and bustle, and then the stamps at the counter. You then put the cards in the red baskets provided on the post office counter. Please check that you're using the international basket and not the one for Hanoi.

An employee then empties the overflowing baskets.

I once saw a tourist try to take postcards from other people's homes that he hadn't written. I stopped him and explained that he had to buy his own cards. Imagine if he discovered the cards in the basket were already written on and simply threw them away or kept them!

The employees don't pay attention to the baskets. Otherwise, try a different post office!

The historic post office is a sight to behold, but simply too crowded. Why does the post office have to sell expensive jewelry? 80 euros for a pair of earrings?

The postcards are often in transparent sleeves, which, due to the lack of wastebaskets, end up lying around everywhere. There are also remnants of stamps and envelopes lying on the counter. That's a shame.

The Street of Books is right next door and just somehow nicer.

Username: 高木それと_東京カレーマガジン
Comment: I couldn't go during the day, so I went to see just the exterior of the post office before returning home. The post office retains the atmosphere of a European train station, and you can really feel the history. I love things like this.

The Saigon Post Office is opposite the church. Inside, there's a shopping area, but it's a bit crowded.

Beside the post office, there are brand-name shops and a coffee shop, similar to those found at ICONSIAM. If you're walking around this area in the heat, you can stop in and take a break.

It's crowded during the day, so it's probably not the best photo opportunity.

Because it is a tourist attraction, it is actually a place worth visiting at least once and a place to take commemorative photos. Other than that, there is nothing special about it; it is just a post office. However, unlike other post offices, it is not an ordinary office-like building, but an old-fashioned European-style building, so it is special and a symbol of Ho Chi Minh.

A beautiful "European-style" building, obviously reminiscent of the colonial period, just like the cathedral located right next door.

While sightseeing in Ho Chi Minh City, I visited the Saigon Central Post Office. Its beautiful, historic architecture had me excited even before I visited.

A Classical Exterior and Open Space
This post office was built during the French colonial period in the late 19th century and is said to have been designed by Gustave Eiffel, also known as the designer of the Eiffel Tower. The orange exterior, repainted in 2015, stood out beautifully against the blue sky.

The moment I stepped inside, I was captivated by the spacious interior. The high ceilings and arched structure create a sense of depth and openness. A large portrait of Vietnamese hero Ho Chi Minh hung in the center, his imposing figure standing out in particular.

A Working Post Office Where You Can Enjoy Souvenir Shopping
This post office is still in operation, and I saw both tourists and locals using it. It's a great place to buy stamps and letters to send to family and friends as mementos of your trip.

Large souvenir corners are set up on both sides of the building. In addition to standard postcards, there are many other items you can only find here, such as commemorative stamps and desk calendars, so I found myself absorbed in choosing souvenirs. 

The nighttime illumination is also a must-see
While the daytime visit is beautiful, the building is lit up at night, creating a completely different, mystical atmosphere. If I have the chance to visit again, I would definitely like to see it at night. This post office is not just a tourist destination, but a fascinating place where you can experience its history, architecture, and the fact that it is still deeply rooted in people's lives.

The Saigon Central Post Office is an absolute must-see in Ho Chi Minh City, offering a gorgeous blend of functioning postal service and magnificent history. This iconic landmark, often credited to the genius of Gustave Eiffel, is a glorious testament to French colonial architecture, primarily in the Neo-Renaissance style.
Stepping inside is like entering a grand European railway station. The soaring, high-vaulted ceiling, the polished marble floors, the intricate wrought-iron details, and the antique wooden phone booths create a spacious, airy, and wonderfully nostalgic atmosphere. The large portrait of President Ho Chi Minh presiding over the hall, alongside the historical maps painted on the walls, beautifully juxtaposes the building's colonial heritage with Vietnam's modern identity.
But the best part? It's a fully operational post office! Taking the time to buy a postcard, write a note, and send it home is a charming, tangible way to engage with this living piece of history. It's a truly beautiful and calming respite from the city's heat and traffic.

A must-visit landmark when you’re in Ho Chi Minh City. Even though I’ve been here a few times before, the charm of the Saigon Central Post Office never seems to fade. This time I only came to buy a few stamps, but ended up staying much longer, once again drawn in by its timeless beauty.

The architecture is a real showstopper — French colonial with ornate detailing that gives the building an almost palace-like feel. The arched ceiling inside, painted in a mix of green and gold with iron support beams, is visually stunning. The mosaic floor tiles are equally beautiful and well preserved, adding another layer of character. At the far end, the large portrait of Ho Chi Minh watching over the bustling space gives the post office both cultural and historical weight.

There are also old French maps painted directly onto the walls, one showing Southern Vietnam and Cambodia, which is a unique historical touch. Despite being a working post office, it also feels like a museum — and it does get quite warm inside due to the constant stream of visitors.

If you haven’t considered visiting this building yet, definitely put it on your list. It’s both iconic and inspiring.

The Saigon Central Post Office is a historic landmark in Ho Chi Minh City and one of its most popular photo spots. Built between 1886 and 1891 during the French colonial period, the building was designed by architect Alfred Foulhoux, though it is often mistakenly attributed to Gustave Eiffel. The post office’s stunning French colonial architecture features grand arches, intricate ironwork, and a beautifully designed interior with a large map of South Vietnam painted on the walls. Despite its historical significance, the post office remains fully operational, serving both locals and tourists. Its charm and historical importance make it a must-visit destination in the city.

The Saigon Central Post Office, located in the heart of Ho Chi Minh City, is a stunning example of French colonial architecture and one of the city's most iconic landmarks. Built in the late 19th century, the building features beautiful details such as its vaulted ceilings, and intricate wooden counters. The interior is equally impressive, with a large central hall, vintage telephone booths, and a giant portrait of Ho Chi Minh overseeing the space. Visitors can admire the historical architecture while also observing the post office in action, as it still serves as a functioning hub for mail and communication. It's a peaceful spot amid the hustle and bustle of the city, and a great place to take photos. In addition to its historical and architectural significance, the post office also houses a small souvenir shop where you can purchase postcards and gifts. For anyone interested in the colonial history of Saigon or looking to appreciate a piece of Vietnam's past, the Saigon Central Post Office is a must-visit spot.

The Saigon Central Post Office is a stunning example of French colonial architecture, standing as a testament to Vietnam's rich history. Constructed in the late 19th century, this iconic building boasts a grand, neo-classical design that immediately captivates visitors. The intricate details, high ceilings, and elegant arches create a truly awe-inspiring atmosphere, transporting you back in time. Beyond its architectural beauty, the post office remains a functional hub, allowing you to experience the vibrant pulse of local life. Whether you're sending a postcard, admiring the stunning interiors, or simply soaking in the historic ambiance, the Saigon Central Post Office is a must-visit destination that will leave a lasting impression.

During our cruise, we visited, among other places, the Old Post Office in Saigon. Architecturally, it's a true gem in the heart of Ho Chi Minh City.

The building was constructed between 1886 and 1891 and combines French colonial architecture with Gothic and Renaissance elements – this is particularly evident in the large, arched windows and the impressive clock at the entrance. The name Gustave Eiffel is often mentioned as the architect, but this isn't entirely accurate; the design is by Alfred Foulhoux, which doesn't detract from the building's charm.

Inside, visitors are greeted by an impressive, light-filled space with a dome and columns. Two enormous, historical maps of Saigon and South Vietnam from the 1890s adorn the walls – perfect for anyone interested in history or who simply appreciates beautiful details. Old mailboxes, telephone booths, and wooden chairs complete the nostalgic atmosphere. The post office is still in operation today, but it's also a popular spot for tourists and a great photo opportunity next to the nearby Notre Dame Cathedral.

Insider tip: There's no entrance fee, and it's especially worthwhile going early in the morning before it gets too crowded.

You can also spend a few minutes here admiring the fine details of the architecture and the old maps.

Otherwise, it's just an old post office, now surrounded by vendors who know they can make money off tourists.

Facing the portrait, the right side is for international and domestic mail. Please pay attention when sending postcards!

The buildings are old, and a small portion still serve as post offices.
